topics.title: Ultrasonic Machining - Elements of Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Machining - Elements of Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Ultrasonic Machining (USM)** is a non-traditional, mechanical type material removal process in which material is removed from the workpiece by the **abrasive action of abrasive particles** suspended in a slurry, which are impinged against the workpiece at high velocity by a tool vibrating at **ultrasonic frequency (20–40 kHz)** with a low amplitude (15–50 µm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** USM is fundamentally classified as a **mechanical-type non-conventional process** (unlike EDM which is electro-thermal). It is *not* dependent on the electrical, chemical, or thermal properties of the workpiece, making it ideal for **non-conductive, hard, and brittle materials** such as ceramics, glass, quartz, germanium, silicon, carbides, and precious stones.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine hammering a small chisel against a stone wall thousands of times per second — but with sand grains as the chisel bits and water to keep things cool and flowing. That is essentially what USM does.

- The **tool** is like a rapidly vibrating hammer head (vibrating at ~20,000 times/second).
- The **abrasive particles** (sand-like grains) act as tiny chisels trapped between the tool and the workpiece.
- Each vibration cycle drives these grains to **punch a microscopic chip** off the workpiece surface.
- The **slurry (water + abrasive)** continuously brings fresh grains into the cutting zone, just like a river delivering fresh sand to a grinding wheel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A dentist's ultrasonic scaler works on the exact same principle — high-frequency vibrations drive a tool against teeth, with a water spray to flush away debris. The only difference is the scale and the material being removed.

### 1.3 Core Physical Metrics

- **Operating Frequency (f):** **19–25 kHz** (typically 20 kHz)
- **Vibration Amplitude (a):** **15–50 µm** (typically 20–25 µm)
- **Static Feed Force (P):** **0.5 to 25 N** depending on tool area
- **Abrasive Types:** **Boron Carbide (B₄C) – hardest**, Silicon Carbide (SiC), Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃)
- **Abrasive Size:** **100–800 mesh** (15–150 µm grain size)
- **Slurry Concentration:** **30–60%** abrasive by weight in water

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Essential Elements of the USM Process

The USM process is a closed-loop mechatronic system with **five functionally distinct elements** working synchronously. The KTU examiner's classic question is *"Explain the elements/process parameters of USM with a neat sketch"* — the depth here directly determines your mark.

#### 2.1.1 Ultrasonic Power Supply / High-Frequency Generator
- Converts **50 Hz mains supply** to **20 kHz electrical signal** at high power (50–2400 W).
- Uses solid-state oscillator circuits to generate the high-frequency alternating current.
- Provides the **electrical energy** input to the transducer.
- Must maintain stable frequency despite load variations (automatic frequency tuning).

#### 2.1.2 Transducer
Converts the high-frequency electrical energy into **high-frequency mechanical vibrations**. Two principal types exist:

| Type | Principle | Material | Amplitude Achieved |
|------|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Piezoelectric** | Inverse piezoelectric effect | Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT), Barium Titanate | 0.005–0.01 mm |
| **Magnetostrictive** | Magnetostriction (Ni, Permendur, Alfer) | Nickel, Permendur alloys | 0.005–0.025 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two types?** Magnetostrictive transducers produce higher amplitude and are more robust for industrial use, but require water cooling (since eddy-current losses generate heat). Piezoelectric units are more efficient (~96% vs ~40% for magnetostrictive) and are compact, but produce lower amplitude — hence they **always require a mechanical amplifier (horn)**.

#### 2.1.3 Tool Holder / Horn / Mechanical Amplifier (Concentrator)
- A **tapered metal block** (typically monel, titanium, or aluminium) that mechanically amplifies the small amplitude from the transducer to the larger amplitude required at the tool tip.
- Amplification factor depends on the **shape ratio** (length to cross-sectional area ratio at the two ends).
- Common shapes: **Cylindrical, Conical, Exponential, Stepped, Catenoidal**.

The amplification ratio for a **tapered (conical) horn** is governed by:

$$\beta = \frac{D_1}{D_2}$$

where $D_1$ is the diameter at the transducer end and $D_2$ is the diameter at the tool end.

#### 2.1.4 The Tool (Sonotrode)
- Made of **soft, low-fatigue-strength material** (monel, mild steel, brass, stainless steel) so it does not wear out faster than the workpiece.
- Tool is shaped as the **mirror image (negative) of the desired cavity** with a small **undercut** to allow abrasive flow.
- Tool area directly affects MRR and accuracy — smaller area = better accuracy but lower MRR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why soft tool material?** The tool itself should not be abraded by the abrasive — it must transfer kinetic energy to the abrasive particles, not compete with the workpiece for abrasion. A common KTU viva question!

#### 2.1.5 Abrasive Slurry
- **Abrasive particles** (B₄C, SiC, Al₂O₃) suspended in a **carrier fluid** (usually water, sometimes light oil).
- **B₄C** is preferred for hard materials like tungsten carbide; **SiC** for general use; **Al₂O₃** for glass and softer ceramics.
- Slurry is continuously recirculated by a slurry pump to bring fresh, sharp abrasive to the cutting zone and to flush away debris (swarf).

### 2.2 Mechanism of Material Removal

The material removal mechanism in USM involves three simultaneous actions:

1. **Direct Hammering (Impact)**: The vibrating tool strikes the abrasive particles which are pinched between the tool and the workpiece. The kinetic energy of the tool is transferred to the particles, which impact the workpiece surface and cause **micro-chipping (Hertzian fracture)**.
2. **Throwing Action**: The tool, on its forward stroke, propels abrasive particles at high velocity against the workpiece.
3. **Cavitation Erosion & Chemical Action**: Collapse of cavitation bubbles in the slurry and mild chemical corrosion of the freshly exposed surface assist the removal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical concept:** The tool never directly contacts the workpiece. Material is removed purely by the **indirect impact of abrasive particles** suspended in the gap (~25–50 µm) between tool and workpiece.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **MRR (Empirical)** | $MRR = C \cdot P \cdot d \cdot a \cdot f$ | C = constant, P = static load (N), d = abrasive grain size (µm), a = amplitude (µm), f = frequency (Hz) | Predicting production rate |
| 2 | **MRR (Theoretical – impact model)** | $MRR = \dfrac{k \cdot P \cdot a \cdot f}{H_v}$ | $H_v$ = Vickers hardness (N/mm²), k = constant | For design calculations |
| 3 | **Impact Force (Hertzian)** | $F_i = \sigma_c \cdot A_c$ | $\sigma_c$ = contact stress, $A_c$ = contact area | Predicting per-grain chip |
| 4 | **Hertzian Contact Stress** | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a^2}$ | F = applied force, a = contact radius | Stress at abrasive–workpiece interface |
| 5 | **Amplification Ratio (Conical Horn)** | $\beta = D_1 / D_2$ | $D_1, D_2$ = end diameters | Horn design |
| 6 | **Slurry Flow Rate** | $Q_{slurry} \approx 1\text{–}5$ L/min | — | Practical pumping sizing |
| 7 | **Tool Wear Ratio** | $TWR = \dfrac{\text{Vol. tool worn}}{\text{Vol. workpiece removed}}$ | Dimensionless | Process economics |
| 8 | **Penetration Rate** | $PR = \dfrac{MRR}{A_{tool}}$ | mm³/s ÷ mm² = mm/s | Practical machining time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Tip:** $MRR \propto P \cdot d \cdot a \cdot f$ — all four process parameters *increase* MRR when increased. However, $MRR \propto 1/H_v$ — *harder workpiece = lower MRR*. This inverse relationship with hardness is a frequent KTU board question.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Electronics industry**: Drilling and slicing of **silicon wafers, germanium, quartz crystals** for semiconductors and oscillators.
- **Aerospace**: Machining of **ceramic matrix composites (CMCs)**, turbine blade holes in nickel superalloys.
- **Medical**: Manufacturing of **ceramic dental implants** and surgical tools.
- **Jewellery & Tooling**: Engraving and shaping of **dies in tungsten carbide**, precious stones, and gemstones.
- **Optics**: Machining of **glass lenses, mirrors**, and optical flats.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Removal Rate (MRR) Based on Impact Energy

This derivation is **frequently asked for 7 marks** in KTU 2019–2023 papers under the heading *"Derive the expression for MRR in USM."*

**Starting assumptions:**
- Abrasive grains are spheres of diameter $d$ and density $\rho$.
- A grain is accelerated by the tool from rest to velocity equal to the **peak tool velocity**.
- Tool vibrates sinusoidally: $x(t) = a \sin(2\pi f t)$.
- Peak velocity of tool: $v_{max} = 2\pi f \cdot a$.

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of a single grain at impact**

The grain, accelerated by the tool over amplitude $a$, acquires kinetic energy:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_{max}^2
$$

where $m$ is the mass of one grain. Substituting $v_{max} = 2\pi f a$:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m (2\pi f a)^2 = 2 \pi^2 m f^2 a^2
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of one grain (spherical)**

For a spherical abrasive of diameter $d$ and density $\rho$:

$$
m = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho
$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy per grain**

$$
E_k = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\pi d^3 \rho}{6}\right) f^2 a^2 = \frac{\pi^3 d^3 \rho f^2 a^2}{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Volume removed per impact**

A portion of this energy is used to remove a small chip. Assuming a constant chip volume $V_c$ proportional to $E_k / H_v$ (where $H_v$ is the hardness of the workpiece in energy-per-volume units):

$$
V_c = \frac{K \cdot E_k}{H_v} = \frac{K \pi^3 d^3 \rho f^2 a^2}{3 H_v}
$$

where $K$ is a dimensionless efficiency constant.

**Step 5 — Number of effective impacts per second**

Assume $n$ grains impact the workpiece per second. Then the **MRR** is:

$$
MRR = n \cdot V_c = \frac{n K \pi^3 d^3 \rho f^2 a^2}{3 H_v}
$$

**Step 6 — Linking to static load**

The number of effective impacts $n$ is proportional to the static load $P$ (more load = more grains held in contact):

$$
n = C_1 \cdot P
$$

**Final consolidated MRR expression:**

$$
\boxed{MRR = \frac{C \, P \, d^3 \, \rho \, f^2 \, a^2}{H_v}}
$$

where $C = \dfrac{K \pi^3 C_1}{3}$ is the consolidated process constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick:** If the examiner asks for a **simplified empirical form**, write: $MRR = C \cdot P \cdot d \cdot a \cdot f$ (first power of each parameter). The full derivation above is for the rigorous analytical form. Always start with the empirical form and then show the detailed derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hertzian Contact Stress at Grain–Workpiece Interface

Used to justify why brittle fracture (chipping) is the dominant removal mode.

**Step 1 — Assumptions**
- A spherical grain of radius $r$ presses into a flat workpiece.
- A vertical force $F$ acts on the grain (from tool impact).

**Step 2 — Hertzian contact radius**

For sphere-on-flat contact:

$$
a_c = \left(\frac{3 F r}{4 E^*}\right)^{1/3}
$$

where $E^*$ is the **effective combined elastic modulus**:

$$
\frac{1}{E^*} = \frac{1 - \nu_1^2}{E_1} + \frac{1 - \nu_2^2}{E_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum contact stress**

The peak compressive stress occurs at the centre of the contact circle:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{3F}{2\pi a_c^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $a_c$**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{3F}{2\pi} \left(\frac{4E^*}{3Fr}\right)^{2/3} = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{6 F E^{*2}}{r^2}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Conclusion:** When $\sigma_{max} \geq \sigma_{fracture}$ of the workpiece, the brittle material fractures and a chip is removed. This **Hertzian fracture model** is the universally accepted explanation for material removal in USM of brittle solids.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Parameters on Performance

| Process Parameter | MRR | Surface Finish (Ra) | HAZ / Damage | Tool Wear | Accuracy |
|------------------|-----|--------------------|--------------|-----------|----------|
| ↑ Static Load (P) | ↑ Increases | Slightly worse | Neutral | ↑ Increases | Neutral |
| ↑ Amplitude (a) | ↑ Increases | Worsens | Neutral | ↑ Increases | Neutral |
| ↑ Frequency (f) | ↑ Increases | Marginal effect | Neutral | Marginal | Marginal |
| ↑ Abrasive Size (d) | ↑ Increases | **Worsens significantly** | Increases | ↑ Increases | Decreases (oversize holes) |
| ↑ Abrasive Hardness | ↑ Increases | Improves | Neutral | ↑ Increases | Neutral |
| ↑ Workpiece Hardness (H_v) | ↓ Decreases | Improves | Neutral | ↓ Decreases | Improves |
| ↑ Slurry Concentration | ↑ up to optimum (~40–60%) | Variable | Neutral | Variable | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam-favourite graph:** Plotting MRR vs. Static Load shows MRR increases up to an optimum and then *plateaus or decreases* (because excess load crushes abrasive grains, dulling them and reducing cutting efficiency). This **non-linear behaviour at high load** is asked as a 3-mark short answer almost every semester.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** In a USM operation, the following data is given: static load $P = 10$ N, frequency $f = 20$ kHz, amplitude $a = 25$ µm, abrasive diameter $d = 100$ µm, workpiece Vickers hardness $H_v = 2000$ HV, process constant $C = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}$ mm³/(N·µm·Hz). Calculate the MRR using the empirical formula $MRR = C \cdot P \cdot d \cdot a \cdot f$.

**Solution:**

$$
MRR = C \cdot P \cdot d \cdot a \cdot f
$$

$$
MRR = (2.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times 10 \times 100 \times 25 \times (20 \times 10^3)
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
= (2.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times 10 = 2.5 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
\times 100 = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
\times 25 = 6.25 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\times (2 \times 10^4) = 1.25 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR = 1.25 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} = 0.75 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

This is a **typical USM MRR** (in the order of 1–15 mm³/min for most operations).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of USM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mains Power 50Hz] --> B[High Frequency Generator 20kHz]
    B --> C[Transducer Piezoelectric or Magnetostrictive]
    C --> D[Mechanical Amplifier Horn Concentrator]
    D --> E[Tool Sonotrode Shaped Cavity]
    E --> F[Abrasive Slurry B4C in Water]
    F --> G[Workpiece Hard Brittle Material]
    G --> H[Slurry Pump Recirculation Tank]
    H --> F
```

### 4.3 Mechanism of Chip Formation — Hertzian Impact Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1: Tool Retracts] --> B[State 2: Fresh Abrasive Enters Gap]
    B --> C[State 3: Tool Descends Pinches Abrasive]
    C --> D[State 4: Abrasive Impacts Workpiece Hertzian Fracture]
    D --> E[State 5: Microchip Detached Debris Flushed]
    E --> A

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2
    style B fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c
    style C fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#fbc02d
    style D fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style E fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2
```

### 4.4 Comparative Hierarchy of USM Elements

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[USM Process] --> B[Energy Source Domain]
    A --> C[Mechanical Domain]
    A --> D[Material Domain]

    C --> C1[Horn Amplifier]

    D --> D1[Abrasive Particles]
    D --> D2[Workpiece]

    E --> E1[Carrier Fluid Water]
    E --> E2[Slurry Pump]
    E --> E3[Recirculation System]

    style B fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams are KTU-recommended:** USM is a process-heavy topic, and the KTU examiner expects a **neat labelled sketch** in every descriptive answer. A block diagram showing the power chain + slurry loop + cutting zone + feedback scores **full 7 marks** in Part B questions, where the diagram itself carries 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2023]**
*List the five essential elements of the Ultrasonic Machining (USM) process and state the function of any two.*

**Model Answer:**

The five essential elements of the USM process are:

1. **Ultrasonic Power Supply / High-Frequency Generator** – Converts 50 Hz mains AC into 20 kHz high-frequency electrical signal.
2. **Transducer** – Converts high-frequency electrical energy into mechanical vibrations of the same frequency.
3. **Tool Holder / Horn (Concentrator)** – Mechanically amplifies the small amplitude from the transducer to the working amplitude at the tool tip.
4. **Tool (Sonotrode)** – A shaped tool (mirror image of the desired cavity) that transmits vibrations to the abrasive slurry.
5. **Abrasive Slurry** – Hard abrasive particles (B₄C, SiC, Al₂O₃) suspended in water that perform the actual material removal by impact.

**Function of any two:**
- *Transducer:* Converts electrical energy → mechanical vibrations (using piezoelectric or magnetostrictive effect).
- *Horn:* Amplifies vibration amplitude (from ~5 µm to ~25–50 µm) to enable cutting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Why is a soft material such as monel or brass used for the USM tool, even though the workpiece is hard?*

**Model Answer:**

The tool in USM should be made of a **soft, low-fatigue-strength material** (monel, mild steel, brass) because:

- The tool should **not be abraded** by the abrasive particles. If a hard tool were used, both the tool and the workpiece would wear, leading to poor dimensional accuracy.
- The tool's role is only to **transfer kinetic energy** to the abrasive particles through vibration. It does not directly cut the workpiece.
- A soft tool ensures the abrasive preferentially wears the (hard) workpiece, giving a good **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** and dimensional control.
- Soft materials also have better **fatigue resistance** under continuous high-frequency vibration, preventing tool fracture.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Dec 2019]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic diagram, explain the construction and working principle of the **Ultrasonic Machining (USM) process**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of **process parameters on Material Removal Rate (MRR)** in USM. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A(a):**

**Construction:**

A USM setup consists of the following components arranged in a vertical column:

1. **High-frequency generator (1):** Mounted separately; produces 20 kHz electrical signal from 50 Hz mains.
2. **Transducer (2):** Coupled below the generator; usually magnetostrictive (Ni) with water cooling coils wound around it.
3. **Coupling (3):** A solid mechanical joint connecting the transducer to the horn.
4. **Horn / Mechanical amplifier (4):** A tapered metallic block (monel/titanium) that amplifies vibration amplitude.
5. **Tool holder (5):** Threaded attachment to the horn.
6. **Tool (6):** Shaped as a negative of the required cavity, with a 1°–2° undercut on the side walls for slurry entry.
7. **Abrasive slurry (7):** Boron carbide particles (15–150 µm) suspended in water, continuously fed by a pump (8) from a slurry tank (9).
8. **Static load mechanism (10):** A pneumatic or weighted ram that applies a constant downward force $P$ (0.5–25 N) on the tool.
9. **Worktable (11):** Holds the workpiece; usually made of stainless steel with provision for slurry collection.

**Working Principle:**

- The 50 Hz mains supply is converted to a 20 kHz electrical signal by the high-frequency generator.
- The **transducer** converts this high-frequency electrical energy into mechanical vibrations of the same frequency.
- The **horn** amplifies the amplitude from ~5 µm to **20–50 µm**.
- The **tool**, vibrating at this amplitude and frequency, transmits its kinetic energy to the abrasive particles trapped in the gap (~25–50 µm) between the tool and the workpiece.
- The abrasive particles are **propelled at high velocity** against the workpiece and cause **brittle (Hertzian) fracture** of the workpiece material, removing micro-chips.
- The **slurry pump** continuously circulates fresh slurry to bring sharp abrasive into the cutting zone and flush away swarf.
- Material removal occurs only where the vibrating tool drives the abrasive against the workpiece, so the cavity produced is the mirror image of the tool shape.

**Diagram (already covered in Section 4 of these notes — refer to Block 4.2).**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A(b): Effect of Process Parameters on MRR**

The MRR in USM is empirically given by:

$$
MRR = C \cdot P \cdot d \cdot a \cdot f
$$

1. **Static Load (P):** Increasing static load increases MRR up to an **optimum value** (typically 5–15 N for small tools). Beyond this optimum, MRR plateaus or decreases because:
   - Excess load crushes the abrasive particles (dulling them).
   - The slurry is expelled from the gap, reducing abrasive supply.
   - Tool wear increases.

2. **Amplitude (a):** Higher amplitude increases the kinetic energy of each grain ($E_k \propto a^2$). MRR increases proportionally. However, very high amplitude damages the tool and causes brittle tool fracture.

3. **Frequency (f):** Higher frequency increases the number of impacts per second. MRR increases linearly with $f$. Practical limit is ~40 kHz (above this, transducer design becomes difficult).

4. **Abrasive Grain Size (d):** Larger grains remove more material per impact, increasing MRR. However, larger grains:
   - Worsen surface finish (higher Ra).
   - Cause oversize holes (radial overcut).
   - Increase tool wear.

5. **Abrasive Hardness:** Harder abrasives (B₄C > SiC > Al₂O₃) give higher MRR for hard workpieces.

6. **Workpiece Hardness (H_v):** MRR is **inversely proportional** to workpiece hardness. Brittle materials fracture more easily and are removed faster. Hard, tough materials resist USM.

7. **Slurry Concentration:** Optimum is 30–60% by weight. Too dilute → insufficient abrasive; too concentrated → viscous flow prevents fresh abrasive entry.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of material removal in USM** with reference to Hertzian fracture. Derive the **empirical expression for MRR**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the construction and performance of **piezoelectric and magnetostrictive transducers** used in USM. List any **four applications of USM**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B(a):**

**Mechanism of Material Removal:**

In USM, material removal does **not** occur by direct tool–workpiece contact. Instead, it occurs through the **indirect impact of abrasive particles** suspended in a slurry. The mechanism is explained as follows:

1. The vibrating tool tip oscillates at ultrasonic frequency with amplitude $a$.
2. Abrasive particles (e.g., B₄C) of diameter $d$ are trapped in the gap between the tool and the workpiece.
3. On the **forward stroke** of the tool, the particles are pinched between the tool and the workpiece and are **accelerated to high velocity** (≈ $2\pi f a$).
4. The particles impact the workpiece surface and exert a **Hertzian contact stress** $\sigma_c$ on the workpiece.
5. When this stress exceeds the **fracture strength** of the (brittle) workpiece, a small chip is detached — this is **Hertzian brittle fracture**.
6. The continuous, cyclic impacts (at ~20,000 per second) cumulatively erode the workpiece, producing a cavity matching the tool shape.
7. Three sub-mechanisms assist: **direct hammering**, **throwing** (particle ejection by tool), and **cavitation erosion** (collapse of slurry bubbles).

**Derivation of MRR** (presented stepwise in Section 3.1 of these notes):

**Final expression:**

$$
\boxed{MRR = \frac{C \, P \, d^3 \, \rho \, f^2 \, a^2}{H_v}}
$$

In simplified empirical form:

$$
MRR = C \cdot P \cdot d \cdot a \cdot f
$$

This shows MRR increases with load, grain size, amplitude, and frequency, and decreases with workpiece hardness.

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B(b):**

**Comparison of Piezoelectric vs. Magnetostrictive Transducers:**

| Feature | Piezoelectric Transducer | Magnetostrictive Transducer |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Principle** | Inverse piezoelectric effect | Magnetostriction of ferromagnetic materials |
| **Materials** | PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), Barium Titanate, Quartz | Nickel, Permendur (49% Co, 49% Fe, 2% V), Alfer (Al + Fe) |
| **Conversion efficiency** | High (~95–96%) | Low (~20–40%) due to eddy current losses |
| **Amplitude produced** | Low (~0.005–0.01 mm) | Higher (~0.005–0.025 mm) |
| **Cooling** | Not required | Water cooling mandatory |
| **Frequency stability** | Excellent | Good, but can drift with temperature |
| **Size** | Compact, lightweight | Bulky |
| **Power handling** | Limited to moderate powers | Can handle very high power |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | Moderate |
| **Service life** | Long (no moving electrical parts) | Shorter (fatigue of magnetic material) |
| **Best suited for** | Precision micro-machining, lab use | Industrial heavy-duty USM |

**Four Applications of USM:**

1. **Drilling and slicing of semiconductor wafers** (silicon, germanium) for chip manufacturing.
2. **Machining of ceramics and glass** — making holes, cavities, and intricate shapes in alumina, quartz, and Pyrex glass.
3. **Engraving and profiling of tungsten carbide dies** used in wire drawing and stamping.
4. **Dental and surgical tool manufacturing** — machining of ceramic dental implants and precision surgical components.
5. *(Bonus, if asked)* Machining of precious stones, ruby bearings for watches, and aerospace CMCs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Diagram Marks Lost:** Many students draw the USM setup but forget to label the slurry tank, pump, transducer cooling coils, or the static load mechanism. Each missing label costs **0.5 marks** (3–4 labels missed = 2 marks lost).
> 2. **Confusing Transducer Types:** Writing "magnetostriction produces electricity" instead of "magnetic field produces strain" is a classic 1-mark deduction. Remember: it's the *inverse* effect — electricity → mechanical strain.
> 3. **MRR Derivation Sign Error:** A common mistake is to write $MRR \propto H_v$ (positive) instead of $MRR \propto 1/H_v$ (inverse). Always mention that **harder workpiece → lower MRR**.
> 4. **Forgetting Hertzian Fracture:** Simply stating "abrasive hits workpiece" is incomplete. You **must** name the **Hertzian brittle fracture** mechanism for full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up USM with EDM:** Some students incorrectly state that USM requires electrically conductive workpieces. **It does not** — this is the unique advantage of USM. Lose 1 mark if you confuse the two.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **USM = Mechanical-type non-conventional process** using **abrasive + vibration** (no electricity through workpiece, no chemistry, no heat).
- **Frequency:** 20–40 kHz (ultrasonic range, above human hearing).
- **Amplitude:** 15–50 µm.
- **The 5 elements:** Generator → Transducer → Horn → Tool → Slurry (on Workpiece).
- **Transducer types:** **Piezoelectric (PZT)** = high efficiency, low amplitude; **Magnetostrictive (Ni)** = high amplitude, needs water cooling.
- **Horn function:** Amplify amplitude from ~5 µm → 25–50 µm.
- **Tool material:** Soft (monel, brass) — must NOT be abraded.
- **Best abrasive:** Boron Carbide (B₄C) for hardest workpieces; SiC for general use; Al₂O₃ for glass.
- **Mechanism:** Hertzian brittle fracture + hammering + throwing + cavitation.
- **MRR formula (empirical):** $MRR = C \cdot P \cdot d \cdot a \cdot f$
- **MRR ∝ 1/H_v** (harder workpiece → lower MRR).
- **MRR is NOT linear with load** — has an **optimum** beyond which it decreases.
- **MRR is NOT linear with grain size** — larger grains give higher MRR but worse finish and accuracy.
- **Typical MRR range:** **1 to 15 mm³/min** (much slower than conventional machining).
- **Typical applications:** Glass, ceramics, quartz, silicon wafers, tungsten carbide dies, precious stones.
- **Key advantage over EDM:** Works on **non-conductive** materials.
- **Key disadvantage:** **Low MRR**, tool wear, limited to brittle materials.
- **Hole quality:** USM produces holes with **slight overcut** (~0.025–0.05 mm larger than tool) and **taper** (top wider than bottom), unlike EDM.
- **Slurry:** 30–60% concentration by weight; recirculated continuously.
- **Undercut on tool:** 1°–2° taper for abrasive flow.
- **Static load range:** 0.5–25 N; **Optimum is critical** — too low = low MRR, too high = abrasive crushing.
- **Frequency effect:** Higher $f$ = more impacts/sec = higher MRR; practical upper limit ~40 kHz.
- **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR):** Soft tool on hard workpiece → low TWR (good); equal hardness → high TWR (bad).
- **Cavitation:** Slurry bubble collapse assists in material removal (minor effect).
- **Horn shapes:** Cylindrical, conical, exponential, stepped, catenoidal — **exponential** gives uniform amplification.
- **Amplification ratio (conical horn):** $\beta = D_1 / D_2$.
- **Hertzian stress formula:** $\sigma_c = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a_c^2}$ — must be ≥ fracture stress of workpiece for chip formation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of "Effect of Parameters" in Advanced Manufacturing

In the context of **Advanced / Non-Traditional Manufacturing Processes (NTMPs)**, the term *"Effect of Parameters"* refers to the systematic study of how controllable input variables (process parameters) of an unconventional machining process influence the resulting **process performance characteristics** (process outputs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Process parameters are the independent, controllable input variables (such as pressure, flow rate, grit size, power, frequency, stand-off distance, etc.) of a manufacturing process whose direct or indirect variation causes a measurable change in the dependent output variables — namely **Material Removal Rate (MRR)**, **Surface Roughness (Ra)**, **Tool Wear Rate (TWR)**, **Dimensional Accuracy (DA)**, **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)**, and **Kerf Width (KW)**.*

### 1.2 Categories of Process Parameters

| Category | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Parameters** | Directly controlled by the operator | Pressure, Voltage, Current, Frequency |
| **Secondary Parameters** | Derived from primary parameters | Power, Energy per pulse, MRR |
| **Fixed Parameters** | Cannot be easily altered during process | Nozzle diameter, Tool geometry |
| **Variable Parameters** | Adjusted based on workpiece / tool | Abrasive grit size, Tool material |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Watering the Garden" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are watering a garden using a hose with a spray nozzle.
> - **Water Pressure** = how hard water hits the soil. Higher pressure digs deeper (more material removal).
> - **Nozzle Size (Orifice Diameter)** = the cross-section of the jet. Larger nozzle = more water flow = faster erosion.
> - **Standoff Distance** = how far the hose is from the soil. Too close = concentrated but small area; too far = dispersed and weak.
> - **Abrasive (Sand mixed)** = like adding sand to the water jet — it cuts through hard surfaces like wood or stone.
> - **Time of exposure** = longer you spray, more material is removed.
>
> In manufacturing, **adjusting these parameters** is exactly how an engineer "fine-tunes" the process to get the right depth, finish, and accuracy — just like a gardener adjusting to water plants properly without damaging them.

## 1.4 Process Performance Outputs Commonly Studied

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) affected by parameters:**
> 1. **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — measured in **mm³/min** or **g/min**
> 2. **Surface Roughness (Ra)** — measured in **μm** (micrometers)
> 3. **Tool Wear Rate (TWR)** — measured in **μm/min**
> 4. **Dimensional Accuracy / Overcut** — measured in **mm**
> 5. **Kerf Width** — measured in **mm**
> 6. **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — relevant for thermal processes

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Framework: Input → Process → Output

Every unconventional manufacturing process can be conceptualized as a **transfer function** in which input parameters are transformed into output characteristics through physical/chemical mechanisms.

$$\text{Inputs} \; (P_1, P_2, P_3, \dots) \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Process Physics}\;}\; \text{Outputs} \; (\text{MRR}, R_a, \text{TWR}, \dots)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study the effect of parameters?**
> - To **optimize** the process for maximum productivity and minimum cost.
> - To **predict** output behavior without performing costly trial runs.
> - To achieve **desired quality** (e.g., low surface roughness, high accuracy).
> - To understand **limitations** of the process for selection in industry.

## 2.2 Effect of Parameters in Mechanical Energy-Based Processes (Module 1 Focus)

Since Module 1 of KTU's *Advanced Manufacturing Processes* syllabus primarily covers **mechanical energy-based unconventional processes** (AJM, USM, WJM, AWJM), the following analysis is the high-yield focus area.

### 2.2.1 Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)

**Working Principle:** High-velocity abrasive particles (typically Al₂O₃) carried by a gas jet impinge on the workpiece and cause micro-chipping.

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Roughness (Ra) |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas Pressure (P)** ↑ | **MRR ↑** (almost linearly) | Ra increases (deeper craters) |
| **Abrasive Flow Rate (mₐ)** ↑ | MRR ↑ up to optimum, then ↓ (nozzle choking) | Ra increases |
| **Grit Size (d)** ↑ | MRR ↑ (larger impact energy) | Ra increases (rougher surface) |
| **Nozzle Tip Distance (SOD)** ↑ | MRR ↓ (jet disperses) | Ra decreases (less concentrated impact) |
| **Abrasive Type** | Harder abrasive (SiC > Al₂O₃) gives higher MRR | — |
| **Nozzle Diameter (D)** ↑ | MRR ↑ (more abrasive flow) | Ra may increase slightly |

**MRR Formulation for AJM:**

$$\text{MRR}_{AJM} = K \cdot P^a \cdot d^b \cdot m_a^c$$

Where:
- $K$ = process constant
- $P$ = gas pressure
- $d$ = average abrasive grit diameter
- $m_a$ = abrasive mass flow rate
- $a, b, c$ = experimentally determined exponents (typically $a \approx 1.0$, $b \approx 1.5$, $c \approx 0.6$)

### 2.2.2 Ultrasonic Machining (USM)

**Working Principle:** Abrasive slurry is hammered by a vibrating tool (frequency ~20–40 kHz). Material removal occurs by **direct hammering** and **cavity erosion (suspension hammering)**.

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Ra / TWR |
|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude of vibration (A)** ↑ | **MRR ↑** (more impact energy) | TWR ↑, Ra ↑ |
| **Frequency (f)** ↑ | MRR ↑ (more impacts per second) | TWR ↑, Ra moderate |
| **Static Load (W)** ↑ | MRR ↑ (tool pressed harder) | TWR ↑ significantly |
| **Abrasive Grit Size (d)** ↑ | MRR ↑ up to optimum, then ↓ | Ra ↑ (rougher) |
| **Abrasive Concentration** ↑ | MRR ↑ up to optimum, then ↓ | Ra ↑ slightly |
| **Tool Material Hardness** | Softer tool → more TWR | — |
| **Workpiece Hardness ↑** | MRR ↓ (harder to remove) | Ra ↓ (smoother) |
| **Slurry Temperature** | MRR peaks at moderate temperature | — |

**MRR Formulation for USM:**

$$\text{MRR}_{USM} = \frac{C \cdot W \cdot A \cdot f \cdot d}{H_w}$$

Where:
- $C$ = constant
- $W$ = static tool load
- $A$ = amplitude of vibration
- $f$ = frequency
- $d$ = abrasive grain size
- $H_w$ = hardness of workpiece

### 2.2.3 Water Jet Machining (WJM) & Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)

**Working Principle:** High-velocity water jet (with or without abrasive) erodes material.

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Ra / Kerf |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Pressure (P)** ↑ | **MRR ↑** (kinetic energy ∝ P) | Kerf width ↑ |
| **Orifice Diameter (d₀)** ↑ | MRR ↑ | Kerf ↑ |
| **Standoff Distance (SOD)** ↑ | MRR ↓ (jet disperses) | Kerf ↑ (wider taper) |
| **Traverse Speed (V)** ↑ | MRR ↓ (less time on workpiece) | Ra ↑ (rougher) |
| **Abrasive Flow Rate** ↑ (AWJM) | MRR ↑ significantly | Ra ↓ (cleaner cut) |
| **Abrasive Grit Size** ↑ (AWJM) | MRR ↑ | Ra ↑ |
| **Jet Impact Angle** | 90° gives max MRR; oblique → less MRR but smoother | Taper increases |

**Kinetic Energy of Jet:**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Insight:** Because MRR depends on $v^3$ (or $P^{1.5}$), small changes in pressure cause **large** changes in MRR. This is why pressure is the most dominant parameter in WJM/AWJM.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as a quick-reference revision sheet before the exam. Avoid using the pipe character `\|` inside cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| Process | Key Equation | Units | Dominant Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AJM** | $\text{MRR} = K \cdot P^a \cdot d^b \cdot m_a^c$ | mm³/min | Gas Pressure $P$ |
| **USM** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{C \cdot W \cdot A \cdot f \cdot d}{H_w}$ | mm³/min | Static Load $W$, Amplitude $A$ |
| **WJM Kinetic Energy** | $KE = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Joules | Jet velocity $v$ |
| **WJM/AWJM Velocity** | $v = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$ | m/s | Water Pressure $P$ |
| **AWJM MRR (empirical)** | $\text{MRR} = k \cdot P^{0.6} \cdot m_a^{0.4} \cdot d^{0.3} \cdot v^{-0.2}$ | g/min | Pressure + Abrasive flow |
| **Surface Roughness (AWJM)** | $R_a = K' \cdot d_g^{0.5} \cdot V^{0.3}$ | μm | Grit + Traverse speed |
| **Taper in WJM/AWJM** | $\text{Taper} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{D_0 - D_b}{2 \cdot \text{SOD}}\right)$ | degrees | SOD, Orifice dia. |
| **Power of Jet** | $P_{jet} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot v^2 = P \cdot Q$ | Watts | Pressure × Flow |
| **MRR — Volume basis** | $\text{MRR} = v \cdot w \cdot t$ | mm³/min | Traverse speed, Kerf |
| **Abrasive Velocity (after mixing)** | $v_a = \dfrac{Q_w}{Q_w + Q_a} \cdot v_j$ | m/s | Water flow ratio |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Studying Parameter Effects:**
> - **Aerospace**: AWJM is used for cutting composites, titanium, and Inconel — *parameter optimization is critical* to avoid delamination.
> - **Automotive**: USM is used for machining ceramics and glass — choosing the right *amplitude and grit* extends tool life.
> - **Electronics**: AJM is used for micro-machining brittle wafers — *low pressure and fine grit* give mirror finish.
> - **Defense & Mining**: WJM/AWJM cuts armor plates and stone — *high pressure and high abrasive flow* are needed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Removal Rate in Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)

The MRR in AJM is derived from the energy transferred by abrasive particles to the workpiece surface.

**Step 1: Number of abrasive particles striking per unit time**

Let $\dot{m}_a$ be the abrasive mass flow rate (kg/s) and $m_p$ be the mass of a single particle (kg).

$$N = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{m_p}$$

The mass of a single (assumed spherical) particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_a$:

$$m_p = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_a$$

So the number of particles per second:

$$N = \frac{6 \dot{m}_a}{\pi d^3 \rho_a}$$

**Step 2: Kinetic energy of one particle**

The abrasive particle is accelerated by the gas jet. Assuming the particle reaches gas velocity $v$:

$$KE_{particle} = \frac{1}{2} m_p v^2 = \frac{\pi}{12} d^3 \rho_a v^2$$

**Step 3: Volume removed per impact**

The volume of material removed per impact is empirically related to the kinetic energy of impact and the hardness of the workpiece $H_w$:

$$V_{per\;impact} = \frac{KE_{particle}}{\eta \cdot H_w} = \frac{\pi d^3 \rho_a v^2}{12 \eta H_w}$$

Where $\eta$ is an efficiency factor (typically $\eta \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$).

**Step 4: Total MRR**

$$\text{MRR} = N \cdot V_{per\;impact}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{MRR} = \left(\frac{6 \dot{m}_a}{\pi d^3 \rho_a}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3 \rho_a v^2}{12 \eta H_w}\right)$$

Cancel $\pi$ and $d^3 \rho_a$:

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{6 \dot{m}_a \cdot v^2}{12 \eta H_w} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot v^2}{2 \eta H_w}$$

**Step 5: Introduce gas pressure**

From Bernoulli's principle, gas (and hence abrasive) velocity is related to gas pressure $P$ and gas density $\rho_g$:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2P}{\rho_g}}$$

Substituting $v^2 = \frac{2P}{\rho_g}$:

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{2 \eta H_w} \cdot \frac{2P}{\rho_g} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot P}{\eta \cdot \rho_g \cdot H_w}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR}_{AJM} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot P}{\eta \cdot \rho_g \cdot H_w}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> - MRR is **directly proportional** to abrasive flow rate $\dot{m}_a$ and gas pressure $P$.
> - MRR is **inversely proportional** to workpiece hardness $H_w$.
> - This is why softer materials (glass, ceramics) are machined faster than hardened steels in AJM.

## 3.2 Derivation: MRR in Ultrasonic Machining (USM)

**Step 1: Volume swept by tool per oscillation**

In one oscillation, the tool tip moves a distance equal to **twice the amplitude** ($2A$). The tool has a cross-sectional area $A_t$.

$$V_{per\;osc} = A_t \cdot 2A$$

**Step 2: Frequency consideration**

If the frequency is $f$ (oscillations per second), volume swept per second:

$$V_{swept/s} = A_t \cdot 2A \cdot f$$

**Step 3: Fraction that causes actual material removal**

Not every oscillation removes material — a fraction $\phi$ (efficiency) actually causes chipping. $\phi$ depends on the static load $W$ and the workpiece hardness $H_w$:

$$\phi = \frac{K' \cdot W}{H_w}$$

Where $K'$ is an experimental constant.

**Step 4: Total MRR**

$$\text{MRR}_{USM} = A_t \cdot 2A \cdot f \cdot \phi = \frac{2 K' \cdot A_t \cdot A \cdot f \cdot W}{H_w}$$

Dropping the constants into a single coefficient $C$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR}_{USM} = \frac{C \cdot A \cdot f \cdot W}{H_w}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** MRR in USM is **linearly proportional** to amplitude $A$, frequency $f$, and static load $W$ — but **inversely** to workpiece hardness $H_w$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Combined Effect Summary

The following table is the **single most important analytical matrix** for the KTU exam on this topic:

| Parameter | AJM MRR | AJM Ra | USM MRR | USM Ra | WJM MRR | AWJM MRR | AWJM Ra |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Pressure | ↑↑ | ↑ | — | — | ↑↑↑ | ↑↑↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Abrasive flow | ↑ (optimum) | ↑ | — | — | — | ↑↑ | ↓ |
| ↑ Grit size | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ (optimum) | ↑ | — | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Standoff dist. | ↓ | ↓ | — | — | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| ↑ Amplitude | — | — | ↑↑ | ↑ | — | — | — |
| ↑ Frequency | — | — | ↑ | — | — | — | — |
| ↑ Static load | — | — | ↑↑ | ↑ | — | — | — |
| ↑ Traverse speed | — | — | — | — | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| ↑ Workpiece hardness | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |

**Legend:** ↑↑ = strong increase, ↑ = increase, ↓ = decrease, ↓↓ = strong decrease, — = no major effect.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem:** In an AJM operation, the gas pressure is 5 bar, abrasive flow rate is 5 g/s, workpiece is glass (Vickers hardness 5 GPa), abrasive is Al₂O₃ (density 3950 kg/m³), gas is air (density 1.2 kg/m³), and efficiency factor is 0.2. Calculate the MRR.

**Given:**
- $P = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\dot{m}_a = 5 \text{ g/s} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$
- $H_w = 5 \text{ GPa} = 5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $\eta = 0.2$
- $\rho_g = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Solution:**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot P}{\eta \cdot \rho_g \cdot H_w}$$

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (5 \times 10^5)}{(0.2) \cdot (1.2) \cdot (5 \times 10^9)}$$

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{3}}{1.2 \times 10^{9}} = 2.083 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to mm³/min:

$$\text{MRR} = 2.083 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^9 \times 60 = 125 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR} \approx 125 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}\;}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Process Parameter → Output Relationship (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input Parameter: Pressure P] --> proc1[Process Physics: Energy Transfer]
    inputB[Input Parameter: Abrasive Flow ma] --> proc1
    inputC[Input Parameter: Grit Size d] --> proc1
    inputD[Input Parameter: Standoff Distance SOD] --> proc1
    inputE[Input Parameter: Amplitude A] --> proc1
    inputF[Input Parameter: Frequency f] --> proc1
    inputG[Input Parameter: Static Load W] --> proc1
    inputH[Input Parameter: Traverse Speed V] --> proc1

    proc1 --> branch1{Which Process?}

    branch1 -->|AJM| outA1[MRR proportional to P and ma]
    branch1 -->|AJM| outA2[Ra increases with d and P]
    branch1 -->|USM| outB1[MRR proportional to A f W]
    branch1 -->|USM| outB2[TWR increases with W and A]
    branch1 -->|WJM| outC1[MRR proportional to v cubed]
    branch1 -->|WJM| outC2[Kerf depends on SOD]
    branch1 -->|AWJM| outD1[MRR enhanced by abrasive addition]
    branch1 -->|AWJM| outD2[Ra depends on grit and traverse]

    outA1 --> finalO[Final Process Output]
    outA2 --> finalO
    outB1 --> finalO
    outB2 --> finalO
    outC1 --> finalO
    outC2 --> finalO
    outD1 --> finalO
    outD2 --> finalO

    finalO --> kpi1[KPI 1: MRR]
    finalO --> kpi2[KPI 2: Surface Roughness Ra]
    finalO --> kpi3[KPI 3: Tool Wear Rate]
    finalO --> kpi4[KPI 4: Dimensional Accuracy]
    finalO --> kpi5[KPI 5: Kerf Width]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Effect Direction Map (Influence Matrix)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pressure] -->|+| MRR[MRR]
    P -->|+| Ra[Surface Roughness]
    SOD[Standoff Distance] -->|-| MRR
    SOD -->|-| Ra
    d[Grit Size] -->|+| MRR
    d -->|+| Ra
    ma[Abrasive Flow] -->|+ to optimum| MRR
    A[Amplitude] -->|+| USM_MRR[USM MRR]
    f[Frequency] -->|+| USM_MRR
    W[Static Load] -->|+| USM_MRR
    W -->|+| TWR[Tool Wear]
    V[Traverse Speed] -->|-| MRR
    V -->|+| Ra
    Hw[Workpiece Hardness] -->|-| MRR
```

## 4.3 Text-Based Schematic: AJM Process Parameter Interaction

```
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                  AJM PROCESS CELL                    │
  │                                                      │
  │  GAS TANK ──► PRESSURE REGULATOR ──► NOZZLE ──┐     │
  │       │                                        │     │
  │       │          ┌───► STANDBY VALVE           │     │
  │       │          │                             ▼     │
  │  ABRASIVE ──► MIXING CHAMBER ◄──── GAS JET  [JET]  │
  │  HOPPER           │                              │  │
  │       │           │                              ▼  │
  │       │           │                      WORKPIECE   │
  │       │           ▼                              │  │
  │       │      STREAM OF                  COLLECTION │
  │       │      ABRASIVE PARTICLES          CHAMBER   │
  │       │           │                              │  │
  │       ▼           ▼                              ▼  │
  │  [ GAS P = ? ] [ ma = ? ] [ SOD = ? ] [ d = ? ]    │
  │       │           │            │            │       │
  │       └───────────┴────────────┴────────────┘       │
  │                       │                             │
  │                       ▼                             │
  │              ┌──────────────────┐                   │
  │              │  PROCESS OUTPUT  │                   │
  │              ├──────────────────┤                   │
  │              │  MRR (mm³/min)   │                   │
  │              │  Ra (μm)         │                   │
  │              │  Taper (deg)     │                   │
  │              │  Overcut (mm)    │                   │
  │              └──────────────────┘                   │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *"List any four process parameters that affect the Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four primary process parameters affecting MRR in AJM are:

1. **Gas Pressure (P):** MRR increases almost linearly with gas pressure because higher pressure accelerates abrasive particles to higher velocities, increasing their kinetic energy.

2. **Abrasive Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}_a$):** Higher abrasive flow delivers more impacting particles per second, increasing MRR — but excessive flow causes nozzle choking and MRR drops.

3. **Abrasive Grit Size (d):** Larger grit particles carry more kinetic energy per impact, increasing MRR. However, MRR is also influenced by grit size exponentially ($d^{1.5}$).

4. **Nozzle Tip Standoff Distance (SOD):** MRR decreases with increasing SOD because the jet disperses and loses kinetic energy before reaching the workpiece.

*(Optional 5th for safety: Nozzle diameter, Abrasive type, Workpiece hardness)*

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *"Explain with a sketch the effect of amplitude of vibration on MRR and surface finish in USM."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In Ultrasonic Machining (USM), the tool vibrates with a longitudinal amplitude (typically 15–50 μm) at ultrasonic frequencies (20–40 kHz).

- **Effect on MRR:** As the amplitude of vibration **increases**, the kinetic energy of each impact of abrasive particles increases, leading to **higher MRR** (proportional to amplitude). However, beyond a certain amplitude, tool wear becomes excessive and MRR may drop.

- **Effect on Surface Finish:** Higher amplitude produces **rougher surfaces** (higher Ra) because deeper indentations are made per impact. Lower amplitude gives smoother finish.

- **Sketch (verbal):** A tool vibrating up and down with amplitude A in a slurry of abrasive — larger A means deeper penetration into the workpiece per oscillation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[Internal Choice Option 1]*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM) in terms of process parameters. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss in detail the effect of various process parameters on MRR and surface roughness in AJM. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a): Derivation of MRR in AJM

**Step 1:** Consider a single spherical abrasive particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_a$. Its mass is:

$$m_p = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_a$$

**Step 2:** When accelerated by gas to velocity $v$, its kinetic energy is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m_p v^2 = \frac{\pi}{12} d^3 \rho_a v^2$$

**Step 3:** This kinetic energy is used to remove a small volume $V_i$ of workpiece material of hardness $H_w$, with an efficiency $\eta$:

$$V_i = \frac{KE}{\eta H_w} = \frac{\pi d^3 \rho_a v^2}{12 \eta H_w}$$

**Step 4:** Number of particles striking per unit time, given abrasive mass flow rate $\dot{m}_a$:

$$N = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{m_p} = \frac{6 \dot{m}_a}{\pi d^3 \rho_a}$$

**Step 5:** Total MRR = Number of particles × Volume per particle:

$$\text{MRR} = N \cdot V_i = \left(\frac{6 \dot{m}_a}{\pi d^3 \rho_a}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^3 \rho_a v^2}{12 \eta H_w}\right) = \frac{\dot{m}_a v^2}{2 \eta H_w}$$

**Step 6:** Using Bernoulli's equation, $v^2 = \dfrac{2P}{\rho_g}$, where $P$ is gas pressure and $\rho_g$ is gas density:

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR}_{AJM} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot P}{\eta \cdot \rho_g \cdot H_w}\;}$$

**Conclusion:** MRR is **directly proportional** to abrasive flow rate and gas pressure, and **inversely proportional** to workpiece hardness and gas density.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b): Effect of Process Parameters on MRR and Ra in AJM

The following parameters influence MRR and Surface Roughness in AJM:

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Ra (Surface Roughness) |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas Pressure (P) ↑** | MRR ↑ linearly (dominant parameter) | Ra ↑ (deeper craters formed) |
| **Abrasive Flow Rate ($\dot{m}_a$) ↑** | MRR ↑ up to optimum value, then ↓ due to nozzle choking | Ra increases |
| **Grit Size (d) ↑** | MRR ↑ (larger impact energy) | Ra ↑ significantly (rougher) |
| **Standoff Distance (SOD) ↑** | MRR ↓ (jet disperses) | Ra ↓ (less concentrated impact) |
| **Abrasive Type** | Harder abrasives (SiC, diamond) → higher MRR | Harder abrasives → may give different finish |
| **Nozzle Diameter ↑** | MRR ↑ (more particles per second) | Marginal effect |
| **Workpiece Hardness ↑** | MRR ↓↓ (most significant negative factor) | Ra ↓ (smoother on hard material) |
| **Carrier Gas Type** | Heavier gas (CO₂) gives more momentum → higher MRR | — |

**Graphical Description (expected in answer):**
- A plot of **MRR vs Gas Pressure** is a **near-linear increasing curve**.
- A plot of **MRR vs SOD** is a **decreasing curve** (often exponential decay).
- A plot of **Ra vs Grit Size** is an **increasing curve**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[Internal Choice Option 2]*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for Material Removal Rate in Ultrasonic Machining (USM) and explain the role of each parameter. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the effect of process parameters on MRR in AJM, USM, and AWJM. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a): Derivation of MRR in USM

**Step 1:** Tool cross-section area = $A_t$; Amplitude of vibration = $A$; Frequency = $f$.

**Step 2:** Volume swept by tool per second:

$$V_{swept} = A_t \cdot 2A \cdot f$$

**Step 3:** Only a fraction $\phi$ of the swept volume actually causes material removal. This fraction depends on the static load $W$ and workpiece hardness $H_w$:

$$\phi = \frac{K' W}{H_w}$$

**Step 4:** Hence the actual MRR:

$$\text{MRR}_{USM} = A_t \cdot 2A \cdot f \cdot \phi = \frac{2 K' \cdot A_t \cdot A \cdot f \cdot W}{H_w}$$

**Step 5:** Combining all constants into a single constant $C$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR}_{USM} = \frac{C \cdot A \cdot f \cdot W}{H_w}\;}$$

**Role of Each Parameter:**

| Parameter | Role |
|---|---|
| **Amplitude (A)** | Higher A → larger impact velocity → higher MRR (linear) |
| **Frequency (f)** | Higher f → more impacts per second → higher MRR (linear) |
| **Static Load (W)** | Higher W → tool pressed harder into slurry → more impacts → higher MRR (linear) |
| **Workpiece Hardness ($H_w$)** | Higher $H_w$ → harder to remove → lower MRR (inverse) |
| **Abrasive Grit Size** | Larger grit → higher MRR up to optimum, then drops |
| **Abrasive Concentration** | Optimum concentration (~30–40% by volume in slurry) gives max MRR |

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b): Comparison of Parameter Effects in AJM, USM, AWJM

| Parameter | AJM | USM | AWJM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dominant Parameter** | Gas Pressure | Static Load + Amplitude | Water Pressure + Abrasive Flow |
| **MRR vs Pressure** | Linear ↑ | Not applicable | $P^{1.5}$ ↑ (strong) |
| **MRR vs Abrasive Grit Size** | Increases | Increases up to optimum | Increases |
| **MRR vs Workpiece Hardness** | Inversely proportional | Inversely proportional | Inversely proportional |
| **Abrasive Flow Rate Effect** | MRR ↑ to optimum | MRR ↑ to optimum (slurry concentration) | MRR ↑ monotonically |
| **Standoff Distance Effect** | MRR ↓ with SOD | Not applicable | MRR ↓ with SOD |
| **Typical MRR** | 0.1–50 mm³/min | 15–1500 mm³/min | 50–5000 mm³/min |
| **Best For** | Micro-machining, brittle materials | Brittle ceramics, glass | Thick metal/composite cutting |
| **Tool Wear Concern** | Nozzle wear | Tool wear significant | Nozzle + focusing tube wear |
| **Heat Affected Zone** | None (cold process) | None | None |

**Conclusion:** AWJM has the highest MRR and is the most versatile. USM is best for non-conductive brittle materials. AJM is for precision micro-machining. None of the three produces a heat-affected zone — a key advantage of mechanical processes over thermal NTMPs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Lead to Mark Deduction in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners *strictly deduct* 0.5–1 mark for missing units like mm³/min or μm. **Always write units explicitly.**
> 2. **Confusing MRR formulas of AJM and USM** — Students often mix up the role of *amplitude* and *abrasive flow rate*. In AJM, abrasive flow is the key; in USM, amplitude of tool vibration is the key.
> 3. **Writing "MRR increases with pressure" without specifying the relationship** — Examiners expect either a *linear*, *proportional*, or *power-law* relationship. Vague answers lose marks.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of spherical particles** in derivations — In AJM derivations, always mention that abrasive particles are assumed *spherical* and of *uniform size*.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert SI units** — Pressure from bar to Pa, hardness from GPa to Pa, etc. Many students lose 1 mark on unit conversion errors.
> 6. **Missing the inverse relationship with workpiece hardness** — Almost every MRR formula contains $H_w$ in the denominator; skipping this is a common error.
> 7. **Not mentioning efficiency factor $\eta$** — AJM derivations must include the *energy transfer efficiency* $\eta$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for the Topic "Effect of Parameters":**

- **AJM**: MRR $\propto$ Gas Pressure × Abrasive flow; MRR $\propto d^{1.5}$ (grit size exponent); MRR $\propto 1/H_w$.
- **USM**: MRR $\propto$ Amplitude × Frequency × Static Load; MRR $\propto 1/H_w$; MRR $\propto d$ (grit size linearly).
- **WJM**: KE $\propto v^3$; velocity $v = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$; pressure is the most dominant parameter.
- **AWJM**: MRR $\propto P^{0.6} \cdot m_a^{0.4} \cdot d^{0.3}$; pressure and abrasive flow both matter.
- **Standoff Distance (SOD)**: ↑ SOD → ↓ MRR, ↓ Ra, ↑ Taper (in WJM/AWJM).
- **Grit Size (d)**: ↑ d → ↑ MRR (up to optimum), ↑ Ra (always rougher).
- **Workpiece Hardness ($H_w$)**: ↑ $H_w$ → ↓ MRR (consistent across all three processes).
- **Mechanical processes have NO Heat Affected Zone** — major advantage over EDM, LBM, etc.
- **Abrasive type ranking (hardness)**: Diamond > SiC > Al₂O₃ > Glass beads > Sand.
- **Taper angle in WJM/AWJM** = $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{D_0 - D_b}{2 \cdot SOD}\right)$ — increase SOD → increase taper.
- **MRR in USM is independent of static load beyond a threshold** — too much load causes tool fracture.
- **For KTU 14-mark derivations**: Always show 5–6 explicit steps, include assumptions, state the final boxed equation, and interpret the result.
- **For 3-mark answers**: Use tabular format whenever listing "effects of parameters" — examiners give full marks faster for well-structured tables.
- **Cold processes** (AJM, USM, WJM, AWJM) are preferred for **heat-sensitive materials** (titanium, composites, bio-implants).
- **AJM is best for micro-machining**; **AWJM is best for thick-section cutting**; **USM is best for ceramics and glass**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEIET521 – Advanced Manufacturing Processes (AMP)**, the term **"Applications"** refers to the systematic mapping of Non-Traditional Manufacturing (NTM) / Advanced Manufacturing processes to real engineering components, materials, and industrial sectors where conventional machining fails to deliver acceptable results. Under the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 1 introduces the classification, working principles, and the **engineering application envelope** of unconventional material removal, joining, and forming processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Advanced Manufacturing Processes* are defined as those processes that remove, join, or deform material using mechanisms **other than the traditional mechanical action of a sharp cutting tool on a harder workpiece** — relying instead on electrical, chemical, thermal, optical, or kinetic energy.

The **application domain** of each AMP is governed by four primary selection parameters:

1. **Workpiece Material Properties** — hardness ($\geq 40$ HRC), brittleness, chemical reactivity, melting point.
2. **Geometric Complexity** — aspect ratio, internal cavity, micro-feature requirement.
3. **Surface Integrity Demand** — heat-affected zone (HAZ) tolerance, residual stress limit, surface roughness ($R_a$).
4. **Production Volume & Tolerance** — batch size, dimensional tolerance ($\pm \mu m$ scale).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Surgeon's Toolkit"**
> Imagine a surgeon who has a single scalpel (the conventional lathe/milling tool). For soft tissue, fine incision, bone cutting, and cauterization of blood vessels, **one tool cannot serve all purposes**. The surgeon therefore uses specialized tools — a laser (for cauterization), an ultrasonic cutter (for bone), a chemical agent (for dissolving tumors), and an electric scalpel (for sealing vessels).
>
> Similarly, when a mechanical engineer encounters a workpiece of **tungsten carbide, Inconel, titanium, or a non-conductive ceramic**, the conventional HSS or carbide tool fails. The engineer must pick the right "energy form" from the advanced manufacturing toolkit — **thermal, electrical, chemical, or kinetic energy** — to achieve the desired material removal, joining, or forming action.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance

The application spectrum of AMPs spans across critical industries:

| Industry Sector | Typical AMP Used | Component Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | EDM, LBM, EBM | Turbine blade cooling holes, combustion chamber slots |
| **Automotive** | AJM, WJM, ECM | Gear tooth finishing, engine block deburring |
| **Die & Mould** | EDM (Wire & Sinker), ECM | Cavity profiles in hardened tool steel |
| **Electronics** | LBM, ECM, USM | PCB drilling, micro-vias, silicon wafer dicing |
| **Medical Implants** | LBM, EBM, ECM | Stent cutting, orthopedic implant surface texturing |
| **Defence & Nuclear** | EBM, PAM, LBM | Specialty alloys, refractory metal components |

### 1.4 The "Why" Behind Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are AMP applications economically vital?**
> - Global market for advanced manufacturing was valued at **USD 250+ billion in 2023** and continues to grow at a CAGR of **~7–9%**.
> - Over **60% of aerospace components** in modern jet engines require at least one AMP operation (drilling, shaping, or surface modification).
> - Micro-machining demand (sub-100 $\mu m$ features) is driven by **MEMS, biomedical, and semiconductor** industries — exclusively served by LBM, EBM, and USM.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Framework for Applications

The application of AMPs is best understood through the **Energy Source Classification (after Benedict & Boothroyd)**:

- **Mechanical Energy-Based**: AJM, WJM, USM
- **Electrical Energy-Based**: EDM, ECG (Electrolytic Grinding)
- **Chemical Energy-Based**: CHM (Chemical Machining)
- **Electrochemical Energy-Based**: ECM, ECG
- **Thermal Energy-Based**: LBM, EBM, PAM (Plasma Arc Machining)

### 2.2 Process-Specific Application Logic

#### 2.2.1 Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)
- **Applicable to**: All electrically **conductive** materials regardless of hardness (hardened tool steel, tungsten carbide, Inconel, titanium).
- **Not applicable to**: Non-conductors (ceramics, plastics).
- **Typical applications**: Die cavities, micro-holes, intricate 3D profiles in hardened steel.
- **Material removal rate (MRR) limiting factor**: Thermal conductivity of workpiece and flushing efficiency.

#### 2.2.2 Electrochemical Machining (ECM)
- **Applicable to**: Hard, tough, difficult-to-machine alloys; thin-walled components.
- **Advantage**: No heat-affected zone, no tool wear, no residual stress.
- **Typical applications**: Turbine blade profiling, gun barrel rifling, surgical blade manufacturing.

#### 2.2.3 Laser Beam Machining (LBM)
- **Applicable to**: Almost **all materials** — metals, ceramics, composites, polymers, organics.
- **Typical applications**: Micro-drilling of fuel injector nozzles, stent cutting, PCB via formation.
- **Wavelength dependency**: $\text{CO}_2$ laser ($10.6 \mu m$) for non-metals; Nd:YAG / Fiber laser ($\sim 1.06 \mu m$) for metals.

#### 2.2.4 Electron Beam Machining (EBM)
- **Applicable to**: Metals (in vacuum) and micro-machining.
- **Distinctive feature**: Beam diameter as small as $10 \mu m$ → ultra-fine holes.
- **Constraint**: Requires vacuum chamber → not suitable for large components.

#### 2.2.5 Ultrasonic Machining (USM)
- **Applicable to**: **Hard, brittle, non-conductive** materials — glass, ceramics, quartz, germanium, silicon, carbides.
- **Typical applications**: Machining of semiconductor wafers, optical lens shaping.

#### 2.2.6 Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM) & Water Jet Machining (WJM)
- **Applicable to**: Heat-sensitive materials (composites, laminates), brittle materials, cleaning, deburring.
- **Typical applications**: Trimming of GFRP/CFRP composites, cleaning of turbine blades, frosting of glass.

#### 2.2.7 Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)
- **Applicable to**: All electrically conductive metals, especially for **cutting thick sections**.
- **Typical applications**: Cutting of stainless steel plates, shipbuilding, structural steel fabrication.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA REFERENCE TABLE — Memorize for ESE**

| Process | Governing Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EDM (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = V_s \times f_p$ | $V_s$ = volume per spark; $f_p$ = pulse frequency | $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ |
| **EDM (Wear Ratio)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V_{\text{tool}}}{V_{\text{work}}}$ | $V_{\text{tool}}$ = tool vol. removed; $V_{\text{work}}$ = work vol. removed | dimensionless |
| **ECM (Faraday's Law)** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $A$= atomic wt; $I$= current; $\eta$= current eff.; $n$= valency; $F$=96500 C/mol; $\rho$= density | $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| **ECM (Specific Volume)** | $V_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{A}{n \cdot \rho}$ | Theoretical volume removed per coulomb | $\text{mm}^3/(\text{A}\cdot\text{s})$ |
| **LBM (Energy Balance)** | $E = V_m \cdot \rho \cdot [c \cdot \Delta T + L_f]$ | $V_m$= vol. melted; $c$=sp. heat; $\Delta T$=temp. rise; $L_f$=latent heat | Joules |
| **LBM (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{P}{[c \cdot \Delta T + L_f] \cdot \rho}$ | $P$ = absorbed laser power | $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| **USM (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{k \cdot F \cdot d_a}{H_v^2}$ | $k$=constant; $F$=static force; $d_a$=avg. abrasive dia; $H_v$=Vickers hardness | $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| **AJM (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} \propto m_a \cdot v_a^2$ | $m_a$=abrasive mass flow; $v_a$=abrasive velocity | $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ |
| **PAM (Heat Input)** | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v}$ | $V$=voltage; $I$=current; $v$=travel speed | J/mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Faraday's Constant** $F = 96500 \text{ C/mol}$ is a fundamental constant for ECM and ECG calculations. Always use it with $\eta$ (current efficiency) — typical $\eta$ for ECM lies between **85–95%**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- The **ECM equation** allows prediction of anode dissolution for a given current, helping tool design for turbine blade machining.
- The **LBM energy balance** is critical for selecting laser pulse energy to avoid excessive HAZ.
- The **USM formula** quantifies the dependence of MRR on abrasive size — doubling $d_a$ quadruples MRR (quadratic dependence).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of ECM Material Removal Rate (Faraday's Two Laws)

The **MRR in ECM** is derived directly from Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU ESE.

**Given:**
- Atomic weight of workpiece material = $A$ (kg/kmol)
- Valency of dissolving ions = $n$ (equivalents/kmol)
- Density of workpiece = $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Current supplied = $I$ (Amperes)
- Current efficiency = $\eta$ (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- Time = $t$ (seconds)

**Step 1 — Faraday's First Law Statement:**
The mass of material dissolved is directly proportional to the charge passed.

$$m \propto Q$$

where $Q = I \cdot t$ is the total charge in Coulombs.

**Step 2 — Faraday's Second Law Statement:**
The mass liberated is proportional to the equivalent weight.

$$m \propto \dfrac{A}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Combining Both Laws with Constant:**

$$m = \dfrac{A}{n} \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot \eta}{F}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Mass to Volume (introduce density $\rho$):**

$$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate w.r.t. time to get MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{\text{ECM}} = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Validation (Iron, Fe):**
- $A = 55.85 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$ (Fe²⁺), $\rho = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\eta = 0.9$, $I = 1000 \text{ A}$, $F = 96500 \text{ C/mol}$.

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{55.85 \times 1000 \times 0.9}{2 \times 96500 \times 7.87}$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{50265}{1518911} = 0.0331 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to engineering units:

$$\text{MRR} \approx 0.0331 \times 60000 \approx 1986 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A current of 1000 A removes ~2 cm³ of iron per minute. This matches industrial ECM machine data sheets.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of EDM Material Removal Rate

**Given:**
- Volume removed per discharge pulse = $V_s$ (mm³/pulse)
- Pulse frequency = $f_p$ (pulses/second)

**Step 1 — Total volume removed in time $t$:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_s \times N_{\text{pulses}}$$

**Step 2 — Number of pulses in time $t$:**

$$N_{\text{pulses}} = f_p \times t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_s \times f_p \times t$$

**Step 4 — MRR (volume per unit time):**

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{\text{EDM}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{total}}}{t} = V_s \times f_p}$$

**Step 5 — Energy-Coupled Form:**
Since each pulse carries energy $E_p = V_e \times I_e \times T_{\text{on}}$ (where $V_e$ = voltage, $I_e$ = current, $T_{\text{on}}$ = pulse on-time), $V_s$ is related to $E_p$ and workpiece properties.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of LBM Energy Balance & MRR

**Step 1 — Energy required to heat unit volume to melting point:**

$$E_1 = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Energy required for phase change (latent heat) of unit volume:**

$$E_2 = \rho \cdot L_f$$

**Step 3 — Total energy per unit volume to melt and vaporize:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = \rho \cdot [c \cdot \Delta T + L_f]$$

**Step 4 — Energy supplied by laser in time $t$:**

$$E_{\text{supplied}} = P \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Equating for volume melted:**

$$V_m \cdot \rho \cdot [c \cdot \Delta T + L_f] = P \cdot t$$

**Step 6 — MRR (volume per unit time):**

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{\text{LBM}} = \dfrac{V_m}{t} = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot [c \cdot \Delta T + L_f]}}$$

**Numerical check (Steel):** $P = 500 \text{ W}$, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c = 500 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\Delta T = 1500 - 25 = 1475 \text{ K}$, $L_f = 2.7 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$.

$$\text{Denominator} = 7850 \times [500 \times 1475 + 2.7 \times 10^5]$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 7850 \times [737500 + 270000] = 7850 \times 1.0075 \times 10^6$$

$$\text{Denominator} \approx 7.91 \times 10^9 \text{ J/m}^3$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{500}{7.91 \times 10^9} \approx 6.32 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 3.79 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Application Matrix — "Which Process for Which Job?"

| Application Requirement | Best AMP | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Drill micro-holes (< 100 $\mu m$) in Inconel | **LBM / EBM** | Focused energy beam, no tool contact |
| Cut a 50 mm thick titanium plate | **PAM / WJM** | High energy density for thick sections |
| Machine a deep, narrow slot in HSS | **EDM (Wire cut)** | Non-contact, ignores hardness |
| Profile a turbine blade from Inconel | **ECM** | No tool wear, no HAZ, complex profile |
| Drill holes in glass/quartz | **USM** | Brittle & non-conductive material handling |
| Cut a composite laminate (CFRP) | **AJM / WJM** | No heat input, no delamination |
| Form a complex 3D die cavity in D2 steel | **EDM (Sinker)** | Hard material, intricate geometry |
| Remove rust/oxide scale from a metal surface | **CHM** | Selective chemical dissolution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Application Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Workpiece and Requirement] --> B{Is Material Electrically Conductive?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Hardness > 40 HRC?}
    B -- No --> D{Need Micro Hole or Complex Profile?}
    C -- Yes --> E{Is Heat Affected Zone Acceptable?}
    C -- No --> F[Use Conventional Machining]
    E -- Yes --> G[Use EDM]
    E -- No --> H[Use ECM or USM]
    D -- Yes --> I{Is Material Brittle?}
    D -- No --> J[Consider LBM or Chemical]
    I -- Yes --> K[Use USM with Abrasive Slurry]
    I -- No --> L[Use LBM or EBM]
    J --> M{Is Surface Sensitivity Critical?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Use Chemical Machining CHM]
    M -- No --> O[Use LBM with Inert Gas Assist]
    F --> P[Process Selected]
    G --> P
    H --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Verify with KTU Application Matrix]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of ECM Process (used in turbine blade application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Power Supply Unit] --> B[Cathode Tool Design with Electrolyte Flow Channels]
    B --> C[Workpiece Anode in Machining Gap 0.1 to 0.5 mm]
    C --> D[Electrolyte Sump and Pump System]
    D --> E[Filter and Cooler Unit]
    E --> F[Sludge Removal and Recycling]
    F --> D
    A --> G[Voltage and Current Control]
    G --> H[MRR Feedback and Tool Feed Rate Adjustment]
    H --> B
    C --> I[Material Dissolved as Hydroxide Sludge]
    I --> D
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparative Process Capability Chart (Applications Oriented)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Advanced Manufacturing Process Applications] --> B[Material Removal]
    A --> C[Material Joining]
    A --> D[Material Forming]
    B --> B1[EDM - Hard Conductive Metals]
    B --> B2[ECM - Hard Tough Alloys]
    B --> B3[LBM - Almost All Materials]
    B --> B4[EBM - Metals in Vacuum]
    B --> B5[USM - Brittle Non Conductive]
    B --> B6[AJM - Heat Sensitive]
    B --> B7[WJM - Composites and Stone]
    B --> B8[CHM - Thin Foil Etching]
    B --> B9[PAM - Thick Plate Cutting]
    C --> C1[Laser Welding]
    C --> C2[Electron Beam Welding]
    C --> C3[Plasma Arc Welding]
    D --> D1[Laser Forming]
    D --> D2[Electroforming]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description of Typical EDM Application

The figure below is a **textual schematic** representing the die-sinker EDM setup for machining a hardened die cavity:

```
        ┌──────────────────────────┐
        │      Dielectric Inlet    │
        └────────────┬─────────────┘
                     │  (Flow)
                     ▼
        ╔════════════════════════╗
        ║   Tool Electrode (Cu/  ║  ◄── Shaped as negative of cavity
        ║   Graphite, cathode)   ║
        ╠════════════════════════╣
        ║ ↕ Spark Gap 25-50 μm   ║
        ║ ↕ (Dielectric flow)    ║
        ╠════════════════════════╣
        ║   Workpiece (Anode)    ║  ◄── Hardened die steel
        ║   H13 / D2 / Carbide   ║
        ╚════════════════════════╝
                     │
                     ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────┐
        │    Dielectric Outlet     │
        │   (Carries debris away)  │
        └──────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Application Insight:** The shape of the **tool electrode** dictates the final cavity shape. Since no mechanical force is involved, the workpiece can be fully hardened **before** machining — this is a unique advantage over conventional milling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any **six industrial applications** of Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM). Mention **one limitation** of the process.*

**Model Answer:**

**Applications of EDM:**
1. Machining of hardened tool steel dies and moulds (H13, D2, SKD11).
2. Production of small-diameter deep holes in fuel injectors and spinnerets.
3. Machining of intricate 3D cavities in aerospace turbine components.
4. Wire-EDM cutting of profiles in conductive but very hard materials (carbide punches).
5. Micro-machining of slots and micro-features in electronic components.
6. Die-making for plastic injection moulding and die-casting.

**Limitation:** EDM is **restricted to electrically conductive materials only** and has a relatively low MRR compared to conventional milling.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between **AJM and WJM** based on their working principle and typical applications.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM) | Water Jet Machining (WJM) |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Abrasive particles (Al₂O₃, SiC) entrained in gas | Pure high-pressure water (no abrasive) |
| Pressure | 0.2 – 0.8 MPa | 200 – 400 MPa |
| Application | Cleaning, deburring, frosting, drilling brittle material | Cutting stone, composites, thick plates, food |
| MRR | Low | Moderate to high |
| Heat generation | Negligible | Negligible |

**Key Application Note:** WJM is preferred for **soft composites and food**; AJM is preferred for **delicate deburring and micro-cleaning** without damaging the substrate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Module 1 Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
