7. **Recovery (Off-Time):** The gap briefly becomes non-conductive again, allowing de-ionization. The servo system advances the tool by one "step-in" amount and the cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Logic:** The gap is essentially a *self-regulating feedback loop*. The **servo controller** senses the gap voltage and continuously adjusts the tool feed rate to maintain a constant "reference gap." If the gap is too small (low voltage), the tool retracts. If too large (high voltage), the tool advances.

## 2.2 Why the Gap Value Matters — The Engineering Trade-Off

| Gap State | Voltage Reading | Consequence | KTU Memory Trick |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Too Small** (short circuit) | V ≈ 0 | Arc damage, tool burn, no MRR | *"Short = Shortage of life"* |
| **Optimal Gap** | V ≈ 25–35 V (for EDM) | Clean spark, controlled MRR | *"Just right spark"* |
| **Too Large** (open circuit) | V ≈ V_open (high) | No spark, no MRR | *"Wide = Wasted energy"* |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{V}{g}$ | Electric field across gap | V/mm | $E \geq E_{crit} \approx 10^5$ V/mm to spark |
| 2 | $V_{gap} = V_{open} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Gap voltage rise after pulse | Volts | $\tau$ = RC time constant |
| 3 | $MRR_{EDM} = K \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$ | EDM Material Removal Rate | mm³/min | $K$ ≈ 0.001–0.01 mm³/(A·s) |
| 4 | $TWR = \dfrac{V_{tool}}{V_{work}} \times 100\%$ | Tool Wear Ratio | % | Typical 0.1% – 25% |
| 5 | $Ra \propto g^{0.3 \text{ to } 0.5}$ | Surface roughness ∝ gap | μm | Empirical |
| 6 | $ECM: \quad g = \dfrac{\kappa \cdot V}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot v_f}$ | Equilibrium gap in ECM | m | $\kappa$ = electrolyte conductivity |
| 7 | $d_c = 7 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5}$ | Crater diameter in EDM (Heuvelman) | μm | Empirical formula |
| 8 | $P_{spark} = V_{gap} \cdot I_{peak}$ | Spark power | W | Avg power in single pulse |
| 9 | $E_{pulse} = \int_0^{t_{on}} V \cdot I \, dt$ | Energy per pulse | J | Determines crater volume |
| 10 | $h_f = \dfrac{V_{work}}{t_{on}} \quad [\mu m/s]$ | Feed-in rate of tool | mm/min | Equals MRR/workpiece height |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** In the formula table above, do **not** confuse the EDM gap (spark gap) with the ECM gap (flow channel). The physics is fundamentally different — EDM gap is **electrically** controlled, ECM gap is **electrochemically and hydrodynamically** controlled.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry:** Machining of superalloy turbine blades (Inconel, Titanium) — impossible by conventional methods due to hardness. EDM's controlled gap enables intricate cooling channel machining.
- **Die & Mold Making:** Tool Work Gap control ensures ±5 μm tolerance in forging dies and injection molds.
- **Medical Device Manufacturing:** Wire-EDM (TWG = 0.01–0.05 mm) fabricates stents and orthopedic implants from stainless steel and titanium.
- **Automotive:** Micro-EDM creates fuel injector nozzles with consistent gap-controlled spark energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equilibrium Gap Voltage in EDM

**Given:** A typical EDM circuit applies an open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ across a parallel RC network (resistor R representing the dielectric resistance + capacitor C representing the ionization capacitance) with the tool work gap in series with the spark plasma resistance $R_{spark}$.

**To Derive:** The gap voltage $V_{gap}(t)$ during the on-time of a pulse.

**Step 1 — Initial condition:** At the start of pulse ($t=0$), the gap is non-conductive, so the full open-circuit voltage appears across the gap:
$$V_{gap}(0) = V_{oc}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitor charging:** The dielectric behaves like a capacitor $C$ that charges through the series resistance. Once the breakdown threshold $V_b$ is reached, the gap ionizes and a spark forms.

**Step 3 — During spark (on-time):** Once the plasma channel is established, the gap voltage drops to the **maintenance voltage** $V_m$ (typically 20–25 V for EDM), because the plasma has very low resistance.

**Step 4 — Voltage recovery (off-time):** After the pulse ends, the capacitor recharges through the dielectric. The voltage builds up exponentially:
$$V_{gap}(t) = V_{oc} - (V_{oc} - V_m) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Time constant definition:** The time constant is
$$\tau = R \cdot C$$

where $R$ is the resistance of the de-ionized dielectric gap and $C$ is the parasitic capacitance of the electrode-fixture system.

**Step 6 — Servo feedback:** The servo system monitors $V_{gap}$ continuously. If $V_{gap}$ falls below a **reference voltage** $V_{ref}$ (e.g., 25 V), the tool is retracted; if it rises above $V_{ref}$, the tool is advanced. The tool's feed rate is therefore:
$$v_f = K_p \cdot (V_{gap} - V_{ref}) + K_i \int (V_{gap} - V_{ref})\, dt$$

This is a classic **PI controller** structure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Gap, Crater Size, and Surface Roughness

**Empirical (Heuvelman's) Crater Diameter Formula:**
$$d_c = 7 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5}$$

**Where:**
- $d_c$ = crater diameter (μm)
- $I$ = peak current (A)
- $t_{on}$ = pulse on-time (μs)

**Step 1 — Volume of single crater:** Approximating the crater as a shallow spherical segment:
$$V_{crater} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_c^2 \cdot h_c}{8}$$

where $h_c$ (crater depth) is empirically related to $d_c$ by:
$$h_c \approx 0.12 \cdot d_c$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$V_{crater} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_c^2 \cdot 0.12 \cdot d_c}{8} = \dfrac{0.03 \cdot \pi \cdot d_c^3}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $d_c$:**
$$V_{crater} = \dfrac{0.015 \pi \cdot \left(7 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5}\right)^3}{1}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the cube:**
$$V_{crater} = 0.015 \pi \cdot 343 \cdot I^{1.2} \cdot t_{on}^{1.5}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify constant:**
$$V_{crater} \approx 16.17 \cdot I^{1.2} \cdot t_{on}^{1.5} \quad [\mu m^3]$$

**Step 6 — MRR derivation:** If there are $f$ pulses per second, MRR is:
$$MRR = V_{crater} \cdot f \quad [\mu m^3/s]$$

Converting to mm³/min:
$$MRR = \dfrac{16.17 \cdot I^{1.2} \cdot t_{on}^{1.5} \cdot f}{1000} \quad [mm^3/min]$$

**Step 7 — Surface roughness link:** The arithmetic mean roughness $R_a$ is empirically:
$$R_a \approx 0.18 \cdot d_c$$

**Substituting $d_c$:**
$$R_a = 0.18 \cdot 7 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5} = 1.26 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5} \quad [\mu m]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The factor **1.26** in Step 7 is important. Notice that **larger current and longer pulse-on time** both *increase the crater size* and thus *worsen surface finish*. This is the fundamental EDM trade-off: faster MRR ⇒ rougher surface.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs Gap Behaviour

| Process Parameter | Effect on Tool Work Gap | Effect on MRR | Effect on Ra |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ | **Increases** optimal gap (larger strike distance) | ↑ Slight | ↑ Slight |
| ↑ Peak current $I$ | **Slightly decreases** (more thermal expansion closes gap) | ↑↑ Strong | ↑↑ Strong |
| ↑ Pulse on-time $t_{on}$ | Slight increase (longer plasma) | ↑↑ Strong | ↑↑ Strong |
| ↓ Dielectric flushing pressure | **Closes gap** (debris bridges) | ↓ (instability) | ↓ (erratic) |
| ↑ Workpiece conductivity | Slight decrease (easier sparking) | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Tool thermal conductivity | **Increases gap stability** (less wear) | Neutral | Neutral |
| ↑ Electrode area | Decreases (more discharge points) | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↓ Dielectric breakdown voltage | **Decreases** gap (easier spark) | ↑ | ↑ |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A die-sinking EDM operates with $V_{oc} = 100$ V, peak current $I = 25$ A, pulse on-time $t_{on} = 100$ μs, and pulse frequency $f = 200$ Hz. The tool material is copper, workpiece is hardened AISI D2 tool steel.

**Calculate:**
1. Single crater diameter
2. MRR in mm³/min
3. Approximate $R_a$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Crater diameter using Heuvelman:**
$$d_c = 7 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5}$$

Substituting:
$$d_c = 7 \cdot 25^{0.4} \cdot 100^{0.5}$$

Compute $25^{0.4}$: $25^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 25} = e^{0.4 \times 3.2189} = e^{1.2876} \approx 3.624$

Compute $100^{0.5} = 10$

$$d_c = 7 \times 3.624 \times 10 = 253.7 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 2 — MRR:** Using the derived formula:
$$MRR = \dfrac{16.17 \cdot I^{1.2} \cdot t_{on}^{1.5} \cdot f}{1000}$$

Compute $25^{1.2} = e^{1.2 \times 3.2189} = e^{3.8627} \approx 47.65$

Compute $100^{1.5} = 1000$

$$MRR = \dfrac{16.17 \times 47.65 \times 1000 \times 200}{1000}$$

$$MRR = 16.17 \times 47.65 \times 200 = 154{,}099 \text{ μm}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to mm³/min (1 mm³ = 10⁹ μm³, 1 min = 60 s):
$$MRR = \dfrac{154{,}099}{10^9} \times 60 = 9.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 0.00925 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Surface Roughness:**
$$R_a = 1.26 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5} = 1.26 \times 3.624 \times 10 = 45.7 \text{ μm}$$

$$\boxed{R_a \approx 45.7 \text{ μm}}$$

This is consistent with **rough EDM finishing** conditions. To improve $R_a$ to < 5 μm, a finishing pass with $I = 1$ A, $t_{on} = 1$ μs is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
