# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define Tool Work Gap. Mention its typical range in EDM and explain why it must be servo-controlled.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Tool Work Gap is the precisely maintained micro-clearance between the tool electrode (cathode) and the workpiece (anode) in Electrical Discharge Machining across which the dielectric medium is ionized to produce a controlled spark discharge for material removal.

> **Typical Range:** In conventional die-sinking EDM, the gap ranges from **0.025 mm to 0.5 mm**, depending on the open-circuit voltage, peak current, and pulse-on time. In wire-EDM, the gap is finer at **0.01 mm to 0.05 mm**.

> **Need for Servo Control:** The gap must be **servo-controlled** because of three unstable states:
> 1. **Short circuit** (gap too small) — causes arc damage to the tool and workpiece.
> 2. **Open circuit** (gap too large) — no spark, no material removal.
> 3. **Optimal state** (gap = reference) — controlled spark and stable MRR.
>
> The servo system continuously senses the gap voltage and adjusts the tool feed rate through a PI controller, maintaining the gap in the optimal window of **25 V – 35 V** for typical EDM.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Differentiate between the Tool Work Gap in EDM and ECM. List any four points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Aspect | EDM Tool Work Gap | ECM Tool Work Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Range** | 0.025 – 0.5 mm | 0.1 – 1.0 mm |
| 2 | **Mechanism** | Spark/plasma discharge in a dielectric | Electrolytic dissolution in an electrolyte |
| 3 | **Control** | Servo-controlled by gap voltage | Function of electrolyte flow, voltage, and feed rate |
| 4 | **Heat** | Spark temperature 8,000–12,000 K; HAZ present | Low temperature (≤ 100°C); no HAZ |
| 5 | **Tool Wear** | Yes (0.1–25%) | Virtually zero (cathode protected by H₂ evolution) |
| 6 | **Surface Finish** | Recast layer + micro-craters | Stress-free, mirror finish possible |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **mechanism of spark generation** in EDM, highlighting the role of the Tool Work Gap. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **relationship between the tool work gap, peak current, pulse on-time, and Material Removal Rate (MRR)** in EDM using Heuvelman's empirical formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

The Tool Work Gap is the small clearance (typically 25–500 μm) between the tool cathode and the workpiece anode, filled with a dielectric fluid (typically EDM oil or de-ionized water). The mechanism proceeds in the following stages:

1. **Approach Phase:** The servo system advances the tool toward the workpiece. As the gap decreases, the electric field intensity $E = V/g$ increases.

2. **Ionization Threshold:** When $E$ exceeds the dielectric strength (~10⁵ V/mm for EDM oil), free electrons gain enough kinetic energy to ionize dielectric molecules, initiating an **electron avalanche**.

3. **Plasma Channel Formation:** A narrow (10–20 μm) conductive plasma channel forms. The temperature rises to **8,000–12,000 K** — hotter than the sun's surface.

4. **Material Removal:** The high temperature melts and vaporizes a small volume of the workpiece locally, forming a roughly hemispherical crater on the anode. A small volume of the tool is also eroded, contributing to tool wear.

5. **Pulse Termination:** After the on-time $t_{on}$ elapses, the voltage is cut off. The plasma collapses, dielectric rushes in to **flush the debris**, and the gap briefly becomes non-conductive (de-ionization time $t_{off}$).

6. **Cycle Repetition:** The servo advances the tool by a small step, and the cycle repeats thousands of times per second.

> **Role of the Tool Work Gap:** It is the *stage* on which the entire spark drama plays out. A gap that is too small causes **arcing** (continuous discharge, tool damage); a gap that is too large prevents **breakdown** (no spark, no MRR). The optimal gap balances **dielectric strength**, **plasma stability**, and **debris evacuation**.

*(Examiner allocates ~3 marks for schematic diagram, ~4 marks for explanation.)*

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Heuvelman's Crater Diameter Formula:**
$$d_c = 7 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5}$$

where $d_c$ is in μm, $I$ in Amperes, $t_{on}$ in μs.

**Step 1 — Crater volume** (hemispherical approximation):
$$V_{crater} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_c^2 \cdot h_c}{8}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical crater depth:**
$$h_c \approx 0.12 \cdot d_c$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$V_{crater} = \dfrac{0.03 \pi}{2} \cdot d_c^3$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $d_c$:**
$$V_{crater} = 0.015 \pi \cdot 343 \cdot I^{1.2} \cdot t_{on}^{1.5}$$

$$V_{crater} \approx 16.17 \cdot I^{1.2} \cdot t_{on}^{1.5} \quad [\mu m^3 \text{ per spark}]$$

**Step 5 — MRR per second:**
$$MRR_{sec} = V_{crater} \cdot f$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mm³/min:**
$$MRR = \dfrac{V_{crater} \cdot f \cdot 60}{10^9}$$

$$\boxed{MRR = \dfrac{16.17 \cdot I^{1.2} \cdot t_{on}^{1.5} \cdot f \cdot 60}{10^9} \quad [mm^3/min]}$$

**Step 7 — Relationship to Tool Work Gap:** The gap is implicitly embedded in the pulse parameters. A larger gap requires:
- Higher open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ to achieve the breakdown field $E_{crit}$
- Allows **larger peak current** without short-circuit
- Permits **longer pulse on-time** without arcing

However, larger gaps produce **larger craters** ($d_c \propto V_{oc}^{0.3}$ empirically) and therefore **rougher surfaces** and **higher MRR**. This is the fundamental EDM trade-off.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the **function of the servo-control system** in maintaining the Tool Work Gap. Derive the **expression for the equilibrium gap voltage** during pulse on-time and off-time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A die-sinking EDM machine is used to machine a die cavity of depth 12 mm in EN31 steel. The pulse parameters are: $I = 50$ A, $t_{on} = 200$ μs, $t_{off} = 100$ μs, $V_{oc} = 120$ V. Assuming the average gap voltage during sparking is 28 V, calculate the **MRR**, **surface roughness**, and **estimated machining time**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Servo System Function:**
The servo-control system in EDM is a **closed-loop feedback controller** whose purpose is to maintain the Tool Work Gap at a value that ensures **stable, repeatable sparks** at a reference gap voltage. The architecture is:

1. **Gap Voltage Sensor:** A high-impedance voltage probe measures the instantaneous voltage between the tool and the workpiece.
2. **Comparator:** This measured voltage is compared with a **reference voltage** $V_{ref}$ (typically 25–30 V for EDM).
3. **PI Controller:** Generates a control signal:
$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt$$
where $e(t) = V_{gap}(t) - V_{ref}$.
4. **Servo Motor:** Drives the tool quill up or down based on $u(t)$.
5. **Decision Logic:**
   - If $V_{gap} < V_{ref}$ (gap too small) → retract tool
   - If $V_{gap} > V_{ref}$ (gap too large) → advance tool
   - If $V_{gap} \approx V_{ref}$ → hold position

**Equilibrium Gap Voltage Derivation:**

**During on-time (spark active):** The plasma channel has low resistance, so the gap voltage drops to a near-constant **maintenance voltage** $V_m \approx 20$–25 V:
$$V_{gap}^{on}(t) \approx V_m = \text{constant}$$

**During off-time (dielectric recovery):** The capacitor formed by the tool-workpiece-dielectric system recharges through the dielectric resistance. Using Kirchhoff's voltage law:
$$V_{oc} = V_R + V_{gap} \implies V_{oc} = R C \dfrac{dV_{gap}}{dt} + V_{gap}$$

Solving the first-order ODE with initial condition $V_{gap}(0) = V_m$:
$$V_{gap}(t) = V_{oc} - (V_{oc} - V_m) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

The **equilibrium reference gap voltage** chosen by the operator is:
$$V_{ref} = k \cdot V_{oc} \quad \text{where } k \approx 0.2 - 0.3$$

For $V_{oc} = 100$ V, $V_{ref} \approx 25$ V, giving a typical gap of ~50 μm.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Given:** $I = 50$ A, $t_{on} = 200$ μs, $t_{off} = 100$ μs, $V_{oc} = 120$ V, Depth $H = 12$ mm.

**Step 1 — Pulse frequency:**
$$f = \dfrac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \dfrac{1}{(200 + 100) \times 10^{-6}} = 3333 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — MRR (using Heuvelman):**

First, $I^{1.2} = 50^{1.2} = e^{1.2 \times \ln 50} = e^{1.2 \times 3.912} = e^{4.694} = 109.3$

$t_{on}^{1.5} = 200^{1.5} = 200 \times \sqrt{200} = 200 \times 14.14 = 2828.4$

$$MRR = \dfrac{16.17 \times 109.3 \times 2828.4 \times 3333 \times 60}{10^9}$$

$$MRR = \dfrac{16.17 \times 109.3 \times 2828.4 \times 199980}{10^9}$$

$$MRR = \dfrac{16.17 \times 109.3 \times 2828.4 \times 199980}{10^9} \approx 101.1 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 101.1 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Surface Roughness:**
$$R_a = 1.26 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5}$$

$I^{0.4} = 50^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \times 3.912} = e^{1.565} = 4.78$

$t_{on}^{0.5} = \sqrt{200} = 14.14$

$$R_a = 1.26 \times 4.78 \times 14.14 = 85.2 \text{ μm}$$

$$\boxed{R_a \approx 85.2 \text{ μm (rough EDM finish)}}$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time:**

For a die cavity of area $A$, the volume to be removed is $V = A \times H$. Assuming a typical cavity area of $30 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm} = 600 \text{ mm}^2$:

$$V = 600 \times 12 = 7200 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$T = \dfrac{V}{MRR} = \dfrac{7200}{101.1} \approx 71.2 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 71 \text{ minutes}}$$

If a finer finish is required ($R_a < 5$ μm), a finishing pass with $I = 1$ A, $t_{on} = 1$ μs would be needed, which would increase the total machining time by approximately 30–40%.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on Tool Work Gap Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EDM gap with ECM gap.** EDM gap is **electrically** maintained (servo-controlled by voltage); ECM gap is **hydrodynamically and electrochemically** maintained (function of electrolyte flow, voltage, feed rate). Marking penalty: **2 marks** if the answer is generic.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in Heuvelman's formula.** The empirical constants are valid ONLY for: $d_c$ in μm, $I$ in Amps, $t_{on}$ in μs. Mixing units (e.g., $t_{on}$ in seconds) gives MRR values off by a factor of $10^6$. **Always declare units at the start of a calculation.**
>
> 3. **Missing the servo-control feedback loop.** Many students describe the gap as static. The KTU examiner expects explicit mention of the **PI controller**, **reference voltage $V_{ref}$**, and **gap voltage sensor**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the de-ionization (off-time).** Off-time is NOT wasted time — it is essential for **plasma collapse**, **debris flushing**, and **dielectric strength recovery**. Skipping this step costs **1–2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
>
> 5. **Confusing crater diameter and crater depth.** Crater depth is approximately **$0.12 \cdot d_c$**, not equal to $d_c$. Drawing the crater as a deep cylinder is a common error.
>
> 6. **Not relating gap to surface finish.** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasises the **process-outcome linkage**. Always end your answer by tying the gap to MRR, Ra, TWR, and accuracy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Tool Work Gap = micro-clearance between tool electrode and workpiece in EDM/ECM where spark (EDM) or electrolytic dissolution (ECM) occurs.
- ✅ **EDM gap range:** 0.025–0.5 mm (die-sinking); 0.01–0.05 mm (Wire-EDM).
- ✅ **ECM gap range:** 0.1–1.0 mm.
- ✅ **Optimal gap voltage in EDM:** 25–35 V (for $V_{oc} \approx 100$ V).
- ✅ **Servo system:** PI controller with reference voltage $V_{ref}$; feedback from gap voltage sensor.
- ✅ **Breakdown field:** $E_{crit} \approx 10^5$ V/mm for EDM oil.
- ✅ **Plasma temperature:** 8,000–12,000 K.
- ✅ **Heuvelman's crater formula:** $d_c = 7 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5}$ (μm).
- ✅ **MRR formula (EDM):** $MRR \approx 16.17 \cdot I^{1.2} \cdot t_{on}^{1.5} \cdot f$ (in appropriate units → mm³/min).
- ✅ **Ra formula:** $R_a \approx 1.26 \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.5}$ (μm).
- ✅ **Crater depth:** $h_c \approx 0.12 \cdot d_c$.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Larger gap → higher MRR, but rougher surface & lower accuracy.
- ✅ **Dielectric role:** Insulation in standby + debris flushing + cooling after spark.
- ✅ **Tool wear:** 0.1–25% in EDM; ~0% in ECM (cathode protected by H₂ gas blanket).
- ✅ **Off-time $t_{off}$:** Essential for de-ionization; without it, gap arcs continuously.
- ✅ **Swarf evacuation:** Done via side flushing, vacuum, or through-tool flushing.
- ✅ **Recast layer:** Formed on EDM surface (~2–10 μm thick); must be removed in finishing.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Process–parameter linkage, servo-control logic, and empirical derivations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** Whenever a question mentions "Tool Work Gap," always write your answer in **three layers**:
> 1. **Define** (with the right range and process — EDM or ECM),
> 2. **Mechanism** (spark vs. electrolytic dissolution),
> 3. **Control & outcomes** (servo-control → MRR, Ra, TWR, accuracy).
>
> This 3-layer structure matches the KTU 2024 scheme's outcome-based evaluation grid and maximises marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemistry of the Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemistry of the Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Manufacturing Processes (PEIET521)**, the "**Chemistry of the Process**" refers to the fundamental **electrochemical, thermochemical, and chemical dissolution mechanisms** that govern material removal (or addition) in non-conventional manufacturing processes. It encompasses the redox reactions, electrolyte behavior, passivation phenomena, and ionic mass transport that dictate the workpiece-tool interaction in processes such as **Electrochemical Machining (ECM)**, **Chemical Machining (CHM)**, and the chemical flushing reactions in **Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 2, the chemistry of the process is primarily centered on **Electrochemical and Chemical Machining**, where material is removed not by mechanical force or thermal melting, but by controlled chemical dissolution governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this — "Rust on Demand":**
> Imagine you want to remove metal from a workpiece. Normally, rusting (oxidation) is a slow, uncontrolled disaster. In **Advanced Chemical/ECM processes**, we essentially **accelerate and control rusting** (anodic dissolution) using an electrolyte bath, a controlled electrical current, or a strong chemical etchant. The tool does not "cut" the metal; instead, atoms are *plucked off* the workpiece one-by-one through ionic reactions. The tool is never touched, never worn, and there is **zero heat-affected zone (HAZ)**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

The following constants are essential for solving KTU numerical problems on this topic:

- **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 Coulombs per mole (C/mol) ≈ 96,500 C/mol**
- **Electron Charge (e) = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ) = 6.022 × 140²³ /mol**
- **Standard Electrode Potentials** are measured in **Volts (V)** against the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Anode vs. Cathode in ECM:**
> - **Anode (+):** The **Workpiece**. It loses electrons (oxidation) → metal dissolves into ions.
> - **Cathode (−):** The **Tool**. It gains electrons (reduction) → typically hydrogen gas evolution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Cell in ECM

An ECM setup is a reverse electroplating cell. A low voltage (typically **5–30 V DC**) and high current (**100–10,000 A**) is passed between the anode (workpiece) and cathode (tool), separated by a flowing electrolyte (commonly **NaCl, NaNO₃, or NaClO₃**).

The fundamental anodic reaction for a generic metal **M** is:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$$

For the cathode (tool), in a neutral electrolyte:

$$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow + 2OH^-$$

This hydrogen gas evolution is critical — it helps flush away the metal hydroxide sludge from the inter-electrode gap.

## 2.2 Faraday's Two Laws of Electrolysis

**First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed.

$$m \propto Q = I \cdot t$$

**Second Law:** The mass of substance liberated is proportional to its **electrochemical equivalent weight (Z)**, which is the atomic weight divided by valency (n).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Definition** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = \dfrac{A \cdot I}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho} \times 10^6$ | A = Atomic wt, I = Current, F = 96500 C/mol, n = Valency, ρ = Density | mm³/min or cm³/min |
| Theoretical MRR (Volume) | $V = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho}$ | t = Time | cm³ |
| Specific Electrode Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Mass of tool consumed}}{\text{Mass of metal removed}}$ | Tool wear ratio | Dimensionless |
| Electrochemical Equivalent (Z) | $Z = \dfrac{A}{n \cdot 96500}$ | Mass deposited per coulomb | kg/C or g/C |
| Current Efficiency (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual MRR}}{\text{Theoretical MRR}} \times 100$ | Ratio of real to ideal removal | % |
| Overpotential (ηₒ) | $\eta_o = E_{actual} - E_{theoretical}$ | Extra voltage needed to drive reaction | Volts (V) |
| Passivation Layer Thickness | $\delta \propto \dfrac{\text{Anodizing time} \times \text{Current density}}{\rho_{oxide}}$ | Time-dependent film growth | μm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** All vertical bars were converted to `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aerospace Industry:** Machining of superalloys (Inconel, Titanium) for turbine blades without inducing thermal stress.
2. **Medical Implants:** Shaping of biocompatible Titanium and Cobalt-Chromium alloys for orthopedic implants.
3. **Automotive:** Die-making for complex curved surfaces in sheet metal stamping.
4. **Chemical Machining (CHM):** Etching of printed circuit boards (PCBs) and decorative panels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's Law for MRR in ECM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** You must show every step of the derivation. Marks are awarded for logical transitions.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of mass dissolved at the anode:**

From Faraday's First Law, the mass of metal dissolved (m) is proportional to the total charge (Q):

$$m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Express Z (Electrochemical Equivalent):**

The mass of one mole of atoms is the **Atomic Weight (A)** grams. The charge required to dissolve one mole of a metal of valency n is **n × F** coulombs. Therefore, the mass dissolved per coulomb is:

$$Z = \frac{A}{n \cdot F} \quad \text{[grams/Coulomb]}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Z into the mass equation:**

$$m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Convert mass to volume using density (ρ):**

Since $V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$, we can write:

$$V = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{[cm}^3\text{]}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the volumetric MRR:**

Dividing by time (t) gives the Material Removal Rate in cm³ per second:

$$MRR = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{[cm}^3\text{/s]}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mm³/min (KTU standard):**

$$MRR_{mm^3/min} = \frac{A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \times \frac{10^6 \times 60}{1} = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Chemical Reactions in Common ECM Electrolytes

| **Electrolyte** | **Anode Reaction (Workpiece)** | **Cathode Reaction (Tool)** | **By-products** | **Key Property** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NaCl (Sodium Chloride) | $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ | $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ | Fe(OH)₂ sludge | High conductivity, aggressive dissolution |
| NaNO₃ (Sodium Nitrate) | $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ | $NO_3^- + H_2O + e^- \rightarrow NO_2 + 2OH^-$ | No H₂ gas | Passive film formation, better surface finish |
| NaClO₃ (Sodium Chlorate) | $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ | $ClO_3^- + 3H_2O + 6e^- \rightarrow Cl^- + 6OH^-$ | Fe(OH)₃ | Excellent throwing power, precise control |
| HCl (Hydrochloric Acid) | $Fe + 2H^+ \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + H_2$ | (Spontaneous, no external current) | FeCl₂ | Used in Chemical Machining (CHM) |

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Style Problem)

> **Problem:** Calculate the MRR in **cm³/min** for machining a steel workpiece (A = 56, n = 2, ρ = 7.85 g/cm³) using a current of **1000 A** with **100% current efficiency**.

**Solution:**

Given: I = 1000 A, A = 56, n = 2, ρ = 7.85 g/cm³, F = 96,500 C/mol

Using the derived MRR formula:

$$MRR = \frac{A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

$$MRR = \frac{56 \times 1000}{2 \times 96500 \times 7.85}$$

$$MRR = \frac{56000}{1515050} = 0.03696 \text{ cm}^3\text{/s}$$

Converting to cm³/min:

$$MRR = 0.03696 \times 60 = 2.217 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Final Answer:** MRR ≈ **2.22 cm³/min** (or equivalently, 2217 mm³/min).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ECM Cell Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC[DC Power Supply 5-30V] -->|Positive Terminal| ANO[Anode Workpiece]
    DC -->|Negative Terminal| CAT[Cathode Tool]
    ANO -->|Metal Dissolution M to Mn+| ELEC[Electrolyte Flow NaCl/NaNO3]
    ELEC -->|H2 Gas and Sludge Removal| PUMP[Pump and Filter]
    PUMP -->|Clean Recirculated Electrolyte| ELEC
    CAT -->|H2 Evolution 2H2O plus 2e| ELEC
    ELEC --> GAP[Inter Electrode Gap 0.1 to 0.5 mm]
    GAP --> ANO
    GAP --> CAT
```

## 4.2 Chemical Machining Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Maskant Application on Metal Sheet] --> B[Drying and Curing]
    B --> C{Selective Exposure}
    C -->|Exposed Areas| D[Chemical Etchant HCl or FeCl3]
    C -->|Masked Areas| E[Protected from Etchant]
    D --> F[Redox Reaction M plus nH to Mn plus plus n/2 H2]
    F --> G[Metal Dissolution in Exposed Zones]
    G --> H[Demasking and Rinsing]
    H --> I[Final Part with Etched Features]
```

## 4.3 Passivation Layer Formation in ECM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Metal Surface Anode] -->|Oxidation| B[Metal Ions Mn plus in solution]
    A -->|Film Growth| C[Passive Oxide Layer]
    C -->|At High Current Density| D[Film Breakdown - Active Dissolution]
    C -->|At Low Current Density| E[Stable Passive Film - Stops Machining]
    D --> F[Material Removal Continues]
    E --> G[Machining Ceases]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why **NaNO₃** is preferred for ECM of steel — it promotes a **passive film** at low current densities, which automatically limits dissolution to the areas closest to the tool (where current density is highest), thereby improving **dimensional accuracy**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: State and explain Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis as applied to Electrochemical Machining (ECM).**

**Model Answer:**
Faraday's First Law states that the **mass of material dissolved at the anode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte**.
Mathematically: $m \propto Q = I \cdot t$.
In ECM, the workpiece is the anode, and metal atoms are removed by oxidation ($M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$). The higher the current (I) and time (t), the greater the mass of metal dissolved, provided current efficiency is 100%.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between Chemical Machining (CHM) and Electrochemical Machining (ECM).**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **CHM** | **ECM** |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Chemical energy only | Electrical + Chemical energy |
| External Current | Not required | Required (DC, 5–30V, high A) |
| Tool | No tool; uses maskant | Cathode tool (shaped) |
| Hydrogen Evolution | Yes (spontaneous) | Yes (at cathode) |
| Control | By etchant concentration & time | By current, voltage, gap |
| MRR Control | Poor (isotropic etching) | Excellent (anisotropic via gap) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of Electrochemical Machining (ECM)**. Discuss the role of electrolyte flow, inter-electrode gap, and hydrogen evolution. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

ECM works on the principle of **controlled anodic dissolution** using Faraday's Laws.

1. **Setup:** A low-voltage, high-current DC power supply is connected with the workpiece as the **anode (+)** and a shaped tool as the **cathode (−)**. A gap of **0.1–0.5 mm** is maintained.

2. **Reaction:** Metal atoms at the anode lose electrons and dissolve as ions: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$. At the cathode, water is reduced: $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow + 2OH^-$.

3. **Role of Electrolyte Flow:** The electrolyte (NaCl/NaNO₃) is pumped at high velocity (5–30 m/s) through the gap to:
   - Flush away metal hydroxide sludge.
   - Remove heat generated (Joule heating).
   - Remove hydrogen gas bubbles (prevents short-circuiting).

4. **Inter-electrode Gap:** The feed mechanism advances the tool at a rate equal to the workpiece dissolution rate, maintaining a constant gap. This ensures **dimensional accuracy**.

5. **Hydrogen Evolution:** Occurs at the cathode. It is essential for sludge removal but must be efficiently evacuated to prevent sparking or cavitation.

**(b)** A steel workpiece (A = 56, n = 2, ρ = 7.85 g/cm³) is machined by ECM with a current of **2000 A** and current efficiency of **90%**. Calculate the **MRR in cm³/min** and the **mass removed in 10 minutes**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** I = 2000 A, A = 56, n = 2, ρ = 7.85 g/cm³, η = 0.90, t = 600 s

**Step 1 — Theoretical MRR:**

$$MRR_{theoretical} = \frac{A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} = \frac{56 \times 2000}{2 \times 96500 \times 7.85}$$

$$MRR_{theoretical} = \frac{112000}{1515050} = 0.07392 \text{ cm}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 2 — Actual MRR (with 90% efficiency):**

$$MRR_{actual} = 0.07392 \times 0.90 = 0.0665 \text{ cm}^3\text{/s}$$

$$MRR_{actual} = 0.0665 \times 60 = 3.99 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed in 10 minutes:**

$$V = MRR \times t = 3.99 \times 10 = 39.9 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Mass removed:**

$$m = V \times \rho = 39.9 \times 7.85 = 313.2 \text{ g}$$

**Final Answer:** MRR = **3.99 cm³/min**, Mass removed = **313.2 g**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **chemistry of the electrolyte** in ECM. Compare the use of **NaCl and NaNO₃** as electrolytes for machining low-carbon steel. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The electrolyte in ECM serves three chemical roles: (1) **charge carrier** (providing ions for current flow), (2) **reactant** (enabling anodic dissolution), and (3) **flushing medium** (removing sludge and heat).

**NaCl (Sodium Chloride):**
- Highly **aggressive** electrolyte. Cl⁻ ions aggressively attack the passive oxide film on steel, ensuring continuous dissolution.
- Generates large amounts of **Fe(OH)₂ sludge** and **H₂ gas**.
- Risk of **localized pitting corrosion** → poor surface finish.
- Used for roughing operations and machining of nickel-based superalloys.

**NaNO₃ (Sodium Nitrate):**
- **Mildly oxidizing**; promotes the formation of a thin **passive Fe₂O₃ film** on the workpiece surface.
- At low current densities (far from the tool), the film remains intact → **no dissolution** (improves accuracy).
- At high current densities (near the tool), the film breaks down → **active dissolution** (machining proceeds).
- Cathode reaction: $NO_3^- + H_2O + e^- \rightarrow NO_2 \uparrow + 2OH^-$ (no H₂ gas → less sparking).
- Used for **finishing** and high-precision ECM of steel.

**(b)** A copper workpiece (A = 63.5, n = 2, ρ = 8.96 g/cm³) is machined at **500 A** for **5 minutes**. If the volume removed is measured as **12 cm³**, determine the **current efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** A = 63.5, n = 2, ρ = 8.96 g/cm³, I = 500 A, t = 300 s, V_actual = 12 cm³

**Step 1 — Theoretical volume removed:**

$$V_{theoretical} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

$$V_{theoretical} = \frac{63.5 \times 500 \times 300}{2 \times 96500 \times 8.96}$$

$$V_{theoretical} = \frac{9525000}{1729280} = 5.508 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Current Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}} \times 100 = \frac{12}{5.508} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 217.85\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> **Efficiency > 100% is physically impossible in pure Faraday electrolysis!** This indicates that **chemical (etching) action** is contributing to material removal alongside the electrochemical process. Students who blindly write "η = 217.85%" without commenting on the physical anomaly will lose **2–3 marks**. Always add: *"This anomalous value suggests parasitic chemical etching or stray current losses, indicating the need to re-check the experimental setup or consider side reactions like H₂SO₄ etching."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Chemistry of the Process"**

- **ECM = Reverse Electroplating:** Workpiece is anode; tool is cathode; metal dissolves as ions.
- **Faraday's Constant = 96,500 C/mol** — Memorize this value.
- **MRR Formula:** $MRR = \dfrac{A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ (mass form: multiply by ρ).
- **Anode Reaction:** $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$ (oxidation).
- **Cathode Reaction:** $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ (hydrogen evolution).
- **NaCl** = Aggressive, pitting, roughing. **NaNO₃** = Passive film, accurate, finishing.
- **No tool wear** in ECM (cathode is inert); **No HAZ**; **No residual stress**.
- **Current Efficiency (η)** is the ratio of actual to theoretical MRR; always < 100% in pure ECM.
- **Chemical Machining (CHM):** Uses **maskants** (e.g., neoprene, polyvinyl chloride) to protect selected areas; etchants are typically **HCl, H₂SO₄, FeCl₃, or NaOH**.
- **Key Advantage of CHM:** Can machine **very thin sheets** and produce **simultaneous multiple features** — impossible with conventional tools.
- **Key Disadvantage of CHM:** **Isotropic etching** causes **undercut** → limits dimensional accuracy.
- **ECM vs. EDM:** ECM is **chemical/electrochemical** (no heat, no sparks); EDM is **thermoelectric** (uses sparks, ~10,000°C plasma, recast layer).
- **Inter-electrode gap in ECM:** 0.1–0.5 mm; smaller gap → higher current density → higher MRR.
- **Hydrogen gas in ECM must be evacuated** to prevent sparking and ensure uniform electrolyte flow.
- **Passivation** is the key to dimensional accuracy in NaNO₃-based ECM of steel.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Removal Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal Removal Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Removal Rate (MRR)** is defined as the volume of parent metal (workpiece material) actually removed per unit time during a machining or material removal process. It is the most fundamental performance index used to evaluate the productivity, efficiency, and economic viability of any non-traditional or conventional manufacturing process.

Mathematically, the general volumetric form of MRR is expressed as:

$$MRR = \frac{\text{Volume of material removed}}{\text{Machining time}} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t}$$

The corresponding **mass-based** form, often used in advanced/abrasive processes, is:

$$MRR_{mass} = \frac{\text{Mass of material removed}}{\text{Machining time}} = \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta t}$$

The two are interconvertible using the density $\rho$ of the workpiece material:

$$\Delta V = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho}$$

## 1.2 Standard Units & Conventions

> [!NOTE]
> - **Volumetric MRR:** mm³/min (SI preferred), cm³/min, or in³/min.
> - **Mass MRR:** g/min or mg/min (used in micro-scale processes like AJM, USM, LBM).
> - **Density (ρ):** g/cm³ or kg/m³.
> - **KTU 2024 Convention:** Always quote volumetric MRR in **mm³/min** unless explicitly asked for mass MRR.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine a **wooden log being planed by a carpenter**. The faster the carpenter pushes the plane across the log and the deeper each shaving is, the more wood is removed per minute. The volume of wood chips flying off per minute is essentially the carpenter's "wood removal rate."

> [!TIP]
> In manufacturing, MRR plays the same role:
> - **High MRR** = High productivity (more parts/hour) = Lower cost per part.
> - **Low MRR** = Finishing/precision operation (better surface finish, lower tolerance).
> - **Trade-off Rule:** Almost always, **higher MRR ↔ poorer surface finish**, and **lower MRR ↔ better accuracy**. KTU exam questions frequently test this trade-off.

In Advanced Manufacturing Processes (PEIET521, Module 2), MRR is computed for each non-traditional process **separately**, because the physical mechanism (mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrochemical) governing the material removal is fundamentally different in each case.

---

## 1.4 Why MRR is a High-Yield KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every KTU 2024 Scheme question paper for PEIET521, **MRR derivation or numerical evaluation** is a guaranteed 7 to 14 mark question because it integrates:
> 1. Process physics (mechanism of removal),
> 2. Material property (density, atomic weight, valency),
> 3. Process parameters (current, voltage, flow rate, frequency),
> 4. Numerical computation.
> 
> Mastering MRR formulas essentially unlocks Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 General Classification of MRR in Advanced Manufacturing Processes

The material removal mechanism in Module 2 processes falls into four physical families, and each family yields a *distinct* MRR equation form.

### A. Mechanical / Abrasive Family
Processes: **Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM), Water Jet Machining (WJM), Ultrasonic Machining (USM)**.
Material is removed by kinetic impact of abrasive particles, water jets, or vibrating abrasive slurry against the workpiece.

### B. Electro-Thermal Family
Processes: **Electric Discharge Machining (EDM), Wire-EDM**.
Material is removed by melting and vaporization due to high-temperature spark/plasma.

### C. Electro-Chemical Family
Processes: **Electrochemical Machining (ECM), Electrochemical Grinding (ECG)**.
Material is removed by controlled anodic dissolution (ion-by-ion).

### D. Thermo-Physical / Beam Energy Family
Processes: **Laser Beam Machining (LBM), Electron Beam Machining (EBM), Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)**.
Material is removed by melting and vaporization due to focused high-energy beam.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The general procedure to compute MRR in any process is:

- **Step 1 — Identify the dominant removal mechanism** (mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrochemical).
- **Step 2 — Identify the governing input parameter** (current, voltage, flow rate, frequency, beam power, etc.).
- **Step 3 — Relate the input parameter to volume/mass removed per unit event** (per spark, per pulse, per ion, per grit impact).
- **Step 4 — Multiply by the rate of events per unit time** (pulses/sec, impacts/sec).
- **Step 5 — Apply efficiency factors** (current efficiency, energy coupling efficiency, etc.).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** that must be memorized verbatim for the KTU ESE.

| # | Process | MRR Formula | Key Variables | Standard Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Turning (conventional reference)** | $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | $v$ = cutting velocity, $f$ = feed, $d$ = depth of cut | mm³/min |
| 2 | **AJM (Abrasive Jet Machining)** | $MRR_m = \frac{m_a \cdot (k_1 \cdot k_2})}{\rho_w \cdot t}$ | $m_a$ = abrasive mass flow, $k_1 k_2$ = coefficients, $\rho_w$ = workpiece density | mg/min |
| 3 | **WJM (Water Jet Machining)** | $MRR = k \cdot P^{1.5}$ (approx) | $P$ = hydraulic pressure, $k$ = empirical constant | mm³/min |
| 4 | **USM (Ultrasonic Machining)** | $MRR \propto \frac{(F_s)^{0.5} \cdot (a)^{0.6} \cdot (d_g)^{1.5}}{(H)^{1.5}}$ | $F_s$ = static load, $a$ = amplitude, $d_g$ = grit size, $H$ = hardness | mm³/min |
| 5 | **EDM (Electric Discharge Machining)** | $MRR = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on}^{1.1}$ | $I$ = current, $T_{on}$ = pulse-on time, $K$ = process constant | mm³/min |
| 6 | **ECM (Electrochemical Machining)** | $MRR = \frac{\eta \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \cdot A$ | $\eta$ = current efficiency, $I$ = current, $n$ = valency, $F$ = Faraday, $A$ = atomic weight, $\rho$ = density | mm³/min |
| 7 | **Wire-EDM** | $MRR = \pi \cdot d \cdot v_f$ | $d$ = wire diameter, $v_f$ = feed rate | mm²/min |
| 8 | **LBM (Laser Beam Machining)** | $MRR = \frac{E_p \cdot f \cdot \alpha}{(\rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T + \rho \cdot L_f)}$ | $E_p$ = pulse energy, $f$ = frequency, $\alpha$ = absorptivity, $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion | mm³/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Selection:** Engineers choose a process (AJM vs EDM vs ECM) by comparing MRR for a given material–geometry combination.
- **Cost Estimation:** Cycle time $\approx$ (Workpiece volume) / MRR; cycle time directly drives cost.
- **Parameter Optimization:** MRR vs. Surface Roughness (Ra) curves are plotted to find the optimum operating window — a common KTU numerical.
- **Tooling Strategy:** In ECM, MRR sets the cathode feed rate; in EDM, MRR controls servo-feed advance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MRR in Turning (Foundation Reference)

Consider a single-point turning operation on a cylindrical workpiece of diameter $D$, rotated at spindle speed $N$ (rev/min). The tool moves longitudinally at feed $f$ (mm/rev) and radially to a depth of cut $d$ (mm).

**Step 1 — Identify the uncut chip geometry.**
In one revolution of the workpiece, the tool advances by a distance $f$ along the axis. The cross-section of the uncut chip is therefore:

$$A_{chip} = f \cdot d \quad [mm^2]$$

**Step 2 — Identify the volumetric removal in one revolution.**
The cutting velocity at the workpiece surface is $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$. In one revolution, the tool sweeps out a cylindrical band of length $\pi D$ and depth $d$. The volume removed in one revolution is:

$$V_{rev} = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \quad [mm^3]$$

**Step 3 — Number of revolutions per unit time.**
Revolutions per minute $= N$. Therefore, volumetric MRR is:

$$MRR = V_{rev} \cdot N = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v = \pi D N$ to obtain the textbook form.**

$$MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d \quad [mm^3/min]$$

> **Logic Check:** If $v = 100$ m/min $= 100{,}000$ mm/min, $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $d = 2$ mm, then $MRR = 100{,}000 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 40{,}000$ mm³/min, which is a realistic rough-turning MRR for mild steel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MRR in AJM (Abrasive Jet Machining)

In AJM, high-velocity abrasive particles (typically Al₂O₃ or SiC) impinge on the workpiece and erode material by micro-chipping.

**Step 1 — Define abrasive mass flow rate.**
Let $m_a$ be the mass flow rate of abrasive (g/min) and $\rho_a$ its density. The volumetric flow of abrasive is $m_a / \rho_a$ (cm³/min).

**Step 2 — Define the material removal coefficient.**
Only a fraction of the abrasive mass actually removes workpiece material. This is captured by the empirical MRR coefficient $K_{AJM}$ which has units of volume per unit mass of abrasive. For ductile metals, $K_{AJM} \approx 0.1$ to $0.4$ mm³/g.

**Step 3 — Express MRR.**

$$MRR = K_{AJM} \cdot m_a \quad [mm^3/min]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass MRR of workpiece.**
If the workpiece density is $\rho_w$, then:

$$MRR_m = \rho_w \cdot K_{AJM} \cdot m_a \quad [mg/min]$$

**Step 5 — Influence of gas pressure.**
The mass flow $m_a$ is proportional to gas pressure $P$ (nozzle pressure). Therefore MRR increases with $P$ approximately linearly up to a saturation limit (~ 8 bar for glass, ~ 12 bar for metals).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MRR in EDM (Electric Discharge Machining)

Material removal in EDM occurs by a series of discrete sparks across a dielectric gap. Each spark melts and vaporizes a small crater of material.

**Step 1 — Crater volume per spark.**
For a single spark of duration $T_{on}$ and discharge current $I$, the energy input is:

$$E_{spark} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

where $V$ is the discharge voltage (≈ 20 V for EDM). A fraction $\eta$ of this energy goes into melting:

$$E_{melt} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

**Step 2 — Mass melted per spark.**
Let $T_m$ be the melting temperature, $C_p$ the specific heat, $L_f$ the latent heat of fusion. The energy required to melt unit mass of workpiece is:

$$E_{req} = C_p (T_m - T_{ambient}) + L_f = H \quad [J/g]$$

Therefore, the mass melted per spark is:

$$m_{spark} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{H}$$

**Step 3 — Sparks per minute.**
The pulse frequency is $f_p$ (Hz). Sparks per minute $= 60 f_p$.

**Step 4 — Volumetric MRR.**

$$MRR_{mass} = \frac{60 \cdot f_p \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{H} \quad [g/min]$$

$$MRR_{vol} = \frac{MRR_{mass}}{\rho_w} = \frac{60 \cdot f_p \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{\rho_w \cdot H} \quad [mm^3/min]$$

**Step 5 — Empirical simplification.**
In practice, the KTU-accepted empirical form is:

$$\boxed{MRR = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on}^{1.1}}$$

where $K$ is a process constant that absorbs $V$, $f_p$, $\eta$, $\rho_w$, and $H$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MRR in ECM (Electrochemical Machining) — The Most Important KTU Derivation

ECM removes metal by **anodic dissolution** following Faraday's laws of electrolysis.

**Step 1 — Faraday's First Law.**
The mass of substance dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the charge passed:

$$m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \cdot \eta$$

where:
- $A$ = atomic weight of workpiece (g/mol),
- $I$ = current (A),
- $t$ = time (s),
- $n$ = valency (number of electrons transferred per atom),
- $F$ = Faraday's constant $\approx 96{,}500$ C/mol,
- $\eta$ = **current efficiency** (typically 90–100% in ECM).

**Step 2 — Express MRR in mass form.**

$$MRR_m = \frac{m}{t} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F} \quad [g/s]$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volumetric MRR using density $\rho$.**

$$MRR_v = \frac{MRR_m}{\rho} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad [cm^3/s]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mm³/min (KTU unit).**
Since 1 cm³ = 1000 mm³ and 1 s = 1/60 min:

$$\boxed{MRR = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot 60 \cdot 1000}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} = \frac{6 \times 10^{4} \cdot \eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad [mm^3/min]}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example (KTU-type).**

> *Given:* $I = 1000$ A, workpiece = iron (Fe, $A = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³), $\eta = 0.95$.
> **Find:** MRR in mm³/min.

**Solution:**

$$MRR = \frac{6 \times 10^{4} \times 0.95 \times 55.85 \times 1000}{2 \times 96{,}500 \times 7.87}$$

**Step 5.1 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$6 \times 10^{4} \times 0.95 \times 55.85 \times 1000 = 3.18345 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 5.2 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$2 \times 96{,}500 \times 7.87 = 1.5189 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 5.3 — Division:**

$$MRR = \frac{3.18345 \times 10^{9}}{1.5189 \times 10^{6}} \approx 2095.9 \;\; mm^3/min$$

> **Sanity check:** This is in the realistic order of magnitude (~ 2 cm³/min) for ECM of iron at 1000 A. Answer accepted.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — MRR in USM (Ultrasonic Machining)

USM uses a vibrating tool (≈ 20 kHz) that drives abrasive slurry against the workpiece.

The empirical volumetric form (most cited in KTU references) is:

$$MRR = C \cdot \frac{(F_s)^{0.5} \cdot (a)^{0.6} \cdot (d_g)^{1.5}}{(H)^{1.5}}$$

where:
- $F_s$ = static load (N),
- $a$ = vibration amplitude (µm),
- $d_g$ = average abrasive grit diameter (µm),
- $H$ = workpiece hardness (HV or HRC equivalent),
- $C$ = empirical constant.

**Physical interpretation:**
- MRR increases with load, amplitude, and grit size (numerator).
- MRR decreases with workpiece hardness (harder material → harder to chip).

---

## 3.6 Comparative Influence Table

| Parameter Increased | AJM | USM | EDM | ECM | LBM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure / Load | ↑ | ↑ | — | — | — |
| Current | — | — | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | — |
| Pulse-on time | — | — | ↑ (peaks then ↓) | — | — |
| Flow rate | ↑ | ↑ | — | ↑ | — |
| Frequency | — | ↑ | ↑ (more sparks) | — | ↑ |
| Beam power | — | — | — | — | ↑↑ |
| Tool feed | — | — | ↑ (with spark gap control) | ↑ (forced feed) | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — MRR Computation Flow in Advanced Manufacturing

The following Mermaid diagram captures the canonical block-level functional architecture for computing MRR in any of the Module 2 processes. It maps the parameter inputs, the physical mechanism, and the output MRR in a unified flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Material Properties] --> B[Process Selection from Module 2]
    C[Input Process Parameters] --> B
    B --> D{Mechanism of Material Removal}
    D -- Mechanical Abrasive --> E1[AJM / WJM / USM Block]
    D -- Electro Thermal --> E2[EDM / Wire EDM Block]
    D -- Electro Chemical --> E3[ECM / ECG Block]
    D -- Beam Energy --> E4[LBM / EBM / PAM Block]
    E1 --> F1[Volumetric MRR by Mass Balance]
    E2 --> F2[Volumetric MRR by Spark Energy Balance]
    E3 --> F3[Volumetric MRR by Faraday Law]
    E4 --> F4[Volumetric MRR by Energy Density Model]
    F1 --> G[Unified MRR Output in mm cubed per min]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H{Cycle Time and Cost Estimation}
    H --> I[Final Manufacturing Decision]
```

## 4.2 Functional Comparison Block — MRR Magnitude Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HighMRR[Highest MRR Tier]
        H1[PAM Plasma Arc]
        H2[EBM Electron Beam]
        H3[LBM Laser Beam]
    end
    subgraph MidMRR[Medium MRR Tier]
        M1[ECM Electrochemical]
        M2[EDM Electric Discharge]
    end
    subgraph LowMRR[Lowest MRR Tier Micro Scale]
        L1[USM Ultrasonic]
        L2[AJM Abrasive Jet]
        L3[WJM Water Jet]
    end
    HighMRR --> MidMRR
    MidMRR --> LowMRR
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above tier diagram is conceptual: beam-energy processes deliver the highest MRR (tens of cm³/min), while abrasive processes deliver the lowest MRR (few mm³/min) but the best surface finish. This MRR-vs-finish trade-off is a frequent KTU 3-mark question.

## 4.3 Logical Decision Tree for Choosing MRR Formula in Exam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Identify Process from Question] --> Q1{Is the process based on electric current}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is material removed by spark or dissolution}
    Q2 -- Spark Melting --> EDMPath[Use MRR equals K times I times T on to power 1.1]
    Q2 -- Dissolution --> ECMPath[Use Faraday MRR equals eta A I over n F rho]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is high energy beam used}
    Q3 -- Yes --> BeamPath[Use Beam Energy Density MRR Model]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is abrasive impact involved}
    Q4 -- Yes --> AbrasivePath[Use Empirical Abrasive MRR Equation]
    Q4 -- No --> ConvPath[Use Conventional MRR equals v f d]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> Define **Metal Removal Rate (MRR)**. State the SI unit and write the general volumetric form of the MRR equation. Mention the parameter that has the strongest influence on MRR in **(a) AJM**, **(b) EDM**, and **(c) ECM**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each Sub-Item):**

- **Definition:** MRR is defined as the volume of parent metal removed per unit time during machining.

$$MRR = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} \quad [mm^3/min]$$

- **Dominant Parameter:**
  - **(a) AJM:** Abrasive mass flow rate / Nozzle gas pressure.
  - **(b) EDM:** Discharge current (and pulse-on time).
  - **(c) ECM:** Applied current between tool (cathode) and workpiece (anode).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Compare MRR in **EDM** and **ECM** with respect to the dominant physical mechanism and the governing equation. State one advantage and one limitation of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | EDM | ECM |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Melting & vaporization by spark | Anodic dissolution by ion transfer |
| Governing equation | $MRR = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on}^{1.1}$ | $MRR = \dfrac{\eta A I}{n F \rho}$ |
| Advantage | Works on any conductive material regardless of hardness | No heat-affected zone, no tool wear |
| Limitation | High tool (electrode) wear, slow | Limited to conductive metals, requires high current |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Never use bar `|` in MRR tables or formulas inside markdown tables** — use `\vert` instead. Even a single `|` in a formula like `|ΔV|` will break the entire question paper.
> 2. Always include **units in every numerical step**. A numerical answer without units loses 0.5 mark per KTU 2024 valuation key.
> 3. For ECM derivations, **never forget the factor of 60 × 1000** when converting s to min and cm³ to mm³. This is the most common deduction (−1.5 marks).
> 4. Density ($\rho$) must be in **g/cm³** when using Faraday's constant in C/mol. Mixing SI and CGS units is a guaranteed −1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Focus: ECM + AJM

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for **volumetric MRR in Electrochemical Machining (ECM)** starting from Faraday's laws of electrolysis. Define each symbol and state the units clearly. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** An abrasive jet machining (AJM) setup uses 25 g/min of aluminum oxide abrasive (ρ_a = 3.95 g/cm³) to machine a glass workpiece (ρ_w = 2.5 g/cm³). The MRR coefficient for the workpiece–abrasive pair is K = 0.25 mm³/g. Calculate:
> - (i) the volumetric MRR in mm³/min,
> - (ii) the mass MRR of the workpiece in mg/min,
> - (iii) the time required to machine a rectangular cavity of dimensions 20 mm × 15 mm × 3 mm depth. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State Faraday's First Law (1 Mark):**
The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed.

$$m \propto Q = I \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce current efficiency (1 Mark):**
Real ECM systems are not 100% efficient due to side reactions (H₂ evolution, heating). Include efficiency $\eta$:

$$m = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Mass MRR (1 Mark):**

$$MRR_m = \frac{m}{t} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F} \quad [g/s]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric form (1 Mark):**

$$MRR_v = \frac{MRR_m}{\rho_w} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_w} \quad [cm^3/s]$$

**Step 5 — Convert to KTU-preferred mm³/min (2 Marks):**
Use 1 cm³ = 1000 mm³ and 1 s = 1/60 min:

$$MRR = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_w} \cdot 1000 \cdot 60 = \frac{6 \times 10^{4} \cdot \eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_w} \quad [mm^3/min]$$

**Step 6 — State the final boxed answer (1 Mark).**

$$\boxed{MRR_{ECM} = \frac{6 \times 10^{4} \cdot \eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_w} \quad [mm^3/min]}$$

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $m_a = 25$ g/min,
- $K = 0.25$ mm³/g,
- $\rho_w = 2.5$ g/cm³.

**(i) Volumetric MRR (2 Marks):**

$$MRR_v = K \cdot m_a = 0.25 \times 25 = 6.25 \;\; mm^3/min$$

**(ii) Mass MRR (2 Marks):**

$$MRR_m = \rho_w \cdot MRR_v = 2.5 \times 6.25 = 15.625 \;\; mg/min$$

(Since 1 g/cm³ = 1 mg/mm³, the numerical product is dimensionally consistent.)

**(iii) Time to machine the cavity (3 Marks):**

Cavity volume:

$$V_{cavity} = 20 \times 15 \times 3 = 900 \;\; mm^3$$

Machining time:

$$t = \frac{V_{cavity}}{MRR_v} = \frac{900}{6.25} = 144 \;\; min = 2 \; hr \; 24 \; min$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Focus: EDM + USM

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** With the help of a suitable derivation, obtain the empirical expression for **MRR in EDM**: $MRR = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on}^{1.1}$. Clearly state the role of pulse-on time $T_{on}$ and discharge current $I$. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** In an Ultrasonic Machining (USM) process, the empirical relation for MRR is given by:
>
> $$MRR = C \cdot \frac{F_s^{0.5} \cdot a^{0.6} \cdot d_g^{1.5}}{H^{1.5}}$$
>
> For two different operating conditions, the parameter values are:
>
> | Parameter | Case 1 | Case 2 |
> |---|---|---|
> | Static load $F_s$ (N) | 10 | 25 |
> | Amplitude $a$ (µm) | 20 | 30 |
> | Grit size $d_g$ (µm) | 100 | 150 |
> | Hardness $H$ (HV) | 200 | 300 |
>
> Find the ratio $MRR_2 / MRR_1$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** In EDM, each spark delivers energy $E = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$, of which a fraction $\eta$ melts/vaporizes the workpiece.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Mass melted per spark: $m_{spark} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{H}$ where $H = C_p \Delta T + L_f$.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** With pulse frequency $f_p$, sparks per minute $= 60 f_p$:

$$MRR_m = \frac{60 \cdot f_p \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{H}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Convert to volumetric form by dividing by $\rho_w$:

$$MRR_v = \frac{60 \cdot f_p \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}}{\rho_w \cdot H}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** All constants can be combined into a single process constant $K$:

$$K = \frac{60 \cdot f_p \cdot \eta \cdot V}{\rho_w \cdot H}$$

**Step 6 (2 Marks):** Experimental observations show that MRR does not increase linearly with $T_{on}$ beyond a critical value (debris flushing becomes inefficient). The empirical fit is:

$$\boxed{MRR = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on}^{1.1}}$$

- **Role of $I$:** Higher current → more energy per spark → larger crater → higher MRR (linear).
- **Role of $T_{on}$:** Longer pulse → more energy per spark → larger crater, but the exponent 1.1 reflects near-linear growth with mild super-linearity due to plasma channel expansion.

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Write the ratio:

$$\frac{MRR_2}{MRR_1} = \left(\frac{F_{s,2}}{F_{s,1}}\right)^{0.5} \cdot \left(\frac{a_2}{a_1}\right)^{0.6} \cdot \left(\frac{d_{g,2}}{d_{g,1}}\right)^{1.5} \cdot \left(\frac{H_1}{H_2}\right)^{1.5}$$

(The constant $C$ cancels.)

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Substitute the load ratio:

$$\left(\frac{25}{10}\right)^{0.5} = (2.5)^{0.5} = 1.5811$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substitute the amplitude ratio:

$$\left(\frac{30}{20}\right)^{0.6} = (1.5)^{0.6} = 1.2759$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Substitute the grit-size ratio:

$$\left(\frac{150}{100}\right)^{1.5} = (1.5)^{1.5} = 1.8371$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substitute the hardness ratio:

$$\left(\frac{200}{300}\right)^{1.5} = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{1.5} = 0.5443$$

**Step 6 (2 Marks):** Multiply all four factors:

$$\frac{MRR_2}{MRR_1} = 1.5811 \times 1.2759 \times 1.8371 \times 0.5443$$

Sequential multiplication:

$$1.5811 \times 1.2759 = 2.0173$$

$$2.0173 \times 1.8371 = 3.7060$$

$$3.7060 \times 0.5443 = 2.0172$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{MRR_2}{MRR_1} \approx 2.02}$$

> **Interpretation (Bonus ½ mark if mentioned):** Doubling the load, increasing amplitude, grit size, and lowering hardness approximately **doubles** the MRR.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEIET521 Module 2 — MRR**

- **Definition:** MRR = Volume of metal removed / time. SI unit: **mm³/min**.
- **General turning formula:** $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d$.
- **AJM:** MRR is proportional to **abrasive mass flow rate**; $MRR = K_{AJM} \cdot m_a$. No thermal/electrical effect — purely mechanical.
- **USM:** MRR increases with load, amplitude, and grit size; decreases with workpiece hardness.
- **EDM:** $MRR = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on}^{1.1}$ — empirical; the **1.1 exponent** is a frequently-asked KTU detail.
- **ECM (most tested):**
  - $MRR = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{4} \cdot \eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ in mm³/min.
  - $\eta$ ≈ 0.9 to 1.0; $F$ = 96,500 C/mol.
  - Always multiply by 60,000 to convert (s, cm³) → (min, mm³).
- **Wire-EDM:** $MRR = \pi \cdot d_{wire} \cdot v_{feed}$ (mm²/min) — a 2-D area-based form.
- **LBM / EBM:** MRR ∝ **Beam power / (ρ·C_p·ΔT + ρ·L_f)** — energy density model.
- **MRR vs Surface Finish Trade-off:** Higher MRR → poorer Ra. This is a guaranteed 3-mark KTU question.
- **Process Tiering (MRR order, high to low):** PAM > EBM > LBM > ECM > EDM > USM > AJM ≈ WJM.
- **Unit conversion trap:** 1 cm³/s = 60,000 mm³/min. Always check units before final substitution.
- **Density trap:** Use $\rho$ in g/cm³ when $F$ is in C/mol.
- **Faraday trap:** $n$ = valency = number of electrons lost per atom in solution (e.g., Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, so $n = 2$).
- **Empirical vs Fundamental:** AJM, USM, EDM have *empirical* MRR equations; ECM has a *fundamental* (Faraday-based) equation. This distinction is itself a 2-mark KTU question.
- **Current efficiency $\eta$:** Defined as (actual mass removed) / (theoretical mass removed by Faraday). It is dimensionless and always ≤ 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy and Surface Finish
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accuracy and Surface Finish

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Accuracy (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Accuracy** in advanced manufacturing is formally defined as the **degree of conformance of a manufactured dimension, form, position, or orientation to its corresponding nominal value specified on the engineering drawing or CAD model**. It is a **qualitative measure of how close a produced part is to the target design value**, expressed numerically using standard units (typically mm or µm).

Mathematically, accuracy is expressed as:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \vert \text{True Value} - \text{Measured Value} \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Accuracy is the closeness of a measurement to the *true* or *target* value. It is **NOT** the same as precision. A machine can be highly precise (repeatable) but inaccurate (consistently offset from the true dimension).

## 1.2 Definition of Surface Finish

**Surface Finish (Surface Texture / Surface Roughness)** refers to the **repetitive or random deviations from the nominal surface geometry** of a manufactured component. These deviations include roughness, waviness, lay, and flaws as classified under **ISO 21920 / ISO 1302 standards**.

The three primary components of surface texture are:

- **Roughness (Primary Texture):** Closely spaced, repetitive irregularities caused directly by the cutting tool, grit, or forming action.
- **Waviness (Secondary Texture):** Widely spaced, periodic undulations typically arising from machine tool vibration, deflection, or heat treatment distortion.
- **Lay:** The predominant direction of the surface pattern (e.g., circumferential, parallel, cross-hatched).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Archery Target:**
> Imagine a student shooting arrows at a target:
> - **Accuracy** = how close the arrows land to the **bullseye (target value)**. An accurate shooter lands near the center even if scattered.
> - **Precision** = how close the arrows land to **each other**. A precise shooter has all arrows in a tight cluster, but the cluster could be far from the center.
> - **Surface Finish** = the **texture of the arrow shaft and the feather fletching**. A smooth shaft with clean fletching (low Ra) flies consistently, while a rough shaft (high Ra) introduces drag and instability.
>
> In manufacturing, we need **both accuracy AND surface finish** to be controlled. A perfectly sized part (high accuracy) with a rough surface (high Ra) may fail in service due to fatigue cracks, friction losses, or poor sealing.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in Advanced Manufacturing

In **Advanced Manufacturing Processes (AMPs)** such as EDM, LBM, AJM, USM, ECM, and PAM, achieving micron-level accuracy and nano-level surface finish is a **core evaluation metric** in KTU examinations. Unlike conventional machining, each AMP has **distinct accuracy and surface finish envelopes** based on its underlying physical mechanism.

| Process Category | Typical Achievable Accuracy | Typical Surface Finish (Ra) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Conventional Turning | ±10 µm to ±50 µm | 0.4 µm to 6.3 µm |
| Precision Grinding | ±1 µm to ±5 µm | 0.1 µm to 0.8 µm |
| EDM (Die-Sink) | ±5 µm to ±20 µm | 0.2 µm to 3.2 µm |
| WEDM | ±1 µm to ±5 µm | 0.1 µm to 0.8 µm |
| LBM (Laser) | ±10 µm to ±50 µm | 1.0 µm to 6.3 µm |
| AJM (Abrasive Jet) | ±25 µm to ±125 µm | 0.4 µm to 3.2 µm |
| USM (Ultrasonic) | ±5 µm to ±25 µm | 0.2 µm to 1.6 µm |
| ECM (Electrochemical) | ±10 µm to ±50 µm | 0.1 µm to 0.8 µm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Accuracy in Manufacturing

Accuracy in engineering metrology is classified into **four primary geometric categories** as per ASME Y14.5 and ISO 1101:

### 2.1.1 Dimensional Accuracy
The degree to which a linear or angular dimension (length, diameter, angle) matches the nominal specification. **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing)** governs this via the **± tolerance band**.

### 2.1.2 Form Accuracy
The conformance of a geometric feature to its ideal shape:
- **Straightness, Flatness, Circularity (Roundness), Cylindricity**

### 2.1.3 Orientation Accuracy
- **Perpendicularity, Parallelism, Angularity**

### 2.1.4 Position & Location Accuracy
- **Position tolerance, Concentricity, Symmetry, True Position**

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Accuracy in Advanced Manufacturing Processes

The accuracy envelope of any AMP is governed by **four interacting subsystems**:

1. **Machine Tool Subsystem:** Static stiffness (k N/µm), thermal stability (drift µm/°C), repeatability, axis resolution (µm), and backlash.
2. **Process Physics:** The fundamental removal mechanism (thermal, mechanical, chemical) dictates intrinsic accuracy limits.
3. **Workpiece Subsystem:** Material homogeneity, residual stresses, fixturing rigidity, and clamping distortion.
4. **Control & Metrology:** Feedback sensor resolution, CNC interpolation algorithm, and online measurement integration.

## 2.3 Surface Roughness Parameters (ISO 21920 Series)

The **mean line (m-line)** is the reference line that divides the profile such that the areas above and below it are equal over the sampling length.

### 2.3.1 Ra — Arithmetic Mean Roughness (CLA / AA)
The **most universally used parameter** in KTU examinations. It is the arithmetic mean of the absolute ordinates of the roughness profile from the mean line.

### 2.3.2 Rz — Mean Peak-to-Valley Height
The **sum of the height of the highest peak and the depth of the deepest valley** within a sampling length, averaged over five consecutive sampling lengths. Used in older ISO standards (ISO 4287:1984) and still referenced in KTU 2024 boards.

### 2.3.3 Rq — Root Mean Square Roughness (RMS)
Statistically significant; used in tribology and fatigue studies.

### 2.3.4 Rt — Total Roughness Height
The maximum peak-to-valley distance over the entire evaluation length.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Formula Table — Memorize These Equations**

| # | Parameter | Mathematical Definition | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **Ra (CLA)** | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | Average height of irregularities from mean line | µm |
| 2 | **Rq (RMS)** | $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L y(x)^2 \, dx}$ | Statistical RMS deviation | µm |
| 3 | **Rz (Mean)** | $R_z = \dfrac{1}{5}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{5} R_{zi}\right)$ where $R_{zi} = Zp_i + Zv_i$ | Average of 5 peak-to-valley sums | µm |
| 4 | **Rt (Total)** | $R_t = R_{p_{\max}} + R_{v_{\max}}$ | Max peak + max valley over full length | µm |
| 5 | **Ra vs Rz (Empirical)** | $R_a \approx \dfrac{R_z}{4} \text{ to } \dfrac{R_z}{6}$ | Empirical ISO approximation | µm |
| 6 | **Ra vs Rq (Empirical)** | $R_a \approx 0.8 \times R_q$ | For Gaussian profile | µm |
| 7 | **Bearing Area Ratio (tp)** | $tp(\%) = \dfrac{\eta_0 - \eta_c}{\eta_0} \times 100$ | Material contact at depth c | % |
| 8 | **Weld Overlap (WEDM)** | $W_{ov} = k \cdot \sqrt{I_p \cdot t_{on}}$ | Empirical WEDM kerf formula | µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Safeguard:** In all formulas above, absolute value is written as `\vert y(x) \vert` — never using bare pipe `|y(x)|` — to comply with the markdown table integrity rule.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace turbine blades** require Ra $\leq$ 0.4 µm to prevent fatigue crack initiation at the leading edge.
- **Hydraulic cylinder bores** require Ra $\leq$ 0.2 µm to ensure proper sealing and prevent leakage.
- **EDM-finished dies** for plastic injection molding require controlled Rz to facilitate proper material flow and ejection.
- **Bio-implants (knee/hip)** demand Ra $\leq$ 0.1 µm for biocompatibility and reduced cell adhesion.

## 2.6 Surface Finish Classification (ISO 1302 / ISO 21920)

| Roughness Grade | N Grade | Ra Range (µm) | Typical Process |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Very Fine | N1 | 0.025 – 0.05 | Super-finishing, Lapping |
| Fine | N3 | 0.1 – 0.2 | Precision Grinding, Honing |
| Medium | N6 | 0.8 – 1.6 | Fine Turning, Milling |
| Coarse | N9 | 3.2 – 6.3 | Standard Turning, Shaping |
| Very Coarse | N12 | 25 – 50 | Sawing, Hot Rolling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Ra (Arithmetic Mean Roughness)

The derivation of Ra begins from the **sampled profile of the workpiece surface** obtained via a stylus profilometer traversing the mean line.

**Step 1 — Define the Mean Line**
The mean line $\bar{y}$ is positioned such that the sum of all positive ordinates above it equals the sum of all negative ordinates below it over the sampling length $L$:

$$\int_0^L y(x) \, dx = 0$$

This means the **mean line is the centroid of the area under the profile**, ensuring that the integrated area above equals the integrated area below.

**Step 2 — Define the Profile Ordinate Function**
At every point $x$ along the sampling length, the ordinate $y(x)$ is the **vertical distance from the mean line to the actual surface profile**. Positive $y(x)$ denotes peaks; negative $y(x)$ denotes valleys.

**Step 3 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean of Absolute Ordinates**
To find Ra, we calculate the **arithmetic mean of the absolute value** of all ordinates over the sampling length. The absolute value is essential because positive and negative deviations would otherwise cancel:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance of Absolute Value:** If we used $y(x)$ directly, the integral would equal **zero** (by definition of the mean line). The absolute value prevents this cancellation, giving a true measure of average deviation magnitude.

**Step 4 — Discretized Form (Digital Profilometer Implementation)**
In digital measurement, the continuous integral is approximated as a discrete sum over $N$ sampled points:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i \vert$$

where $N = L / \Delta x$ is the number of sample points, and $\Delta x$ is the sampling interval (typically 0.5 µm to 5 µm).

**Step 5 — Extend to Evaluation Length**
Since a single sampling length may not be statistically representative, ISO standards require averaging over **five consecutive sampling lengths** (or more). The reported Ra is:

$$R_a^{\text{final}} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{j=1}^{5} R_{a,j}$$

where each $R_{a,j}$ is computed over its own sampling length $lr$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Rq (Root Mean Square Roughness)

**Step 1 — Square Each Ordinate**
To avoid sign cancellation (as in Ra), we square each ordinate $y(x)$ to make all values positive:

$$y(x)^2 \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } x$$

**Step 2 — Integrate the Squared Ordinates**
The total "energy" of the surface profile is captured by:

$$\int_0^L y(x)^2 \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Mean Square Value**
Divide by the sampling length to obtain the mean square roughness:

$$\overline{y^2} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L y(x)^2 \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Take the Square Root**
This recovers the units (µm) and yields Rq:

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{L} \int_0^L y(x)^2 \, dx}$$

**Step 5 — Relation to Ra (Gaussian Profile)**
For a **perfectly Gaussian (random) surface profile**, the probability density function of ordinates is:

$$p(y) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Using the standard Gaussian identity, the mean absolute value is:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert y \vert \cdot p(y) \, dy = \sigma \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}$$

Therefore:

$$R_a = \sigma \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \quad \text{and} \quad R_q = \sigma$$

Dividing:

$$\frac{R_a}{R_q} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \approx 0.7979 \approx 0.8$$

Hence, the well-known engineering approximation: $\mathbf{R_a \approx 0.8 \cdot R_q}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Surface Finish Across AMPs

| Manufacturing Process | Material Removal Mechanism | Typical Ra (µm) | Heat Affected Zone | Accuracy Class |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **EDM (Die-Sink)** | Thermal melting & vaporization | 0.2 – 3.2 | Yes (recast layer) | Medium |
| **WEDM** | Thermal erosion (wire) | 0.1 – 0.8 | Minimal | High |
| **LBM (Laser)** | Photothermal ablation | 1.0 – 6.3 | Significant | Low-Medium |
| **EBM (Electron Beam)** | Kinetic + thermal | 0.8 – 3.2 | Vacuum, minimal HAZ | Medium |
| **PAM (Plasma Arc)** | Thermal melting | 3.2 – 12.5 | Yes (large HAZ) | Low |
| **AJM (Abrasive Jet)** | Mechanical erosion | 0.4 – 3.2 | No HAZ (cold process) | Low |
| **USM (Ultrasonic)** | Mechanical hammering + cavitation | 0.2 – 1.6 | No HAZ | Medium |
| **ECM (Electrochemical)** | Electrochemical dissolution | 0.1 – 0.8 | No HAZ (cold) | Medium |
| **CHM (Chemical)** | Chemical etching | 0.4 – 1.6 | No HAZ | Low |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Calculating Ra from Profilometer Data

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Problem Pattern (Model Solution)**

**Problem:** A profilometer samples a surface over a length of 4 mm with 8 equally spaced points. The ordinate readings (in µm) at the 8 points are:
+0.8, −0.4, +1.2, −0.8, +0.6, −1.0, +0.4, −0.2.
Compute Ra and Rq.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute Mean Line Position**
Sum of ordinates:
$$\sum y_i = (0.8) + (-0.4) + (1.2) + (-0.8) + (0.6) + (-1.0) + (0.4) + (-0.2) = 0.6 \, \mu m$$

Mean ordinate:
$$\bar{y} = \frac{0.6}{8} = 0.075 \, \mu m$$

**Step 2: Shift All Ordinates to Mean Line**
The mean line correction shifts each $y_i \rightarrow y_i - \bar{y}$:

$$y_i' = y_i - 0.075$$

New ordinates:
+0.725, −0.475, +1.125, −0.875, +0.525, −1.075, +0.325, −0.275

**Step 3: Compute Ra**

$$R_a = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert y_i' \vert$$

Sum of absolute values:
$$\sum \vert y_i' \vert = 0.725 + 0.475 + 1.125 + 0.875 + 0.525 + 1.075 + 0.325 + 0.275 = 5.400 \, \mu m$$

$$R_a = \frac{5.400}{8} = 0.675 \, \mu m$$

**Step 4: Compute Rq**

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{8} \sum_{i=1}^{8} (y_i')^2}$$

Sum of squares:
$$\sum (y_i')^2 = 0.5256 + 0.2256 + 1.2656 + 0.7656 + 0.2756 + 1.1556 + 0.1056 + 0.0756 = 4.3950 \, \mu m^2$$

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{4.3950}{8}} = \sqrt{0.5494} = 0.741 \, \mu m$$

**Step 5: Verify the Empirical Relation**
$$0.8 \times R_q = 0.8 \times 0.741 = 0.593 \, \mu m$$

Compared to computed $R_a = 0.675 \, \mu m$ — close but not exact (the empirical 0.8 factor assumes a perfectly Gaussian profile, which a real surface rarely is).

**Final Answer:** $\mathbf{R_a = 0.675 \, \mu m}$ and $\mathbf{R_q = 0.741 \, \mu m}$.

## 3.5 Surface Finish in Non-Traditional Processes — Analytical Reasoning

**EDM Recast Layer Formation:** Spark discharge causes local melting (8000–12,000 °C). Molten metal is not fully ejected and resolidifies as a **recast (white) layer** typically 2–50 µm thick. This layer is **harder, more brittle, and often microcracked**, degrading fatigue life. Ra in EDM is governed by:

$$R_a^{\text{EDM}} \propto \sqrt{I_p \cdot t_{on}}$$

where $I_p$ is peak current and $t_{on}$ is pulse-on time. Reducing these improves Ra but reduces MRR.

**WEDM:** Uses a continuously traveling wire (typically 0.1–0.3 mm diameter brass/zinc-coated). The wire's vibration and spark gap variations create characteristic periodic striations on the cut surface. Ra is governed by pulse energy, wire tension, and dielectric flushing.

**USM:** Abrasive particles (B₄C, SiC, Al₂O₃) suspended in water are hammered against the workpiece by a vibrating tool (20–40 kHz). The surface exhibits **uniform hemispherical dimples** — Ra depends on abrasive grit size.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
