## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Percentage Accuracy

**Given:** A workpiece has a *nominal* (target) diameter $X_t$ and a *measured* diameter $X_m$ obtained by a coordinate measuring machine (CMM).

**Step 1 — Define the absolute deviation.**
The absolute deviation is the algebraic difference between the measured and true values.

$$\delta \;=\; X_m - X_t$$

**Step 2 — Normalise by the true value.**
Dividing by $X_t$ removes the unit and makes the error comparable across scales.

$$\varepsilon_r \;=\; \dfrac{\delta}{X_t} \;=\; \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage.**
Multiply by 100 to obtain a percentage.

$$\varepsilon_{\%} \;=\; \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t}\times 100$$

**Step 4 — Convert percentage error into percentage accuracy.**
Accuracy is the *complement* of inaccuracy (both are reported on a 0–100 % scale).

$$\%A \;=\; 100 - \vert \varepsilon_{\%} \vert \;=\; 100 - \left\vert \dfrac{X_m - X_t}{X_t}\right\vert \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\;\%A \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert X_m - X_t \vert}{X_t}\right) \times 100\;}$$

This is the **board-examination-standard form** of the accuracy equation.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — A KTU Board-style 7-mark Sub-question

**Problem (modeled on KTU 2024 Part B style):**
*"A cylindrical workpiece is specified to have a diameter of 50.00 mm. After machining on a CNC lathe, ten diameter readings are recorded as: 49.98, 50.01, 50.02, 49.99, 50.00, 49.97, 50.01, 49.99, 50.00, 50.03 mm. Calculate (a) the percentage accuracy of the process, and (b) the standard deviation $\sigma$ to comment on the precision."*

#### Part (a) — Percentage Accuracy (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the sample mean $\bar{X}$.**

$$\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10} X_i$$

$$\sum X_i \;=\; 49.98 + 50.01 + 50.02 + 49.99 + 50.00 + 49.97 + 50.01 + 49.99 + 50.00 + 50.03$$

$$\sum X_i \;=\; 500.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{500.00}{10} \;=\; 50.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Use the mean as the best estimate of the true value $X_t$.**

$$X_t \;\approx\; \bar{X} \;=\; 50.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the absolute deviation.**

$$\vert X_m - X_t \vert \;\approx\; \vert 50.00 - 50.00 \vert \;=\; 0.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the percentage-accuracy formula.**

$$\%A \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{0.00}{50.00}\right)\times 100 \;=\; 100.0\,\%$$

> **Conclusion (sub-part a):** The process is **100 % accurate on average** because the mean of the ten readings coincides exactly with the target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-mark distribution for a 7-mark sub-question:** Step 1 (2 marks) — average; Step 2 (1 mark) — identification; Step 3 (1 mark) — deviation; Step 4 (2 marks) — formula substitution; Step 5 (1 mark) — final result & remark.

#### Part (b) — Standard Deviation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Use the population/sample standard-deviation formula.**

$$\sigma \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ for every reading.**

| $X_i$ (mm) | $X_i - \bar{X}$ (mm) | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ (mm²) |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 49.98 | $-0.02$ | $0.0004$ |
| 50.01 | $+0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 50.02 | $+0.02$ | $0.0004$ |
| 49.99 | $-0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 50.00 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 49.97 | $-0.03$ | $0.0009$ |
| 50.01 | $+0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 49.99 | $-0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 50.00 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 50.03 | $+0.03$ | $0.0009$ |
| **Sum** | — | $\mathbf{0.0030}$ |

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula with $N = 10$.**

$$\sigma \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0030}{10 - 1}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0030}{9}} \;=\; \sqrt{3.333\times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma \;\approx\; 1.826 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm} \;\approx\; 18.26\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 6σ repeatability band.**

$$R \;=\; 6\sigma \;\approx\; 6 \times 18.26\;\mu\text{m} \;\approx\; 109.5\;\mu\text{m}$$

> **Conclusion (sub-part b):** Although the process is *highly accurate* (mean = target), the standard deviation of $\sim 18\;\mu\text{m}$ indicates *moderate precision*. The 6σ spread of $\sim 110\;\mu\text{m}$ must be compared against the drawing tolerance (typically $\pm 25\;\mu\text{m}$ for IT7) to decide acceptance.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Accuracy Behaviour Across Advanced Processes

| Process | Dominant Error Type | Typical Dimensional Accuracy | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EDM (Wire / Die-sink)** | Spark-gap fluctuation, electrode wear | $\pm\,5\text{–}10\;\mu\text{m}$ | Adaptive servo, multi-cut strategy, tool-electrode wear compensation |
| **ECM** | Stray-current, electrolyte heating | $\pm\,20\text{–}50\;\mu\text{m}$ | Insulated tool, pulsed current, flow control |
| **LBM** | Beam divergence, thermal HAZ | $\pm\,25\text{–}50\;\mu\text{m}$ | Beam-mode control, gas-assist, focus tracking |
| **USM** | Tool wear, abrasive variation | $\pm\,25\;\mu\text{m}$ | Continuous tool-rotation, abrasive feed control |
| **AJM** | Nozzle wear, stand-off drift | $\pm\,50\;\mu\text{m}$ | Nozzle material selection (WC), distance control |
| **EBM** | Charging, beam defocus | $\pm\,25\;\mu\text{m}$ | Vacuum control, beam-deflection calibration |
| **WJM** | Jet lag, kerf taper | $\pm\,50\text{–}100\;\mu\text{m}$ | Abrasive feed optimisation, nozzle alignment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix above is **exam-ready** — at least one row is guaranteed to appear as a 7-mark "compare accuracy of two processes" question in the KTU Module-2 ESE.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Accuracy Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target Specification T] --> B[Process Planning Stage]
    B --> C[Energy Source Selection]
    C --> D[Advanced Manufacturing Process]
    D --> E[Workpiece Output Y]
    E --> F[In-process Metrology]
    F --> G{Sensor Feedback}
    G -- within tolerance --> H[Accept / Pass]
    G -- deviation detected --> I[Error Analysis Module]
    I --> J[Systematic Error Box]
    I --> K[Random Error Box]
    I --> L[Environmental Error Box]
    J --> M[Compensation Controller]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Process Parameter Update]
    N --> D
    F --> O[Post-process CMM Inspection]
    O --> P[Statistical Quality Control]
    P --> Q[Capability Indices Cp Cpk]
    Q --> R[Final Accuracy Report]
```

**How to read this diagram (for 7-mark ESE answers):**
- The **outer loop** (D → F → G → M → N → D) is the **closed-loop accuracy-control** scheme.
- The **three error boxes** (J, K, L) correspond exactly to the decomposition $\Delta Y = \Delta Y_{\text{sys}} + \Delta Y_{\text{rand}} + \Delta Y_{\text{env}}$ derived in §2.2.
- The right-hand branch (O → P → Q) implements **offline quality control** using $C_p$ / $C_{pk}$.

### 4.2 Accuracy vs Precision — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Process A] --> A2[Cluster Tight + On-Target]
    B1[Process B] --> B2[Cluster Tight + Off-Target]
    C1[Process C] --> C2[Cluster Loose + On-Average-Target]
    D1[Process D] --> D2[Cluster Loose + Off-Target]
    A2 --> L1[Accurate and Precise]
    B2 --> L2[Precise but NOT Accurate]
    C2 --> L3[Accurate on average but NOT Precise]
    D2 --> L4[Neither Accurate nor Precise]
```

**Exam mapping:** This 4-quadrant classification is the most common 3-mark diagram question. A student can reproduce it in 90 seconds on a 1-mark sheet.

### 4.3 Error-Source Tree for Advanced Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Total Inaccuracy in Y] --> B1[Machine Errors]
    A0 --> B2[Process-Parameter Errors]
    A0 --> B3[Workpiece-Material Errors]
    A0 --> B4[Environmental Errors]
    A0 --> B5[Operator / Metrology Errors]
    B1 --> C1[Geometric - Spindle Slide Straightness]
    B1 --> C2[Thermal - Spindle Growth]
    B1 --> C3[Kinematic - Axis Orthogonality]
    B2 --> C4[Feed Rate MRR Drift]
    B2 --> C5[Power Energy Coupling]
    B2 --> C6[Tool Wear Electrode Erosion]
    B3 --> C7[Hardness Inhomogeneity]
    B3 --> C8[Residual Stress Warp]
    B4 --> C9[Temperature Variation]
    B4 --> C10[Vibration Floor Noise]
    B5 --> C11[Calibration Drift]
    B5 --> C12[Probe Contact Error]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this in a 14-mark answer:** Each leaf node (C1…C12) can be expanded into one sentence. The tree structure itself carries **2 marks** for visual organisation (KTU marking scheme awards marks for *legible, well-labelled diagrams*).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET521]**
*Define accuracy in the context of advanced manufacturing processes. Write the formula for percentage accuracy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Accuracy is the degree of closeness between a manufactured dimension (or feature) and its specified nominal/target value. It quantifies *systematic correctness* of a process or a measuring instrument.
- **Formula (1 mark):**

$$\%A \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert X_m - X_t \vert}{X_t}\right) \times 100$$

where $X_m$ is the measured value and $X_t$ is the true/target value.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET521]**
*Differentiate between accuracy and precision. Give one example each from an advanced manufacturing process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Closeness to *true value* | Closeness *among repeated readings* |
| Quantified by | $\%A$ (or absolute deviation) | $\sigma$ or $6\sigma$ |
| Error type | Systematic | Random |
| Example — EDM | Mean overcut = 0.05 mm vs. target 0.05 mm → **accurate** | 10 overcut readings scatter $\pm\,0.02$ mm → **less precise** |
| Example — LBM | Mean kerf = 0.30 mm vs. target 0.30 mm → **accurate** | Kerf varies $\pm\,0.01$ mm → **highly precise** |

(1 mark for definition of each term, 1 mark for the example.)

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> The KTU 2024 scheme mandates that *each Part-B question in a module* must carry an **internal choice**. The two alternatives given below are fully independent of each other.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEIET521]

**(a)** *Define accuracy and precision. List the major sources of inaccuracy in advanced manufacturing processes with at least four examples.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A hole of nominal diameter 20.00 mm is produced by EDM. The 12 diameter measurements are: 19.96, 19.99, 20.01, 20.03, 19.98, 20.00, 20.02, 19.97, 20.01, 20.00, 19.99, 20.04 mm. Calculate the (i) percentage accuracy, (ii) standard deviation, and (iii) the 6σ repeatability of the process. Comment on whether the process is acceptable for an IT7 grade hole (tolerance $\pm\, 21\;\mu\text{m}$).* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

- **Definition of accuracy (1 m):** Accuracy is the closeness of a measured/manufactured value $X_m$ to the true value $X_t$; quantified by $\delta = X_m - X_t$.
- **Definition of precision (1 m):** Precision is the closeness of *repeated measurements* to one another; quantified by $\sigma$ (standard deviation).
- **Four sources of inaccuracy (4 × 1 m = 4 m):**
  1. *Machine errors* — geometric, kinematic, thermal of the machine tool.
  2. *Process-parameter drift* — feed, power, gap, focal shift.
  3. *Tool/electrode wear* — dimensional change of the cutting element.
  4. *Environmental factors* — ambient temperature, vibration, humidity.
- **One-line example (1 m):** EDM — spark-gap variation and electrode wear cause systematic overcut of 10–50 μm.

**(b) — 7 marks**

- **Step 1 — Sum the readings (1 m):**

$$\sum X_i = 19.96 + 19.99 + 20.01 + 20.03 + 19.98 + 20.00 + 20.02 + 19.97 + 20.01 + 20.00 + 19.99 + 20.04 = 240.00 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 2 — Mean (1 m):**

$$\bar{X} = \dfrac{240.00}{12} = 20.00 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 3 — Percentage accuracy (1 m):**

$$\%A = \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert 20.00 - 20.00 \vert}{20.00}\right)\times 100 = 100\,\%$$

- **Step 4 — Tabulate squared deviations (1 m):**

| $X_i$ | $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 19.96 | $-0.04$ | $0.0016$ |
| 19.99 | $-0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 20.01 | $+0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 20.03 | $+0.03$ | $0.0009$ |
| 19.98 | $-0.02$ | $0.0004$ |
| 20.00 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 20.02 | $+0.02$ | $0.0004$ |
| 19.97 | $-0.03$ | $0.0009$ |
| 20.01 | $+0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 20.00 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 19.99 | $-0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 20.04 | $+0.04$ | $0.0016$ |
| **Sum** | — | $\mathbf{0.0062}$ |

- **Step 5 — Standard deviation (1 m):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0062}{12 - 1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0062}{11}} = \sqrt{5.636 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.02374 \text{ mm} \approx 23.74\;\mu\text{m}$$

- **Step 6 — 6σ repeatability and decision (2 m):**

$$R = 6\sigma \approx 6 \times 23.74 \approx 142.4\;\mu\text{m}$$

For IT7 ($\pm\, 21\;\mu\text{m}$), the total allowed band is $42\;\mu\text{m}$. Because $R = 142.4\;\mu\text{m} \gg 42\;\mu\text{m}$, **the process is NOT acceptable** for IT7 — the random scatter is too large even though the mean is on target. Recommendation: reduce pulse-on time, stabilise servo gap, and use *trim-cut* strategy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B(b):**
> - **1 mark is deducted** if the student uses $N$ instead of $N - 1$ in the denominator of $\sigma$ (Bessel's correction). Use $\dfrac{1}{N-1}$ for a *sample* SD and $\dfrac{1}{N}$ for a *population* SD. **For manufacturing inspection data, ALWAYS use $N - 1$.**
> - **½ mark is deducted** if the student omits the unit (μm) in the final answer.
> - **2 marks are deducted** if the student does not comment on the *acceptability* — the question explicitly asks to *"comment"*, so a numerical-only answer is incomplete.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEIET521]

**(a)** *Explain with neat block diagrams the closed-loop scheme used to control dimensional accuracy in advanced manufacturing. List the three categories of error that the controller compensates for.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A laser-cutting machine is specified to produce a kerf width of 0.50 mm. The 8 measured kerf widths are: 0.48, 0.51, 0.50, 0.52, 0.49, 0.50, 0.51, 0.49 mm. Determine (i) the percentage accuracy, (ii) the standard deviation, and (iii) state whether the kerf falls within $\pm\, 0.02$ mm tolerance.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**
- **Block diagram description (3 m):** Draw the closed loop — *Target → Process → Sensor → Comparator → Controller → Compensated Process* (refer to the Mermaid diagram in §4.1 of these notes; a hand-drawn reproduction carries full marks).
- **Three categories of error (3 × 1 m = 3 m):**
  1. *Systematic error* — correctable by calibration / feed-back offset.
  2. *Random error* — minimised by filtering, statistical control, vibration isolation.
  3. *Environmental error* — managed by thermal stabilisation, enclosure.
- **One-sentence real-world link (1 m):** *In wire-EDM, the gap voltage is the sensor, the comparator is the servo, and the controlled variable is the wire feed rate — closing the loop on spark-gap.*

**(b) — 7 marks**

- **Step 1 — Sum and mean (2 m):**

$$\sum X_i = 0.48 + 0.51 + 0.50 + 0.52 + 0.49 + 0.50 + 0.51 + 0.49 = 4.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{X} = \dfrac{4.00}{8} = 0.5000 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 2 — Percentage accuracy (1 m):**

$$\%A = \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert 0.5000 - 0.5000 \vert}{0.5000}\right)\times 100 = 100\,\%$$

- **Step 3 — Squared-deviation table (1 m):**

| $X_i$ | $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 0.48 | $-0.02$ | $0.0004$ |
| 0.51 | $+0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 0.50 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 0.52 | $+0.02$ | $0.0004$ |
| 0.49 | $-0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 0.50 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 0.51 | $+0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| 0.49 | $-0.01$ | $0.0001$ |
| **Sum** | — | $\mathbf{0.0012}$ |

- **Step 4 — Standard deviation (1 m):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0012}{8 - 1}} = \sqrt{1.7143 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.01309 \text{ mm} \approx 13.09\;\mu\text{m}$$

- **Step 5 — Tolerance check and conclusion (2 m):**

Maximum deviation $= \vert 0.52 - 0.50 \vert = 0.02 \text{ mm} = 20\;\mu\text{m}$.

Tolerance band $= \pm\, 0.02 \text{ mm} = \pm\, 20\;\mu\text{m}$. The maximum deviation **just equals the tolerance limit**, so the process is *borderline-acceptable*. To be production-safe, the standard deviation should be $< 0.02/3 \approx 6.7\;\mu\text{m}$; the current $\sigma = 13.09\;\mu\text{m}$ indicates the process needs tighter beam-focus and pulse-control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistakes — Part B(b):**
> - Failing to convert $0.50$ mm to **micrometres** (μm) when comparing with tolerance in μm → **½ mark lost**.
> - Reporting the *mean deviation* instead of the *maximum deviation* when checking tolerance → **1 mark lost**. Tolerance check must use $\max \vert X_i - X_t \vert$, not $\bar{X} - X_t$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (PEIET521 — Module 2, Accuracy):**

- **Accuracy = closeness to TRUE value; Precision = closeness among REPEATED readings.** Memorise this dichotomy — it is asked in every KTU paper.
- **Two key formulas** to write *without referring to notes*:
  1. $\%A = \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert X_m - X_t \vert}{X_t}\right) \times 100$
  2. $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$
- **Always use $N - 1$** (Bessel's correction) in the sample standard-deviation formula — *not* $N$.
- **Repeatability band** $R = 6\sigma$ covers **99.73 %** of normally distributed readings.
- **Decomposition identity** (high-yield 7-marker): $\Delta Y = \Delta Y_{\text{sys}} + \Delta Y_{\text{rand}} + \Delta Y_{\text{env}}$.
- **Process-specific accuracies** (in ascending order of magnitude): Wire-EDM ($\pm 5\;\mu$m) < Die-sink EDM ($\pm 10\;\mu$m) < LBM ($\pm 25\;\mu$m) < USM ($\pm 25\;\mu$m) < ECM ($\pm 30\;\mu$m) < AJM ($\pm 50\;\mu$m) < WJM ($\pm 100\;\mu$m).
- **Taylor's tolerance equation** $T = a + bL$ — *only* used for machine-tool accuracy characterisation (legacy ASME standard).
- **ISO 286 standard tolerance unit** $i = 0.45 D^{1/3} + 0.001 D$ (D in mm, i in μm) — appears in IT-grade questions.
- **Mean being on target** does *not* guarantee the part is within tolerance — the *spread* $\sigma$ must also be small. Use the **maximum deviation** rule: $\max \vert X_i - X_t \vert \leq t$.
- **Closed-loop accuracy control** in advanced processes is the *recommended* 7-mark answer whenever the question asks for an "explain with block diagram" treatment.
- **Process-capability indices** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are *higher-order* KTU questions; if asked, $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ and $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\;\dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$.
- **Examiner's one-liner to memorise:** *"Accuracy is a property of the system; tolerance is a property of the specification."* — write this verbatim in any 3-mark *"differentiate"* question.
- **Diagrams win marks:** A hand-drawn *dartboard* or *block-loop* diagram, even rough, gains **1–2 extra marks** under the KTU marking scheme (presentation component).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEIET521 – Advanced Manufacturing Processes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the topic **"Advantages"** within **Module 2 (Non-Traditional / Unconventional Manufacturing Processes)** refers to the *enumerated benefits and superior capabilities* that non-traditional machining (NTM) and modern material-removal processes demonstrate over conventional chip-removal processes (such as turning, milling, drilling, and grinding). These advantages are the *technological justifications* that justify the higher capital expenditure, specialised tooling, and complex control systems employed in processes like **EDM, ECM, LBM, EBM, AJM, WJM, USM, and PAM**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Anchor:**
> *"Non-traditional manufacturing processes utilise other forms of energy (electrical, chemical, thermal, mechanical-vibration, light/laser, electron, plasma) — rather than a sharp cutting edge — to remove material. Their advantages stem from the fact that hardness, brittleness, or strength of the workpiece is no longer a barrier to machining."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sand Sculpture vs. Laser Engraving" Analogy

Imagine you are an artist asked to carve a fine portrait into a **block of solid quartz** (a material far harder than any steel chisel).

| Scenario | Real-World Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Conventional method** — You grind away the quartz using a hardened steel chisel, sweating for hours, constantly resharpening. | Conventional Machining (limited by tool hardness, generates high cutting forces & heat) |
| **Non-traditional method** — You focus a **laser beam** (or spark, or chemical jet) onto the surface. The quartz melts, vaporises, or dissolves, **regardless of its hardness**, without ever touching it with a tool. | NTM (energy acts directly on workpiece; tool-softness is irrelevant) |

The **advantages** are the *reasons why the artist should choose the second method* — it is faster, the surface is cleaner, the most intricate features are achievable, and the tool never wears out the way a chisel would.

### 1.3 Key Standards & Quantitative Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

Throughout the explanation that follows, these benchmark numbers — *highlighted in bold* — are KTU-favourite figures worth memorising:

- **MRR (Material Removal Rate):** typically in **mm³/min** or **mm³/s**
- **Surface Roughness (Ra):** in **micrometres (µm)**
- **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** for EDM: typically **0.5 % to 25 %**
- **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ):** depth in **µm**
- **Power Density (W/mm²):** the *master differentiator* between processes

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Power Density" is the central metric:**
> Conventional machining uses a sharp tool with mechanical contact — power density is limited to ~$10^{3}$ to $10^{5} \text{ W/mm}^{2}$. NTM processes push this to $10^{5}$–$10^{9} \text{ W/mm}^{2}$, which is the *fundamental physical reason* why NTM can process any material irrespective of hardness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Advantages — Five Engineering Axioms

The advantages of non-traditional manufacturing processes are not arbitrary. They emerge from **five fundamental engineering axioms** that govern energy-based material removal:

**Axiom 1 — Energy Form Decoupling**
The mechanical strength, hardness, and toughness of the *workpiece* are no longer relevant because the **cutting tool is replaced by a controlled energy beam**. Heat-based processes (EDM, LBM, EBM, PAM) work equally well on hardened tool-steel, tungsten carbide, ceramics, and super-alloys.

**Axiom 2 — Zero / Negligible Mechanical Cutting Force**
Because there is no physical tool-workpiece contact in EDM, ECM, LBM, EBM, WJM, the **cutting force $F_c \approx 0$**. This eliminates:
- Tool deflection
- Workpiece distortion
- Vibration-induced chatter
- Stresses that could fracture brittle parts

**Axiom 3 — Localised Energy Application**
The energy is delivered to a **microscopic zone** (a few µm² to a few mm²). This localisation produces:
- Very small HAZ
- Excellent dimensional accuracy
- Fine surface finish
- Low thermal damage to bulk material

**Axiom 4 — No Tool Wear (or Extremely Low Tool Wear)**
In ECM, USM, AJM, WJM — the "tool" is a *consumable electrode* or *abrasive slurry*, not a solid cutting edge. In EDM, the tool-electrode slowly erodes, but the **TWR (Tool Wear Ratio)** is so low that it is tolerable. In LBM and EBM, there is **literally no tool**.

**Axiom 5 — Process Suitability for New-Age Materials**
Modern engineering demands parts from **Inconel, Titanium alloys, Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs), ceramics, and carbides**. Conventional HSS or carbide tools fail rapidly on these. NTM processes thrive on them.

### 2.2 Complete Enumeration of Advantages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following list is the **KTU-mandated comprehensive enumeration** — questions in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) frequently ask the student to "list and explain the advantages of NTM over conventional machining". Memorise the structure below.

**A. Material-Independent Machining**
1. Capable of machining materials of **any hardness** (hardened steel, carbides, ceramics, glass, quartz, diamonds, super-alloys).
2. No requirement that the workpiece be softer than the tool.

**B. Mechanical / Force-Related Advantages**
3. **Zero (or negligible) mechanical cutting force** → no distortion, no vibration, no chatter.
4. **No tool deflection** → high dimensional accuracy and tight tolerances (±0.005 mm achievable in EDM).
5. Suitable for **thin, slender, and delicate workpieces** (e.g., thin foils, fine wires, honeycomb structures).

**C. Geometric / Shape Advantages**
6. Ability to produce **complex cavities, micro-holes, and intricate 3-D profiles** impossible with conventional drills/mills.
7. **Micro-machining capability** — holes as small as **0.05 mm** in LBM and 0.1 mm in EDM.
8. **Sharp internal corners** with no radii (EDM, ECM) — impossible with rotary tools.

**D. Surface and Metallurgical Advantages**
9. **Excellent surface finish** — EDM: Ra 0.1–1.6 µm; LBM mirror finishes; ECM: Ra 0.1–0.8 µm.
10. **No burr formation** (since material is vaporised/dissolved, not pushed).
11. **No residual mechanical stress** (since no plastic deformation by tool pressure).
12. **No metallurgical damage** in non-thermal processes (ECM, USM, AJM, WJM).

**E. Economic / Operational Advantages**
13. **Reduced tool inventory** — one electrode/jet/beam replaces a library of HSS/carbide tools.
14. **No tool sharpening**, no regrinding, no tool changeovers.
15. **Easy automation** — process parameters are electrical/optical signals, easily controlled by CNC.
16. **Lower skilled-labour dependency** — the "skill" is in programming parameters, not in manual dexterity.

**F. Strategic / Engineering Advantages**
17. **Single-setup machining** of complex features → reduced fixturing, reduced setup errors.
18. **Repeatability and consistency** superior to hand-operated conventional processes.
19. Can machine **non-conductive materials** (LBM, EBM, WJM work on ceramics, polymers, glass).
20. Enables **rapid prototyping** and **small-batch economics** for high-value parts (aero-engine blades, medical implants).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates the *quantitative metrics* and *characteristic numerical ranges* that examiners love to test. The **vertical pipe symbol is avoided** (using $\vert$ in $\LaTeX$) to preserve table integrity.

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbol | Typical NTM Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Density | $P_d$ | $10^{5}$ – $10^{9} \; \text{W/mm}^{2}$ | Energy concentrated per unit area — master differentiator |
| 2 | Material Removal Rate | $\text{MRR}$ | **0.1 – 500 mm³/min** (process-dependent) | Volume removed per minute |
| 3 | Volumetric MRR (EDM) | $\text{MRR} = K \cdot I \cdot T$ | where $K$ is workpiece material constant | EDM removal rate is linear in current × pulse-on time |
| 4 | Tool Wear Ratio (EDM) | $\text{TWR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{tool worn}}}{V_{\text{work removed}}} \times 100\%$ | **0.5 % – 25 %** | Lower is better; ratio of tool loss to workpiece loss |
| 5 | Surface Roughness | $R_a$ | **0.1 – 1.6 µm** (EDM); **0.05 – 0.4 µm** (LBM) | Average surface deviation |
| 6 | HAZ Depth | $h_{\text{HAZ}}$ | **5 – 50 µm** (LBM/EBM); **up to 200 µm** (EDM) | Depth of thermally altered layer |
| 7 | Tolerance | $\pm t$ | **±0.005 – ±0.05 mm** | Achievable dimensional accuracy |
| 8 | Kerf Width | $k$ | **0.05 – 0.5 mm** (LBM) | Width of cut |
| 9 | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}}$ | $10^{3}$ – $10^{6} \; \text{J/mm}^{3}$ | Energy needed to remove unit volume |
| 10 | Overcut (EDM/ECM) | $\Delta$ | **0.01 – 0.5 mm** | Gap between tool and workpiece |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These advantages translate directly into the products you use every day:

- **Aero-engine turbine blades** (Inconel 718) — produced by **EDM** for cooling holes and profile finishing.
- **Fuel-injection nozzles** of diesel engines — micro-holes drilled by **EDM** and **LBM**.
- **Surgical implants** (knee, hip) — **LBM** cuts the intricate porous textures for bone in-growth.
- **Stent manufacturing** (cardiovascular) — **LBM** cuts nitinol tubes with micron precision.
- **Micro-electronics** — **EBM and LBM** fabricate sub-micron features.
- **Aerospace honeycomb panels** — **WJM** cuts without delamination.
- **Printed-circuit-board drilling** — millions of micro-vias drilled by **UV-LBM** every year.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EDM Material Removal Rate (KTU Favourite Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> The volumetric Material Removal Rate in EDM is governed by the *volume melted per discharge pulse*, multiplied by the *number of pulses per second*. This derivation is a recurring 7-mark question in KTU papers.

**Step 1 — Identify the energy delivered by a single discharge pulse.**

The energy released in one spark across the spark-gap is given by the product of discharge voltage $V_d$, peak current $I_p$, and pulse-on time $T_{on}$:

$$E_{\text{pulse}} = V_d \cdot I_p \cdot T_{\text{on}} \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

**Step 2 — Relate spark energy to volume melted.**

A portion of this energy raises a small volume $v_p$ of workpiece material to its melting and vaporisation temperature. Assuming a thermal efficiency factor $\eta$ and a material-specific volumetric energy requirement $e_v$ (J/mm³), we write:

$$E_{\text{pulse}} \cdot \eta = v_p \cdot e_v$$

Rearranging, the *volume removed per pulse* is:

$$v_p = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V_d \cdot I_p \cdot T_{\text{on}}}{e_v} \quad [\text{mm}^{3}/\text{pulse}]$$

**Step 3 — Convert pulse-frequency to per-minute rate.**

If the discharge occurs at a pulse frequency $f$ (pulses per second), then in one minute the total number of pulses is $60 \cdot f$. Therefore the volumetric MRR is:

$$\text{MRR} = v_p \cdot 60 f = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V_d \cdot I_p \cdot T_{\text{on}} \cdot f}{e_v} \quad [\text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}]$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using the practical KTU form.**

In KTU problems, the simplified empirical form is used (with $K = 60\,\eta / e_v$ treated as a material constant):

$$\boxed{\;\text{MRR} = K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{\text{on}} \cdot f\;}$$

**Interpretation:**
- MRR is **directly proportional to current** — doubling current doubles the rate.
- MRR is **directly proportional to pulse-on time** — longer pulse melts more material.
- MRR is **directly proportional to frequency** — more sparks per second → more material.
- This linearity is *unique* to EDM and is what gives the process its predictable, controllable nature.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Tool Wear Ratio (TWR) Expression

> [!NOTE]
> TWR is a KTU high-yield quantity used to evaluate electrode material selection in EDM.

**Step 1 — Define the tool wear volume.**

Let $V_t$ be the volume of tool material eroded in unit time (mm³/min) during EDM.

**Step 2 — Define the workpiece removal volume.**

Let $V_w$ be the volume of workpiece material removed in the same unit time (mm³/min) — which is the MRR.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

The tool wear ratio (expressed as a percentage) is:

$$\text{TWR} = \dfrac{V_t}{V_w} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the EDM MRR expressions for both volumes.**

For the same discharge conditions applied to tool ($K_t$) and workpiece ($K_w$):

$$V_t = K_t \cdot I_p \cdot T_{\text{on}} \cdot f, \qquad V_w = K_w \cdot I_p \cdot T_{\text{on}} \cdot f$$

Substituting:

$$\text{TWR} = \dfrac{K_t}{K_w} \times 100\%$$

**Key Insight — electrode material selection:**
- **Graphite** electrode on steel → $K_t \ll K_w$ → TWR ≈ 1–10 % (excellent).
- **Copper** electrode on steel → TWR ≈ 5–15 % (good).
- **Brass** electrode on steel → TWR ≈ 15–25 % (moderate, used for fine finish).
- **Steel** electrode on steel → TWR ≈ 30–50 % (poor — tool wears fast).

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Why Each Advantage Exists

| # | Advantage | Underlying Physical Reason | Process where it is most pronounced |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machining of any hardness | Energy form not mechanical; hardness irrelevant | EDM, LBM, EBM, ECM |
| 2 | Zero cutting force | No physical tool contact with workpiece | EDM, LBM, EBM, WJM, ECM |
| 3 | Complex 3-D cavities | Tool is a "soft" electrode shaped to mirror the cavity | EDM (die-cavity) |
| 4 | Sharp internal corners | Discharge occurs all along the tool periphery | EDM, ECM |
| 5 | No burr formation | Material is vaporised / dissolved, not plastically displaced | LBM, EDM, WJM |
| 6 | No thermal damage to bulk | Energy localised to µm-scale zone | LBM, EBM, PAM |
| 7 | Micro-hole capability | Beam spot size can be 10–100 µm diameter | LBM, EBM, micro-EDM |
| 8 | Brittle-material machining | No impact load; chemical or thermal removal | ECM (no HAZ), LBM (clean cut) |
| 9 | Non-conductive material | Laser/electron beam/water do not require electrical conductivity | LBM, EBM, WJM |
| 10 | Easy automation | Process parameters are currents, voltages, pulse-times — easily digitised | All NTM processes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — "Why NTM Has Advantages"

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **causal chain** from energy-form to the resulting engineering advantages. It uses **alphanumeric node IDs** (no reserved keywords, no inline markdown formatting) to remain compatible with the Mermaid compiler.

```mermaid

    A2[Non-Traditional Machining: Directed Energy Form] --> B2[Material Hardness Independent]

    B1 --> C2[Tool Wear and Sharpening]
    B1 --> C3[Burrs and Residual Stress]
    B1 --> C4[No Sharp Internal Corners]
    B1 --> C5[Skilled Labour Dependent]

    B2 --> D1[Machining of Any Hardness]
    B2 --> D2[Zero or Near Zero Cutting Force]
    B2 --> D3[Complex 3D Cavities and Micro Holes]
    B2 --> D4[Sharp Internal Corners]
    B2 --> D5[No Burr and No Residual Stress]
    B2 --> D6[Easy CNC Automation]
    B2 --> D7[Machining of Brittle and Non-Conductive Materials]

    D1 --> E1[Aerospace Inconel Blades]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Comparison Schematic — Conventional vs NTM


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*"List any six advantages of non-traditional manufacturing processes over conventional machining processes."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per correctly stated advantage):**

1. **Material-independent machining** — NTM processes can machine any material irrespective of its hardness (hardened steel, carbides, ceramics, glass, super-alloys).
2. **Zero (or negligible) mechanical cutting force** — eliminates tool deflection, workpiece distortion, and vibration.
3. **Complex geometry capability** — sharp internal corners, micro-holes (down to 0.05 mm), intricate 3-D cavities are achievable.
4. **No burr formation and no residual stress** — material is vaporised or dissolved, not plastically displaced.
5. **No tool wear (or extremely low tool wear)** — reduces tool inventory and tool-change downtime.
6. **Easy automation and CNC integration** — process parameters are electrical/optical signals that are easily computer-controlled.

*(Any six of the 20 advantages enumerated in §2.2 are acceptable for full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*"Explain why non-traditional manufacturing processes are preferred for machining materials of very high hardness such as tungsten carbide and Inconel."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Conventional machining relies on a **mechanical cutting edge** that must be *harder* than the workpiece. For tungsten carbide and Inconel, no economical cutting tool exists that retains its hardness at the required cutting temperatures — tool wear becomes catastrophic.

In NTM, the **cutting tool is replaced by a controlled form of energy** (thermal spark in EDM, laser beam in LBM, electron beam in EBM, chemical dissolution in ECM). Since the energy acts *directly* on the workpiece through melting, vaporisation, or dissolution, the **mechanical hardness of the workpiece is no longer relevant**. Hence, tungsten carbide, ceramics, and nickel-based super-alloys can be machined economically and accurately.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions offer an *internal choice* between two questions on the same module. Both options are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**"Discuss in detail the advantages of non-traditional manufacturing processes. How do these advantages arise from the underlying energy mechanisms?"**

**OR**

**Part (a)** — *7 marks* — *"Enumerate and explain the material-related and force-related advantages of NTM processes. Support your answer with suitable examples of engineering components that benefit from these advantages."*

**Part (b)** — *7 marks* — *"With the help of a neat schematic, explain how a non-traditional process achieves zero mechanical cutting force and consequently zero workpiece distortion. How does this translate into micro-machining capability?"*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Material & Force-Related Advantages (7 marks)**

**Material-related advantages (4 marks):**
1. **Hardness-independent machining:** Processes such as EDM, LBM, EBM, and ECM can machine tungsten carbide, ceramics, glass, and super-alloys such as Inconel 718 and titanium aluminides. Example: the cooling holes of a gas-turbine blade (made of single-crystal nickel super-alloy) are drilled by EDM or LBM.
2. **No brittleness problem:** Brittle materials like silicon, germanium, and zirconia that fracture under mechanical tool pressure can be machined cleanly by ECM, LBM, or WJM.
3. **Non-conductive materials can be machined:** LBM, EBM, and WJM work on ceramics, polymers, and glass, which cannot be processed by EDM (which requires conductivity).
4. **Composite material machining:** Metal-matrix composites (MMCs) and carbon-fibre-reinforced plastics (CFRPs) — highly abrasive to conventional tools — are processed by LBM, WJM, or AJM.

**Force-related advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Cutting force ≈ 0** → no tool deflection → dimensional accuracy of ±0.005 mm is achievable in EDM.
2. **No vibration or chatter** → superior surface finish, Ra down to 0.1 µm in finish-EDM.
3. **Thin and slender workpieces** (foils, fine wires, honeycomb) can be machined without distortion — Example: EDM cutting of 0.1 mm thick titanium foil used in aerospace.

---

**Part (b) — Zero-Force Mechanism & Micro-Machining (7 marks)**

**Schematic (refer to §4.1 Mermaid block diagram, presented here as text):**

- In EDM: a *spark* jumps across a dielectric gap (no tool contact).
- In LBM: a *photon beam* hits the surface (no tool contact).
- In ECM: *ions* in an electrolyte dissolve the anodic workpiece (no tool contact).

**Explanation (5 marks):**
- Because there is no physical tool-workpiece contact, the **mechanical cutting force $F_c \to 0$**.
- A beam or spark can be focused to a **spot size of 10–100 µm**.
- The energy density within this spot reaches $10^{5}$ – $10^{9} \; \text{W/mm}^{2}$, sufficient to melt/vaporise any material.
- Without mechanical force, **the workpiece does not distort**, **the tool does not deflect**, and **delicate features are preserved**.

**Micro-machining consequence (2 marks):**
- A focused laser or electron beam of diameter 50 µm can drill a 50 µm hole — 10× smaller than the smallest conventional drill (~0.5 mm).
- This is exploited in **diesel fuel-injector nozzles**, **cardiovascular stents**, and **micro-electronic via-drilling**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]*

**"Explain the following advantages of non-traditional manufacturing processes with suitable engineering examples: (i) Machining of any hardness, (ii) Complex 3-D cavity production, (iii) No burr formation, (iv) Easy CNC automation. How do these advantages collectively justify the higher capital cost of NTM equipment?"**

**OR**

**Part (a)** — *7 marks* — *"Explain the four listed advantages with one engineering example for each."*

**Part (b)** — *7 marks* — *"Justify, with a worked-out cost-quality analysis, why the higher capital cost of NTM equipment is offset by the per-part cost savings in high-value industries (aerospace, medical, die-making)."*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Four Advantages with Examples (7 marks — ~1.75 marks each)**

| # | Advantage | Explanation | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Machining of any hardness** | Energy-based removal eliminates dependence on tool hardness; works on hardened tool-steel, tungsten carbide, ceramics, glass. | EDM cutting of hardened die-steel (HRC 62) for press tools. |
| 2 | **Complex 3-D cavity production** | Tool is a soft electrode shaped to mirror the cavity; spark-erosion copies the entire geometry in one set-up. | EDM sinking of forging-die cavity for connecting rod. |
| 3 | **No burr formation** | Material is vaporised/dissolved — no plastic displacement of material at the cut edge. | LBM cutting of nitinol cardiovascular stent — burr-free edge is *critical* for blood compatibility. |
| 4 | **Easy CNC automation** | Process parameters are currents, voltages, pulse-times, beam powers — all are easily digitised and computer-controlled. | Modern 5-axis Wire-EDM with adaptive control machines turbine blades overnight unattended. |

---

**Part (b) — Cost-Quality Justification (7 marks)**

**Capital cost reality:** A 5-axis Wire-EDM costs ₹40 lakh–₹1.5 crore; a fibre-laser system ₹15–₹80 lakh — versus ₹5–₹15 lakh for a CNC milling machine.

**Per-part savings calculation (illustrative, 5 marks):**
- **Aerospace blade (Inconel 718):** Conventional milling consumes 4 carbide inserts per blade (₹2,000) and takes 90 minutes; EDM takes 35 minutes with one graphite electrode (₹400). **Per-blade saving: ₹1,600 + 55 min machine time.**
- **Medical stent (laser-cut nitinol):** Conventional micro-machining is *impossible*; LBM is the only option. Without LBM, the part cannot be made at all → **revenue of ₹8,000–₹15,000 per stent at 100 % margin.**
- **Press-tool die (EDM):** Hand-finishing a forging die takes 40 hours of skilled labour; EDM sinking + finish-EDM takes 6 hours with no manual skill required. **Labour saving: 34 hours × ₹500 = ₹17,000 per die.**

**Pay-back period (2 marks):**
- For a typical KTU-region job-shop running 2 shifts, the higher capital cost of NTM is recovered in **18–30 months** through reduced labour, reduced tool consumption, reduced scrap, and the ability to *win contracts for high-value parts that competitors cannot produce*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not write only "advantages" in generic terms** — examiners expect you to *link each advantage to the underlying physical mechanism* (e.g., zero force → because there is no tool contact). Marks are specifically awarded for the **why**, not the **what**.
> 2. **Do not omit engineering examples** — a 14-mark question without concrete examples (turbine blades, stents, dies) is penalised 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Do not confuse "advantages" with "limitations"** — a common error is to list the *disadvantages* of NTM (high capital cost, slow MRR in some processes) when the question asks for advantages. Re-read the question stem.
> 4. **In numerical/diagram questions** — if the question includes "with neat sketch" or "with diagram", failing to include one costs at least 1–2 marks. Use the Mermaid/ASCII schematics provided in §4 as a template.
> 5. **Avoid "more" and "less"** as engineering language — use quantitative terms ("MRR doubles when current doubles", "TWR is 2 % for graphite vs 30 % for steel electrode"). Examiners reward precision.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **last-15-minutes revision sheet** before the KTU exam hall. Read it twice.

- ✅ The topic **"Advantages"** in Module 2 refers to advantages of **Non-Traditional / Unconventional Manufacturing Processes** over **conventional chip-removal processes**.
- ✅ The **single most important reason** for these advantages is the **replacement of a mechanical cutting tool by a controlled energy form** — making the workpiece's mechanical hardness irrelevant.
- ✅ **Five root axioms** generate all advantages: Energy Decoupling, Zero Force, Localised Energy, Low Tool Wear, and Material Universality.
- ✅ **20 enumerated advantages** across six categories — Material, Mechanical, Geometric, Surface, Economic, Strategic (see §2.2).
- ✅ **MRR in EDM:** $\text{MRR} = K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{\text{on}} \cdot f$ (linear in current, pulse-time, frequency).
- ✅ **TWR formula:** $\text{TWR} = \dfrac{V_t}{V_w} \times 100\% = \dfrac{K_t}{K_w} \times 100\%$. Lower is better; graphite ≈ 1–10 %, copper ≈ 5–15 %, brass ≈ 15–25 %, steel-on-steel ≈ 30–50 %.
- ✅ **Power density** is the master differentiator: NTM reaches $10^{5}$–$10^{9} \; \text{W/mm}^{2}$, conventional stays at $10^{3}$–$10^{5} \; \text{W/mm}^{2}$.
- ✅ **Benchmark values to memorise:** Ra 0.1–1.6 µm (EDM), micro-holes 0.05 mm (LBM), tolerance ±0.005 mm (EDM), HAZ 5–50 µm (LBM/EBM), overcut 0.01–0.5 mm (EDM/ECM).
- ✅ **Examples for each advantage:** aero-engine blades (hardness), forging dies (complex cavities), stents (no burr), fuel injectors (micro-holes), PCB vias (automation), honeycomb panels (WJM).
- ✅ **Avoid the common pitfalls** flagged in the Examiner's Warning — always link advantage to physics, always give examples, never confuse advantages with limitations, always include a diagram when asked.
- ✅ **In Part B (14 marks)** — structure your answer as: *Introduction (2 marks) → Axioms / physical basis (4 marks) → Enumerated advantages with examples (6 marks) → Conclusion linking to industrial relevance (2 marks)*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Applications of Non-Traditional Machining (NTM) Processes

In the context of **PEIET521 – Advanced Manufacturing Processes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, **Module 2** focuses on the working principles, mechanism, and process parameters of major non-traditional machining (NTM) processes. The specific sub-topic **"Applications"** deals with the *industrial, commercial, and research-oriented use-cases* of these processes, mapping each unique material-removal mechanism to the geometries, materials, and tolerances for which it is most economically and technically suited.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Applications of non-traditional machining processes refer to the systematic selection and deployment of energy-based, chemical, electrochemical, and hybrid material-removal techniques to manufacture components from advanced engineering materials (superalloys, ceramics, composites, hardened tool steels) and to produce complex geometries (micro-holes, non-circular profiles, 3D cavities) that are either impossible or uneconomical to produce by conventional chip-removal processes.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a sculptor has been given a block of **tempered glass** (extremely hard and brittle) and asked to carve a delicate flower with thin petals. Traditional tools (chisel and hammer) will crack the glass. What options does the sculptor have?

- **Sandblasting** (Abrasive Jet) — grinds away the glass gently.
- **Acid etching** (Chemical Machining) — chemically dissolves selected regions.
- **Concentrated sunlight through a lens** (Laser Beam Machining) — melts/vaporises tiny precise regions.
- **A vibrating needle with sand slurry** (Ultrasonic Machining) — hammers the surface into dust.

Each method is selected based on the **material**, the **required geometry**, the **tolerances**, and the **production volume**. Similarly, in advanced manufacturing, the engineer must match the *capability profile* of each NTM process to the *manufacturing requirement* of the part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Selection of an appropriate NTM process depends on four key decision variables: **(1) Workpiece Material**, **(2) Shape Complexity**, **(3) Accuracy / Surface Finish (Ra in μm)**, and **(4) Production Volume.** A correctly chosen NTM process can reduce machining time by up to **80%** and tool wear cost by nearly **100%** for hard/brittle materials.

### 1.3 Scope of "Applications" in Module 2

The principal processes whose applications are examined in Module 2 are:

| Acronym | Process Name | Energy / Mechanism Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **AJM** | Abrasive Jet Machining | Mechanical (Kinetic energy of abrasive grains) |
| **WJM** | Water Jet Machining | Mechanical (High-pressure water + abrasive) |
| **USM** | Ultrasonic Machining | Mechanical (Vibration + abrasive slurry) |
| **CHM** | Chemical Machining | Chemical (Etchant reaction) |
| **ECM** | Electrochemical Machining | Electrochemical (Faraday's laws) |
| **EDM** | Electrical Discharge Machining | Thermal-Electrical (Spark erosion) |
| **LBM** | Laser Beam Machining | Thermal (Photon energy) |
| **EBM** | Electron Beam Machining | Thermal (Kinetic energy of electrons) |
| **PAM** | Plasma Arc Machining | Thermal (Ionised gas plasma) |

The applications discussed in the rest of these notes are grouped by **industrial sector** (Aerospace, Automotive, Electronics, Medical, Die & Mold, Defence) and by **manufacturing function** (Drilling, Cutting, Shaping, Micro-machining, Surface treatment).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process-wise Applications — Theory and Decision Logic

### 2.1.1 Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)

**Operating Principle:** High-velocity (≈ 200 m/s) stream of abrasive particles (Al₂O₃, SiC, glass beads) carried by a compressed gas (air / N₂ / CO₂) impinges on the workpiece and erodes material by micro-chipping.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- The process is **cold-cutting** (no thermal damage to workpiece).
- Suitable for **brittle materials** (glass, ceramics, quartz, germanium, silicon, graphite).
- Used for **cleaning** surfaces (removal of paint, rust, scale) without affecting substrate.
- **Micro-machining** of thin, fragile wafers in semiconductor industry.

**Typical Industries:** Optical lens manufacturing, semiconductor wafer dicing, aircraft engine overhaul (cleaning turbine blades), art & decoration on glass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Removal Rate (MRR) for AJM:**
> $$MRR_{AJM} = K \cdot m_a \cdot V_a^2$$
> Where $m_a$ = mass flow rate of abrasive (g/s), $V_a$ = velocity of abrasive (m/s), $K$ = process constant. The MRR is *proportional to the square of the abrasive velocity*, making nozzle pressure the most critical parameter.

---

### 2.1.2 Water Jet Machining (WJM) / Abrasive Water Jet (AWJ)

**Operating Principle:** A high-pressure (up to **400 MPa**) jet of water, optionally mixed with abrasive (garnet), is forced through a small orifice (≈ 0.1–0.4 mm) to cut materials.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- **No Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** — critical for titanium, Inconel, and composites used in aerospace.
- Capable of cutting **thick plates** (steel up to 100 mm, aluminium up to 150 mm).
- Can cut **stacked / laminated materials** (e.g., laminated PCB, sandwich panels).
- Easily programmable for **2D complex contours** without specialised tooling.

**Typical Industries:** Aerospace (cutting titanium fuselage panels, CFRP composites), shipbuilding (steel plate cutting), stone & marble industry, automotive prototyping, food industry (frozen food cutting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nozzle Pressure vs. Kerf Width:**
> The effective cutting pressure for AWJ typically lies between **200–400 MPa**. The kerf width is approximately **1.0 to 1.5 times the orifice diameter**. Lower pressure gives wider kerf and tapered cut (diverging taper angle ≈ 0.5°–2°).

---

### 2.1.3 Ultrasonic Machining (USM)

**Operating Principle:** A tool (sonotrode) vibrating at **20–40 kHz** with an amplitude of 15–50 μm drives abrasive slurry (B₄C or SiC) against the workpiece. Material is removed by **hammering action** of abrasive particles.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- Ideal for **non-conductive, brittle materials** — glass, quartz, germanium, silicon, ceramics, PCD, ferrites.
- **High dimensional accuracy** (± 0.025 mm) and **excellent surface finish** (Ra 0.2–0.8 μm).
- **Round and non-round holes** (including square, oval, irregular profiles) can be produced.
- Used for **die-making**, **wire-drawing dies**, and **engraving on hard stones**.

**Typical Industries:** Electronics (drilling holes in semiconductor wafers, ferrite cores), optics (lens and prism shaping), jewellery, dental laboratory, aerospace (machining ceramic matrix composites).

> [!NOTE]
> **MRR for USM:**
> $$MRR_{USM} = C \cdot F_a \cdot f \cdot d$$
> Where $F_a$ = static feed force, $f$ = vibration frequency, $d$ = mean abrasive diameter, $C$ = process constant. MRR is *directly proportional* to vibration frequency and feed force.

---

### 2.1.4 Chemical Machining (CHM) / Chemical Milling

**Operating Principle:** Selective dissolution of workpiece material by a chemical reagent (etchant). Material covered by a **maskant** is protected, while exposed areas are chemically dissolved.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- **No mechanical or thermal stress** on the workpiece.
- Used for **lightweight pocket machining** in aircraft skin panels (weight saving of 30–60% over solid panels).
- Produces **shallow cavities, contours, and patterns** on large sheets.
- Etching of **printed circuit boards (PCBs)**, nameplates, decorative panels.
- Manufacture of **thin foils** and **microfluidic devices**.

**Typical Industries:** Aerospace (wing-skin milling, integrally stiffened panels), electronics (PCB fabrication), decorative arts, instrumentation (thin metal shims).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Etch Rate (CHM) — Vertical Penetration:**
> $$R = \frac{T}{t}$$
> Where $R$ = etch rate (mm/min), $T$ = depth of etch (mm), $t$ = time of immersion (min). Etch factor = $R_{\text{vertical}} / R_{\text{lateral}}$ — a *high etch factor* gives vertical side walls and is desired for accurate dimensional reproduction.

---

### 2.1.5 Electrochemical Machining (ECM)

**Operating Principle:** Controlled anodic dissolution of the workpiece (anode) in an electrolyte (NaCl, NaNO₃) under an applied DC voltage (5–30 V) and high current (100–10000 A). The tool (cathode) does **not** touch the workpiece.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- Excellent for **superalloys and hardened steels** (Inconel, Waspaloy, Nimonic, Ti-6Al-4V) used in gas turbine and rocket engine components.
- **No tool wear**, **no thermal damage**, **no residual stress**.
- Used to produce **complex 3D cavities** in turbine blades (cooling holes, airfoil profiles), blisks, and integrally bladed rotors (IBRs).
- **Deburring** of cross-drilled holes and internal passages.
- **Die and mold making** for forging and die-casting.

**Typical Industries:** Aerospace (turbine blade machining, blisks), automotive (gear and shaft deburring), medical implants (orthopaedic implants), defence (gun barrel rifling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Faraday's Law governing ECM:**
> $$V = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot A_w}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$
> Where $V$ = volume removed (m³), $\eta$ = current efficiency, $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $A_w$ = atomic weight (kg/mol), $Z$ = valency, $F$ = Faraday constant ≈ **96485 C/mol**, $\rho$ = density (kg/m³).

---

### 2.1.6 Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)

**Operating Principle:** Material is removed by **controlled sparks** generated between the tool (electrode) and workpiece submerged in a dielectric fluid (kerosene, EDM oil). Each spark generates a temperature of **8000–12000 °C**, vaporising and melting a small volume of material.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- Hardness of workpiece is **irrelevant** — any electrically conductive material can be machined irrespective of its hardness (> 60 HRC easily).
- Used to produce **complex dies and molds** (e.g., forging dies, plastic injection molds, stamping dies).
- **Wire-EDM (WEDM)** is used for **2D profile cutting** of punches, dies, gears, and intricate contours.
- **Small / micro-holes** (diameter 0.05–1.0 mm) in fuel injectors, spinnerets, and aerospace turbine cooling holes.
- **Hard-to-machine alloys** — tungsten carbide, stellite, Inconel, cemented carbides.

**Typical Industries:** Tool and die making, aerospace, automotive (gear and spline production), medical (orthopaedic instruments), electronics (lead frames, micro-holes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MRR for EDM:**
> $$MRR_{EDM} = K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$$
> Where $I_p$ = peak current, $T_{on}$ = pulse-on time, $K$ = constant. MRR is *directly proportional* to the energy per spark. Increasing $T_{on}$ increases MRR but worsens surface finish (higher Ra).

---

### 2.1.7 Laser Beam Machining (LBM)

**Operating Principle:** A highly focused, high-power density laser beam (10⁶–10⁹ W/mm²) melts and vaporises the workpiece. The most common lasers in industry: **CO₂ (10.6 μm)** and **Nd:YAG / Fiber (1.06 μm)**.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- **Non-contact**, no tool wear, no workpiece clamping force.
- Cuts **metals, non-metals, ceramics, polymers, composites, wood, leather** — extremely versatile.
- **Micro-machining** of micro-holes (fuel injector nozzles, turbine cooling holes, watch components).
- **Welding, drilling, cutting, marking, engraving, and 3D printing (SLM/DMLS)** applications.
- **Medical**: laser surgery, stents fabrication, ophthalmology (LASIK).
- Rapid prototyping directly from CAD (SLA, SLS, DMLS).

**Typical Industries:** Automotive (laser welding of car bodies, gear cutting), aerospace (drilling cooling holes in turbine blades), medical (stents, implants), electronics (PCB drilling), packaging (marking and coding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Density Governing Equation:**
> $$q = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$
> Where $q$ = power density (W/mm²), $P$ = laser power (W), $d$ = focused beam diameter (mm). To *vaporise* a material, the power density must exceed **10⁶ W/mm²**.

---

### 2.1.8 Electron Beam Machining (EBM)

**Operating Principle:** A focused beam of high-velocity electrons (accelerated at 50–200 kV) strikes the workpiece in a vacuum chamber, converting kinetic energy into intense heat to melt/vaporise material.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- **Sub-zero heat-affected zone** due to extremely localised heating (beam spot ≈ 10–100 μm).
- **Welding in vacuum** — used for critical aerospace and nuclear components (jet engine blades, nuclear reactor parts).
- **Drilling micro-holes** in turbine blades (cooling holes of diameter 0.1–0.5 mm) with high aspect ratios.
- Used in **Additive Manufacturing (EBM)** of titanium orthopaedic implants.

**Typical Industries:** Aerospace (vacuum welding of critical components), nuclear (welding of reactor components), medical (EBM 3D-printed titanium implants), automotive (gear welding).

---

### 2.1.9 Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)

**Operating Principle:** An electric arc ionises a gas (Ar, N₂, H₂ mixture) producing a high-temperature plasma jet (≈ 16,000–30,000 °C) that melts and displaces the workpiece material.

**Why and How it is Applied:**
- **High-speed cutting of thick metal plates** (stainless steel, aluminium, mild steel up to 150 mm).
- **Surfacing and hard-facing** for wear-resistant coatings on valve bodies, mining equipment.
- **Profile cutting** of structural steel in shipbuilding and heavy engineering.
- Lower cost and faster than laser for thick-section cutting of carbon steel.

**Typical Industries:** Shipbuilding, structural steel fabrication, mining & earth-moving equipment, foundries, power plant maintenance.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Process | Key Formula | Parameters & Units | Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AJM** | $MRR = K \cdot m_a \cdot V_a^2$ | $m_a$ in g/s, $V_a$ in m/s | $V_a$ = 150–250 m/s |
| 2 | **USM** | $MRR = C \cdot F_a \cdot f \cdot d$ | $F_a$ in N, $f$ in Hz, $d$ in μm | $f$ = 20–40 kHz, $d$ = 15–150 μm |
| 3 | **WJM** | $P_{jet} = Q \cdot \Delta P$ | $Q$ = flow (L/min), $\Delta P$ in MPa | $P_{jet}$ up to 400 MPa |
| 4 | **CHM** | $R = T/t$ | $T$ in mm, $t$ in min | Etch rate 0.025 mm/min (Al) |
| 5 | **ECM** | $V = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot A_w}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $I$ in A, $t$ in s, $F$ = 96485 C/mol | $F$ = Faraday constant |
| 6 | **ECM (current density form)** | $MRR = \dfrac{\eta \cdot J \cdot A_w}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $J$ in A/m² | $J$ = 30–200 A/cm² |
| 7 | **EDM** | $MRR = K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$ | $I_p$ in A, $T_{on}$ in μs | $I_p$ = 5–500 A |
| 8 | **EDM (wear ratio)** | $\theta = V_w / V_e$ | $V_w$ = wear vol, $V_e$ = electrode vol | $\theta$ < 1% ideal |
| 9 | **LBM** | $q = 4P / (\pi d^2)$ | $P$ in W, $d$ in mm | $q$ > 10⁶ W/mm² for vaporisation |
| 10 | **EBM** | $KE = e \cdot V$ | $V$ in volts | $V$ = 50–200 kV |
| 11 | **PAM** | $Q = k \cdot I$ (heat input) | $k$ = 0.24 (Ar), $I$ in A | $T$ ≈ 16,000–30,000 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, when the question says *"list the applications of..."*, the examiner awards **2 marks** for industrial sector identification and **1 mark** for the specific component / geometry (e.g., *turbine blade cooling holes*). Always write both.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Process Selection

The applications of NTM processes are best understood as a **process capability matrix**:

| Process | Best for Material | Best for Geometry | Best for Surface Finish | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AJM** | Brittle, thin | Cleaning, frosting | Moderate (Ra 0.4–1.6 μm) | Low MRR |
| **WJM** | Any | 2D contour cutting | Good | Taper on thick cut |
| **USM** | Brittle, non-conductive | Round/non-round holes | Excellent (Ra 0.2–0.8 μm) | < 10 mm depth |
| **CHM** | Any chemically reactive | Shallow pockets, PCB | Poor (Ra 1.6–3.2 μm) | Hazardous chemicals |
| **ECM** | Hard, conductive | 3D cavities, blisks | Good (Ra 0.2–1.6 μm) | Electrolyte handling |
| **EDM** | Any conductive | Dies, micro-holes | Excellent (Ra 0.1–0.8 μm) | Slow, electrode wear |
| **LBM** | Almost all | Micro-holes, 2D/3D | Good (Ra 0.8–3.2 μm) | High capital cost |
| **EBM** | All metals, vacuum only | Micro-holes, welding | Excellent | Vacuum chamber needed |
| **PAM** | Conductive metals | Thick-plate cutting | Moderate | Limited to conductive |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: ECM Material Removal Rate (Faraday's Law)

Theoretical removal rate in ECM is governed by **Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis**.

**Given data (typical KTU problem):**
- Workpiece: Iron (Fe), atomic weight $A_w = 55.85$ g/mol, valency $Z = 2$, density $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³
- Current $I = 1000$ A
- Time $t = 60$ s
- Current efficiency $\eta = 0.90$ (90%)

**Step 1 — Write Faraday's mass equation:**

$$m = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot A_w}{Z \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (note the unit alignment carefully):**

$$m = \frac{0.90 \times 1000 \times 60 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96485} \text{ grams}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$0.90 \times 1000 \times 60 \times 55.85 = 3{,}015{,}900$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$2 \times 96485 = 192{,}970$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass removed:**

$$m = \frac{3{,}015{,}900}{192{,}970} \approx 15.63 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6 — Convert mass to volume removed using density:**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{15.63}{7.87} \approx 1.986 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 7 — Express MRR per unit time:**

$$MRR = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{1.986}{60} \approx 0.0331 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to more practical units:**

$$MRR \approx 0.0331 \times 60 = 1.986 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \approx 1986 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For an iron workpiece under the given conditions, ECM removes roughly **2 cm³ of material per minute**. This is the theoretical maximum; real-world values are 10–25% lower due to hydrogen evolution, side-gap losses, and electrolyte heating.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: AJM Material Removal Rate

**Given data:**
- Abrasive: Al₂O₃, mass flow rate $m_a = 5$ g/s
- Nozzle pressure $P = 0.6$ MPa
- Nozzle diameter $D = 3$ mm
- Process constant $K = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ mm³·s/g (calibrated for Al₂O₃ on glass)
- Density of abrasive $\rho_a = 3.95$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Estimate the abrasive velocity from nozzle exit conditions:**

$$V_a = \sqrt{\frac{2 P}{\rho_a}}$$

Substituting:

$$V_a = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.6 \times 10^6}{3950}} = \sqrt{303.8} \approx 17.43 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the MRR equation:**

$$MRR_{AJM} = K \cdot m_a \cdot V_a^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$MRR_{AJM} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times (17.43)^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the square:**

$$(17.43)^2 \approx 303.8$$

**Step 5 — Compute MRR:**

$$MRR_{AJM} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 303.8 = 0.1823 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Express in practical units:**

$$MRR_{AJM} \approx 0.1823 \times 60 \approx 10.94 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** Because $MRR \propto V_a^2$, a *small* 10% increase in nozzle pressure can lead to nearly a *20% increase* in MRR. This quadratic dependence is why nozzle pressure is the **most sensitive control variable** in AJM.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: EDM Theoretical MRR

**Given data:**
- Peak current $I_p = 50$ A
- Pulse-on time $T_{on} = 100$ μs
- Process constant for steel $K_{steel} = 9 \times 10^{-3}$ mm³/(A·μs)

**Step 1 — Apply the EDM MRR equation:**

$$MRR_{EDM} = K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$MRR_{EDM} = 9 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 100 = 45 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Compute MRR per hour (for production planning):**

$$MRR_{EDM} = 45 \times 60 = 2700 \text{ mm}^3/\text{h} = 2.7 \text{ cm}^3/\text{h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Doubling $T_{on}$ doubles MRR but **worsens surface roughness** by roughly $\sqrt{2}$ and increases the **recast layer (heat-affected zone)** depth. EDM parameter selection is therefore a balance between *productivity*, *finish*, and *integrity*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection by Industry

| Industry Component / Requirement | Recommended NTM Process | Reason for Selection |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling holes in gas turbine blade (Inconel) | **EDM (Die-sink or Micro-EDM)** | Hardness independent; high aspect ratio |
| Micro-holes in fuel injector nozzle | **LBM or EDM** | Small diameter (≈ 0.1–0.3 mm) |
| Airfoil profile in turbine blade | **ECM** | 3D complex cavity, no HAZ |
| Composite panel cutting (CFRP) | **AWJ** | No HAZ, no delamination |
| Decorative frosting on glass | **AJM** | Gentle, cold process |
| PCB etching | **CHM** | Selective chemical dissolution |
| Wire-drawing die for tungsten | **USM** | Hard brittle material, accurate hole |
| Die cavity for forging (H13 steel) | **EDM + WEDM** | Hardness independent, complex cavity |
| Vacuum welding of reactor parts | **EBM** | Clean vacuum environment |
| Thick steel plate (100 mm) cutting | **PAM or AWJ** | High speed, thick sections |
| Watch gear (jewellery) micro-feature | **LBM** | Sub-mm precision |
| Medical stent (nitinol) cutting | **LBM / WEDM** | Bio-compatible material, micro-feature |
| Titanium hip implant (3D printed) | **EBM (Additive)** | Biocompatible, complex porous structure |
| Sheet metal pocket (aircraft skin) | **CHM** | Lightweight, no stress |
| 3D cavity in blisk (IBR) | **ECM** | Integral blisk, no stress |
| Engraving on granite / marble | **AJM or USM** | Hard brittle material |
| Heat-exchanger tube deburring | **ECM** | Internal passages, no tool access |
| Microfluidic chip fabrication | **LBM / CHM** | Micro-channel geometry |
| Stamping die for electrical steel | **Wire-EDM** | 2D profile, hard material |
| Optical lens (glass) shaping | **USM** | Brittle, smooth finish |

## 3.5 Comparative Decision Flowchart for Process Selection

The selection logic, in plain language, is:

1. **Is the material electrically conductive and very hard (HRC > 55)?**
   - YES, complex 3D cavity → **ECM**
   - YES, 2D profile or micro-hole → **EDM / WEDM**
   - NO (non-conductive) → Go to step 2.

2. **Is the material brittle (glass, ceramic, semiconductor)?**
   - YES, hole or cavity with smooth finish → **USM**
   - YES, surface cleaning or frosting → **AJM**
   - NO → Go to step 3.

3. **Is the requirement a large 2D contour cut in a plate?**
   - YES, no HAZ allowed → **AWJ**
   - YES, conductive and thick → **PAM**

4. **Is the requirement a shallow pocket on a sheet metal?**
   - YES → **CHM**

5. **Is the requirement a micro-feature (< 0.5 mm) on any material?**
   - YES, in vacuum / nuclear-grade → **EBM**
   - YES, on shop floor → **LBM**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
