# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Past Year Style Question Bank

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three industrial applications of Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM). State the material types for which AJM is most suited.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) AJM is used for **cleaning surfaces** of aircraft engine turbine blades (removal of carbon deposits and corrosion) without damaging the base superalloy.
- (1 Mark) AJM is used for **frosting and decorative engraving on glass** articles and for **dicing thin semiconductor wafers** (silicon, germanium) in the electronics industry.
- (1 Mark) AJM is most suited for **hard, brittle, non-metallic materials** such as glass, ceramics, quartz, and ferrites — materials that cannot be machined by conventional chip-removal processes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Mention any three specific applications of Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM) in the modern manufacturing industry.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) EDM is extensively used for **die and mold making** in forging, stamping, and plastic injection — including the manufacture of complex forging dies made of H13 / D2 tool steel (hardness 58–62 HRC).
- (1 Mark) **Wire-EDM (WEDM)** is used for producing **punches, profile cutters, gears, and intricate 2D contours** in hardened tool steels.
- (1 Mark) EDM (especially micro-EDM and die-sink EDM) is used to drill **micro-cooling holes (diameter 0.1–0.5 mm)** in gas turbine blades and fuel injector nozzles.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose marks by writing **"EDM is used for hard materials"** without specifying the geometry, industry, and reason.
> - Always pair every application with **"used in _____, because _____"** to earn the *reasoning* mark.
> - In numerical problems, marks are deducted for *unit errors* (e.g., mixing mm³/s with cm³/min). Always **state units at every step**.
> - Do not confuse **MRR formulas** between processes (e.g., using EDM's $K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$ for ECM).

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose A or B**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of **Electrochemical Machining (ECM)**. List any **five specific industrial applications** of ECM with materials and components.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A workpiece of **Inconel 718** is to be machined by ECM. Given: atomic weight $A_w = 58.7$ g/mol (effectively for Ni), valency $Z = 2$, density $\rho = 8.19$ g/cm³, current $I = 2000$ A, time $t = 90$ s, current efficiency $\eta = 0.95$. Calculate the theoretical mass removed and volume removed.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle of ECM (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
ECM is based on **Faraday's Law of Electrolysis**. The workpiece is connected as the **anode (+)** and the tool is connected as the **cathode (−)** of a DC power supply. A salt-based electrolyte (NaCl or NaNO₃) is forced through the small inter-electrode gap (0.1–0.5 mm) at high velocity (5–50 m/s). When DC voltage (5–30 V) is applied, the anodic metal dissolves into the electrolyte as metal ions, producing a mirror-image cavity of the tool shape in the workpiece.

**Key Reactions (1 Mark):**
At anode (workpiece): $M \rightarrow M^{Z+} + Ze^{-}$
At cathode (tool): $2H^+ + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow$

**Important Features (1 Mark):**
- Tool does not physically contact the workpiece → no tool wear, no mechanical stress.
- High current (1000–10000 A) and low voltage (5–30 V) are used.
- Electrolyte removes heat, debris, and hydrogen bubbles.

**Five Industrial Applications of ECM (3 Marks, distribute 0.5–1 Mark each):**
- (i) Machining of **complex 3D cooling passages and airfoil profiles in gas turbine blades** (Inconel, Waspaloy) — the dominant application in the aerospace industry.
- (ii) **Electrochemical deburring** of cross-drilled holes and internal passages in hydraulic manifolds and automotive gearbox components.
- (iii) **Die-sinking for forging and die-casting dies** made of hardened die steels.
- (iv) Machining of **integrally bladed rotors (IBRs / blisks)** in jet engines — eliminating blade-disc assembly.
- (v) **Profiling and shaping of medical implants** (orthopaedic knee and hip implants) from titanium and cobalt-chromium alloys.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical on ECM (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the mass-removal equation (1 Mark):**

$$m = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot A_w}{Z \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (2 Marks):**

$$m = \frac{0.95 \times 2000 \times 90 \times 58.7}{2 \times 96485}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator (1 Mark):**

$$0.95 \times 2000 \times 90 \times 58.7 = 10{,}037{,}700$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator (1 Mark):**

$$2 \times 96485 = 192{,}970$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass removed (1 Mark):**

$$m = \frac{10{,}037{,}700}{192{,}970} \approx 52.0 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6 — Compute volume removed (1 Mark):**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{52.0}{8.19} \approx 6.35 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Final Answer (boxed for KTU valuation):** Mass removed ≈ **52.0 g**; Volume removed ≈ **6.35 cm³**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Describe the working principle of **Ultrasonic Machining (USM)**. List **five specific applications** of USM in modern industry, and state the material types for which USM is preferred over other NTM processes.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A USM operation is performed on a **glass plate** using boron carbide (B₄C) abrasive. The vibration frequency is 25 kHz, the static feed force is 6 N, the mean abrasive diameter is 80 μm, and the calibrated process constant is $C = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mm³/(N·Hz·μm). Compute the **theoretical MRR** and the **time required to machine a circular hole of 10 mm diameter and 5 mm depth** in the glass. (Assume 100% material efficiency.)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — USM Working Principle and Applications (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
In USM, a **sonotrode (tool holder)** vibrates at ultrasonic frequency (20–40 kHz) with low amplitude (15–50 μm). Abrasive slurry (B₄C or SiC particles suspended in water) is continuously fed into the gap between the tool and the workpiece. The vibrating tool forces the abrasive particles to **hammer the workpiece surface**, causing **micro-chipping** and material removal. Material is removed only in regions directly under the tool, producing a cavity that is a mirror image of the tool cross-section.

**Key Points (1 Mark):**
- The process is purely **mechanical** — no chemical or thermal action.
- The workpiece must be **brittle** — soft ductile materials absorb the impact without chipping.
- Abrasive particles are *consumed* and must be continuously replenished.

**Material Suitability (1 Mark):**
USM is preferred for **hard, brittle, non-conductive materials** such as glass, quartz, germanium, silicon, ferrites, PCD, ceramics, and graphite — for which EDM is unsuitable (non-conductive) and conventional machining causes fracture.

**Five Applications (3 Marks):**
- (i) **Drilling round and non-round holes** in glass and ceramics for optical and electronic components.
- (ii) **Die-making** for wire-drawing of tungsten and molybdenum wires.
- (iii) **Engraving on hard stones** and jewellery (signet rings, cameos).
- (iv) **Machining of ferrite cores, piezoelectric ceramics, and semiconductor wafers** in the electronics industry.
- (v) **Dental laboratory applications** — machining of ceramic dental crowns and prostheses.

---

**Part (b) — USM Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given values (1 Mark):**
- $f = 25{,}000$ Hz, $F_a = 6$ N, $d = 80$ μm, $C = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mm³/(N·Hz·μm).

**Step 2 — Apply the MRR formula (1 Mark):**

$$MRR_{USM} = C \cdot F_a \cdot f \cdot d$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (1 Mark):**

$$MRR_{USM} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 6 \times 25{,}000 \times 80$$

**Step 4 — Compute step by step (1 Mark):**

$$4.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 6 = 0.024$$
$$0.024 \times 25{,}000 = 600$$
$$600 \times 80 = 48{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to practical units (1 Mark):**

$$MRR = 48{,}000 \times 60 = 2{,}880{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 2.88 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 6 — Compute volume of the hole (1 Mark):**

$$V_{hole} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot h = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^2 \times 5 \approx 392.7 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 7 — Compute machining time (1 Mark):**

$$t = \frac{V_{hole}}{MRR} = \frac{392.7}{48{,}000} \approx 0.00818 \text{ s}$$

(Note: In practice, MRR is much lower due to slurry inefficiencies and tool wear; the value above is the *theoretical* maximum.)

**Final Answer:** Theoretical MRR ≈ **48,000 mm³/s (≈ 2.88 × 10⁶ mm³/min)**; theoretical machining time ≈ **0.0082 s**.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications of NTM Processes (Module 2)**

- **AJM** → Cleaning, frosting, brittle-material micro-machining. Best for **glass, ceramics, semiconductors**.
- **WJM / AWJ** → 2D contour cutting of **CFRP, titanium, thick plates**; no HAZ; no material restriction.
- **USM** → Holes, cavities, engraving on **hard, brittle, non-conductive** materials (glass, ceramics, PCD).
- **CHM** → **Aerospace skin milling** (lightweight pockets), **PCB etching**, nameplate manufacture.
- **ECM** → **Turbine blade airfoils, blisks, IBRs**, die-sinking, deburring; **no tool wear, no HAZ**.
- **EDM** → **Dies, molds, micro-holes, punches, wire-EDM profiles**; any conductive material regardless of hardness.
- **LBM** → **Micro-hole drilling (fuel injectors, turbine cooling)**, cutting, welding, marking, additive manufacturing.
- **EBM** → **Vacuum welding (nuclear, aerospace)**, micro-holes, 3D printing of titanium implants.
- **PAM** → **Thick plate cutting (steel > 25 mm)**, shipbuilding, structural steel, hard-facing.

### Memory Aid — "ABCD-P-L-E" Rule of Thumb

| Letter | Process | Best Material Class |
|---|---|---|
| A | AJM | Brittle, non-metallic |
| B (USM) | Ultrasonic | Brittle, non-conductive |
| C (CHM) | Chemical | Reactive metals, PCBs |
| D (EDM) | Discharge | Conductive, hardened |
| P | PAM | Conductive, thick |
| L | LBM | Universal |
| E | EBM / ECM | ECM: conductive / EBM: vacuum-grade |

### Key Constants to Memorise

- **Faraday constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- **Plasma temperature** ≈ 16,000–30,000 °C.
- **Spark temperature in EDM** ≈ 8,000–12,000 °C.
- **USM frequency** = 20–40 kHz.
- **AWJ pressure** up to **400 MPa**.
- **LBM power density threshold for vaporisation** > 10⁶ W/mm².

### Most-Mistaken Confusions (Avoid These!)

1. **EDM works on conductive materials only** — not on glass, ceramics, or plastics.
2. **ECM leaves no HAZ** — there is no spark and no temperature rise in the workpiece.
3. **WJM is *not* thermal** — it uses kinetic energy, so no HAZ.
4. **LBM can machine *almost* everything** — but is most economical for thin sections and micro-features.
5. **CHM does not produce sharp edges** — etch factor < 1 means tapered side walls.
6. **USM is *not* for ductile metals** — the abrasive just burnishes the surface without chipping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** For any "Applications of ___" question, structure your answer as **Process → Mechanism → Industry Sector → Specific Component → Reason for Selection.** This 5-point structure consistently fetches full marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Discharge Machining (EDM) or Spark Erosion Machining Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electric Discharge Machining (EDM) or Spark Erosion Machining Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Electric Discharge Machining (EDM) – Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electric Discharge Machining (EDM)**, also called **Spark Erosion Machining (SEM)** or **Die-Sinking EDM**, is a non-traditional, **thermal-electrical material removal process** in which the metal is removed from the workpiece by a series of **rapid, repetitive electrical discharges (sparks)** occurring in the small gap between the tool (electrode) and the workpiece, both immersed in a **dielectric fluid**.

The process is governed by **controlled spark erosion** — material is melted and vaporized by the high temperature (≈ **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C**) of the spark channel, and the molten debris is flushed away by the dielectric. There is **no direct mechanical contact** between the tool and the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition – For Exam Hall:**
> *“EDM is a controlled metal-removal process in which electrical energy in the form of a series of discrete sparks is used to erode material from the electrically conductive workpiece, with the tool and workpiece separated by a small gap filled with a dielectric fluid.”*

**Standardized IS / ISO Reference Symbol for EDM:** The process is classified under **thermal material-removal processes** by ASTM / ISO 3685 and by the KTU manufacturing process taxonomy under the **non-conventional / advanced manufacturing** group.

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Spark Temperature | **8,000 – 12,000 °C** |
| Spark Duration (Pulse on-time) | **1 µs – 2000 µs** |
| Gap (Spark Gap) | **0.01 – 0.5 mm** |
| Dielectric Strength Required | **10 – 100 kV/mm** |
| Workpiece Hardness Limit | None (irrelevant — purely thermal) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World "Feel" of EDM)

Imagine a **tiny, controlled lightning bolt** firing thousands of times per second between a metal pencil (the electrode) and a metal block (the workpiece) — both are submerged in a special oil bath (dielectric) so the lightning can't take a wrong path. Every flash of lightning is so hot (hotter than the sun's surface) that it **vaporizes a microscopic crater** of metal. The pencil never *touches* the block — it is always held a hair's breadth away, just close enough for the spark to jump. Over time, the pencil "sculpts" a mirror-image cavity in the block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is EDM so important?**
> It can machine **hardened tool steel, tungsten carbide, titanium, Inconel, and even diamond** — materials that would destroy conventional cutting tools. The only requirement is that the workpiece must be **electrically conductive**.

## 1.3 Historical & Syllabus Context

EDM was first developed in the **1940s** by B.R. and N.I. Lazarenko (USSR) while studying the destructive effects of electric sparks on switch contacts. They reversed the phenomenon and turned it into a productive machining process. It is now indispensable for **die-making, aerospace, medical implants, and electronics**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid for viva:**
> *EDM = "Lightning + Oil = Machining."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle – The 5-Stage Spark Mechanism

The EDM process is **NOT continuous** — it is a discrete pulse-by-pulse event. Every single spark follows this exact sequence:

### Stage 1 — Approach & Gap Formation
The servo motor drives the tool (cathode, –) downward towards the workpiece (anode, +) until the **inter-electrode gap** is reduced to a critical distance (typically **0.01 – 0.05 mm**).

### Stage 2 — Dielectric Ionization
The dielectric fluid (e.g., EDM oil, kerosene, deionized water) at this narrow gap breaks down electrically. The normally insulating fluid becomes **plasma** — a channel of ionized atoms and free electrons.

### Stage 3 — Spark Discharge
A controlled, instantaneous current pulse (typically **0.5 A to 400 A**) flows through the plasma channel. The localized temperature soars to **8,000–12,000 °C** in a few microseconds.

### Stage 4 — Material Removal
The intense heat **melts and vaporizes** a tiny volume of the workpiece. Some material also vaporizes from the tool (tool wear). A small hemispherical **crater** is formed on the workpiece surface.

### Stage 5 — Dielectric Flushing & Debris Removal
The pulse is switched off. The plasma collapses, the dielectric de-ionizes, and the high-pressure vapor bubble implodes. Fresh dielectric flows in, **flushing the eroded particles (debris)** out of the gap, ready for the next spark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Cycle Time (T) = Pulse on-time ($t_{on}$) + Pulse off-time ($t_{off}$)**
> The off-time is *crucial* — it allows the dielectric to de-ionize and recover its insulating strength.

## 2.2 Essential Components of an EDM Machine

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pulse Generator / Power Supply** | Generates precisely controlled DC pulses (V and I). Modern machines use **transistorized (ISOPULSE / MOSFET) or RC** circuits for micro-second control. |
| 2 | **Servo Control System** | Maintains a constant **spark gap** by advancing/retracting the tool. Critical for stable sparking. |
| 3 | **Dielectric Reservoir & Pump** | Stores, cools, and circulates dielectric through the work tank. |
| 4 | **Dielectric Filter System** | Removes micro-debris to maintain dielectric cleanliness. |
| 5 | **Tool (Electrode) Holder** | Holds and feeds the tool. |
| 6 | **Work Tank & Worktable** | Immerses the workpiece in dielectric. |
| 7 | **CNC Controller** (modern machines) | Programs the path for die-sinking or wire EDM. |

## 2.3 Process Parameters

The four **primary controllable parameters** (KTU 2024 frequently-tested):

1. **Open Circuit Voltage (V)** — 50 to 380 V
2. **Peak Current (I)** — 0.5 to 400 A
3. **Pulse on-time ($t_{on}$)** — 1 µs to 2000 µs
4. **Pulse off-time ($t_{off}$)** — same range as on-time

**Derived parameters:**

- **Pulse Frequency (f)**: $f = 1 / (t_{on} + t_{off})$
- **Duty Cycle (η)**: $\eta = t_{on} / (t_{on} + t_{off})$
- **Energy per pulse (E_p)**: $E_p = V \times I \times t_{on}$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All marks are awarded in KTU for correct formula substitution with units. Memorize the symbols and units below.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MRR = k \cdot I \cdot t_{on} \cdot f$ | Volumetric material removal rate (mm³/min); $k$ = material constant |
| 2 | $MRR = V_s \cdot f$ | $V_s$ = volume of single spark crater (mm³); $f$ = pulse frequency |
| 3 | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 h$ | Spark crater volume; $d$ = crater diameter, $h$ = crater depth |
| 4 | $E_p = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$ | Energy discharged in one pulse (Joules) |
| 5 | $f = 1 / (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | Pulse frequency in Hz |
| 6 | $\eta = t_{on} / (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | Duty cycle (dimensionless, $\eta < 1$) |
| 7 | $P_{avg} = V \cdot I \cdot \eta$ | Average power supplied (Watts) |
| 8 | $TWR = (V_{tool} / V_{work}) \times 100\%$ | Tool Wear Ratio (TWR); $V_{tool}$ = tool material removed, $V_{work}$ = workpiece material removed |
| 9 | $R_a \approx k_s \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot t_{on}^{0.3}$ | Empirical surface roughness (µm); lower $I$ and $t_{on}$ → better finish |
| 10 | $V_{gap} = k_1 \cdot V + k_2$ | Spark gap width (mm) — empirical, increases with voltage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In the formula table, **never use the vertical bar symbol '|'** for absolute value; use `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the KTU answer-sheet markdown rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Tool & Die** | Sinking cavities in hardened HSS / carbide dies — no tool wear from hardness. |
| **Aerospace** | Machining **titanium and Inconel turbine blades**, cooling holes, combustor liners. |
| **Medical** | Manufacturing **orthopedic implants** (hip, knee) from cobalt-chrome / titanium. |
| **Electronics** | Drilling **micro-holes** in printed circuit boards (down to **0.05 mm**). |
| **Automotive** | EDM of injection molds, gear forging dies. |
| **Molds for Plastics** | Complex 3D cavities with sharp internal corners (impossible by milling). |

## 2.6 Types of EDM (KTU Module 3 – Must-Know Variants)

| Type | Tool Form | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Die-Sinking / Ram / Vertical EDM** | Shaped 3D electrode | Cavities, dies, 3D shapes |
| **(b) Wire EDM (WEDM)** | Continuously moving thin wire (brass, 0.05–0.3 mm) | 2D profile cutting, punches |
| **(c) Small Hole EDM** | Rotating tubular electrode | Cooling holes, start holes in turbine blades |
| **(d) Micro-EDM** | Micro-electrode (≤ 50 µm) | MEMS, micro-fluidics |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) from First Principles

The MRR in EDM is built up from the **mass or volume removed per single spark** multiplied by the **number of sparks per unit time**. We will derive the volumetric MRR by tracking the energy balance of one spark.

### Assumptions

1. The spark energy is fully absorbed by the workpiece (neglecting the energy lost to the dielectric or to radiation).
2. The workpiece material is removed by **melting + vaporization** (latent heat dominates).
3. Each spark produces a hemispherical crater of **diameter $d$** and **depth $h$**.
4. The sparks are uniformly distributed in time.

### Step 1 — Energy Input per Spark

The pulse generator supplies a voltage $V$ and a current $I$ for a duration $t_{on}$. The electrical energy delivered to the spark channel during one pulse is:

$$E_p = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$$

Units: $V$ (V) × $I$ (A) × $t_{on}$ (s) = **Joules (J)**.

### Step 2 — Crater Volume per Spark

A single spark produces a hemispherical crater. The volume of this crater is:

$$V_s = \frac{2}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{2}{3} \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{\pi d^3}{12}$$

Equivalently, approximating the crater as a hemisphere with average depth $h$ and surface diameter $d$:

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot h$$

Both forms are valid; the second is more practical because $d$ and $h$ can be directly measured from micrographs of EDM craters.

### Step 3 — Linking Crater Volume to Pulse Energy

Empirically (and theoretically via thermal balance), the crater volume is proportional to the pulse energy:

$$V_s = k_m \cdot E_p = k_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$$

where $k_m$ is the **material machinability constant** (mm³/J), depending on the workpiece material's density, melting point, latent heat, etc.

**Engineering Note:** This linear relation is approximate — at very high currents, the relation saturates because of plasma shielding, vaporization losses, and debris re-deposition.

### Step 4 — Number of Sparks per Unit Time

The pulse cycle (period) is:

$$T = t_{on} + t_{off}$$

Hence the pulse frequency (number of sparks per second) is:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

### Step 5 — Final Volumetric MRR

Multiply the volume of one spark by the number of sparks per second to obtain the volumetric MRR:

$$MRR = V_s \cdot f$$

Substituting Step 3 and Step 4:

$$MRR = k_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t_{on} \cdot \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

Factor the duty cycle $\eta = t_{on}/(t_{on} + t_{off})$:

$$MRR = k_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t_{on} \cdot f = k_m \cdot E_p \cdot f$$

**Or, equivalently in terms of average power:**

$$MRR = k_m \cdot P_{avg} = k_m \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \eta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU-Ready Form (single line, easy to write in the answer book):**
>
> $$MRR = k \cdot I \cdot t_{on} \cdot f \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/s]}$$
>
> where $k$ absorbs both the material constant and the voltage (since $V$ is approximately constant for a given EDM machine setting).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)

The TWR is a dimensionless quality indicator. For the same period, the tool and the workpiece both lose material.

### Step 1 — Tool Volume Lost per Unit Time

By the same logic as MRR, the **tool wear rate (TWRate)** is:

$$TWRate = k_{tool} \cdot I \cdot t_{on} \cdot f$$

where $k_{tool}$ is a constant for the tool material (graphite, copper, brass, tungsten, etc.).

### Step 2 — Workpiece Volume Lost per Unit Time

$$WRR = k_{work} \cdot I \cdot t_{on} \cdot f$$

### Step 3 — Definition of TWR

By KTU/standard convention:

$$TWR (\%) = \frac{TWRate}{WRR} \times 100\% = \frac{k_{tool}}{k_{work}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - **TWR < 1 %** → Excellent electrode life (typical of **graphite** electrodes on steel).
> - **TWR > 50 %** → Poor choice of electrode material (e.g., copper on tungsten carbide is high wear).
> - For **Wire EDM**, the wire wear is expressed as a *length* rather than volume: $WW = f(t_{on}, V, I, wire \, material)$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on Output (Most Asked KTU 2-Mark Question)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "up arrow ↑ means increases, down arrow ↓ means decreases." Memorize this table for KTU Module 3 short questions.**

| Parameter ↑ | MRR | Surface Roughness $R_a$ | TWR | HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Peak Current (I)** | ↑↑ | ↑ (worse) | ↑ | ↑ (deeper) |
| **Pulse on-time ($t_{on}$)** | ↑↑ | ↑ (worse) | ↑ | ↑ (deeper) |
| **Pulse off-time ($t_{off}$)** | ↓ | (negligible) | ↓ | (negligible) |
| **Open Circuit Voltage (V)** | ↑ | ↑ slightly | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Pulse Frequency (f)** | ↑ | ↓ (better) | ↑ slightly | ↓ (shallower) |
| **Dielectric Flow** | ↑ up to optimum, then ↓ | improves flushing | improves cooling | smaller with good flushing |
| **Electrode Material Hardness** | (no direct effect) | improves wear | ↓ (less wear) | (no direct effect) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style Practice)

**Problem:** In an EDM operation on a steel workpiece, the average energy per pulse is $E_p = 0.6$ J, the pulse on-time is $t_{on} = 200$ μs, the pulse off-time is $t_{off} = 100$ μs, and the material machinability constant for the steel is $k_m = 0.011 \, mm^3/J$. Calculate the **MRR** and the **average power supplied** if the open-circuit voltage is $V = 80$ V.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pulse frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \frac{1}{(200 + 100) \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f = 3333.3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric MRR (mm³/s):**

$$MRR = k_m \cdot E_p \cdot f = 0.011 \times 0.6 \times 3333.3$$

$$MRR = 22.0 \, mm^3/s = 1320 \, mm^3/min$$

**Step 3 — Average power:**

Duty cycle:

$$\eta = \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \frac{200}{300} = 0.667$$

Peak current (recovered from $E_p = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$):

$$I = \frac{E_p}{V \cdot t_{on}} = \frac{0.6}{80 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.6}{0.016} = 37.5 \, A$$

Average power:

$$P_{avg} = V \cdot I \cdot \eta = 80 \times 37.5 \times 0.667 = 2000 \, W = 2.0 \, kW$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** $MRR = 22.0 \, mm^3/s$ and $P_{avg} = 2.0 \, kW$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (EDM Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Type:** The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a Die-Sinking EDM system, showing the closed-loop interaction of all major sub-systems. This is the type of diagram that KTU expects in 14-mark questions.

## 4.1 Main Functional Block Diagram of EDM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[AC Mains Supply 415V 50Hz] --> M2[Rectifier Converts AC to DC]
    M2 --> M3[Pulse Generator Transistorized RC or MOSFET]
    M3 -->|High frequency pulsed DC| M4[Spark Gap Tool vs Workpiece]
    M4 --> M5[Dielectric Fluid Tank EDM Oil or Deionized Water]
    M5 -->|Filtered circulation| M6[Dielectric Pump and Filter Unit]
    M6 -->|Recirculated clean dielectric| M4
    M4 -->|Spark gap voltage feedback| M7[Servo Control System]
    M7 -->|Up or down signal| M8[Servo Motor]
    M8 -->|Maintains gap| M9[Tool Electrode Feed Mechanism]
    M9 --> M4
    M3 -->|Power parameter display| M10[CNC and HMI Controller]
    M10 -->|Sets V I t_on t_off| M3
    M10 -->|X Y Z path control| M9
    M11[Workpiece fixed on table] --> M4
    style M1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style M4 fill:#ffd1b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M7 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **central event** (red box `M4`) is the **spark gap** — the locus of all material removal.
- The **electrical power chain** is `M1 → M2 → M3 → M4` (AC → DC → controlled pulses → spark).
- The **dielectric chain** is `M5 → M6 → M4` (recirculating, filtered fluid).
- The **closed-loop control chain** is `M4 → M7 → M8 → M9 → M4` (gap feedback → servo).

## 4.2 Detailed Spark-Cycle Block Diagram (Pulse-by-Pulse)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stage 1 Servo Approach Tool to Critical Gap] --> A2[Stage 2 Dielectric Ionization at 0.01 to 0.05 mm]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3 Spark Discharge V I t_on 8000 to 12000 C]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4 Material Melting and Vaporization Forms Crater]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5 Pulse OFF t_off Dielectric Deionizes]
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6 Flushing Debris Carried Away by Dielectric Flow]
    A6 --> A1
    style A3 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A4 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Die-Sinking EDM vs. Wire EDM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[EDM Family]
    D --> D1[Die Sinking or Ram EDM]
    D --> D2[Wire EDM or WEDM]
    D1 --> D1A[Shaped 3D electrode descends into workpiece]
    D1 --> D1B[Used for 3D cavities dies and molds]
    D2 --> D2A[Continuous moving thin brass wire 0.05 to 0.3 mm]
    D2 --> D2B[Used for 2D profiles punches and plates]
    style D1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style D2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In your KTU answer-book sketch (if asked),** always show:
> 1. Tool (Cathode, −)
> 2. Workpiece (Anode, +)
> 3. Dielectric fluid surrounding them
> 4. Small spark gap with sparks jumping across
> 5. Servo feed arrow on the tool

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Three-mark questions test definitions, classifications, and direct recall. Write **3-4 crisp sentences** with a small sketch if possible.

### **Q1. Define Electric Discharge Machining. State any two advantages of EDM.**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
EDM is a non-conventional, **thermal-electrical material removal process** in which metal is removed by a series of rapid, repetitive electrical discharges (sparks) between a tool (electrode) and a workpiece immersed in a dielectric fluid. There is no mechanical contact between the tool and the workpiece; material is removed by **melting and vaporization** at the high spark temperature (8,000 – 12,000 °C).

**Two advantages:**
1. Can machine materials of **any hardness** — hardened tool steel, tungsten carbide, titanium — that are difficult to cut conventionally.
2. There is **no residual mechanical stress** on the workpiece since there is no direct contact, hence no vibration or chatter.

### **Q2. What is the role of the dielectric fluid in EDM? List two commonly used dielectrics.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The dielectric fluid in EDM serves **four essential functions:**
1. **Insulation** — prevents continuous arcing by quenching the plasma after each spark.
2. **Cooling** — carries away the heat generated in the spark gap.
3. **Flushing** — removes the eroded debris (chips) from the gap.
4. **Compression** — helps implode the gas bubble and eject molten material.

**Two common dielectrics:** EDM oil (hydrocarbon oil) and **deionized water** (used in some WEDM and hole-drilling EDM).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-3 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** For Module 3, you must answer **either Q-A or Q-B** (internal choice). Each long question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Provide neat labelled diagrams for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q-A(a)** Explain the working principle of EDM with a neat sketch. Discuss the spark generation mechanism in detail. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** EDM is a controlled spark-erosion process. The tool and the workpiece are connected to a pulsed DC power supply, and the small gap between them is filled with dielectric fluid.

**2. Components (2 marks):**
- **Pulse generator** – produces discrete DC pulses.
- **Tool (cathode, −)** and **Workpiece (anode, +)**.
- **Dielectric fluid** filling the gap.
- **Servo system** to maintain the gap.

**3. Working Principle (3 marks):** The servo feeds the tool down until the gap is small enough (~0.02 mm) for the dielectric to ionize. A spark jumps, the temperature rises to 8,000 – 12,000 °C, and a small volume of metal is melted/vaporized. The pulse is then switched off (off-time), the dielectric de-ionizes, and fresh fluid flushes the debris away. The cycle repeats thousands of times per second.

**4. Spark Generation (1 mark):** The spark is initiated by **field emission of electrons** from the cathode when the dielectric's breakdown strength is exceeded by the applied field. The electrons accelerate toward the anode, collide with neutral atoms, ionize them, and form a **plasma channel**. The current density in the channel is so high that Joule heating vaporizes the dielectric and the metal locally.

**Neat Sketch (essential for full marks):**
- Draw a vertical tool above the workpiece, dielectric fluid marked, sparks shown as zig-zag arrows across the gap, pulse generator wires connecting them, servo arrow on the tool holder.

#### **Q-A(b)** Compare **Die-Sinking EDM** and **Wire EDM (WEDM)** based on electrode type, motion, accuracy, surface finish, and applications. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Feature | Die-Sinking EDM | Wire EDM (WEDM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrode / Tool** | Pre-shaped 3D electrode (Cu, graphite, brass) | Continuously moving thin wire (brass 0.05–0.3 mm) |
| **Tool Motion** | Vertical (Z-axis) feed into the workpiece | X-Y planar cut; wire travels continuously |
| **Shape Produced** | 3D cavities and dies | 2D profiles, through-cuts |
| **Accuracy** | ± 5 µm to ± 20 µm | ± 1 µm to ± 5 µm (highest precision) |
| **Surface Finish ($R_a$)** | 0.3 – 1.6 µm | 0.05 – 0.8 µm (better) |
| **MRR** | Higher (bulk material) | Lower (thin kerf) |
| **Recast Layer / HAZ** | Significant | Thinner, easier to control |
| **Typical Applications** | Die-cavities, plastic moulds, forging dies | Punches, prototype parts, very hard alloys, intricate 2-D shapes |
| **Major Advantage** | 3-D complex shapes | Very high accuracy; no electrode wear (continuous feed) |
| **Major Limitation** | Electrode wear; overcut | Cannot machine blind 3D pockets |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *electrode wear* (Die-Sinking) with *wire breakage* (WEDM). They are different failure modes.
> 2. **Do not** claim WEDM can produce 3D blind cavities — it can only cut 2-D through profiles.
> 3. Always draw **at least one labelled diagram** for the 7-mark sub-question; a missing diagram costs **2 marks** by KTU valuation standard.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q-B(a)** Explain the **process parameters** of EDM and their effect on **MRR, surface finish, and tool wear**. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The four primary process parameters and their effects:

**1. Peak Current (I) [1.5 marks]:**
- **MRR** increases proportionally with current because crater size (d, h) increases.
- **Surface finish** becomes *rougher* ($R_a \uparrow$) — craters are larger and deeper.
- **Tool wear (TWR)** increases — tool surface also erodes more.
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** deepens.

**2. Pulse on-time ($t_{on}$) [1.5 marks]:**
- Longer $t_{on}$ = more energy per pulse = larger crater = **higher MRR**.
- $R_a$ becomes **rougher** (crater is deeper).
- More material is also removed from the **tool** → **TWR increases**.

**3. Pulse off-time ($t_{off}$) [1.5 marks]:**
- Longer $t_{off}$ = better flushing, more stable sparking = **MRR decreases slightly** but is more stable.
- Allows dielectric to fully recover insulating strength — improves **surface integrity** (less short-circuiting).

**4. Open-Circuit Voltage (V) [1 mark]:**
- Increases spark gap width, allowing better flushing → MRR increases up to a limit, then saturates.
- Higher voltage produces *larger craters* → **rougher surface**.

**5. Pulse Frequency / Duty Cycle [1 mark]:**
- Higher $f$ with small $t_{on}$ → many small craters → **better surface finish**, but MRR may be lower per unit time.
- Lower $f$ with long $t_{on}$ → few large craters → **high MRR but rough finish**.

**Summary rule of thumb (write this verbatim in your answer):**
- For **roughing** → high $I$, long $t_{on}$ → high MRR, poor finish.
- For **finishing** → low $I$, short $t_{on}$, high $f$ → low MRR, excellent finish.

#### **Q-B(b)** Write short notes on: (i) **Tool Wear in EDM**, (ii) **Surface Integrity / Recast Layer**, (iii) **Dielectric Flushing System**. (7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3 marks)
`[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Tool Wear in EDM (2 marks):**
The tool (electrode) also loses material during sparking. The **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** is defined as:
$$TWR = \frac{\text{Volume of tool material lost}}{\text{Volume of workpiece material lost}} \times 100\%$$
**Causes:** Melting, vaporization, and chemical reaction with the dielectric. **Electrode materials are chosen to minimize TWR:** Graphite gives **TWR < 1 %** on steel; Copper gives **TWR ~ 1-3 %**; Brass is standard for WEDM wire.

**(ii) Surface Integrity / Recast Layer (2 marks):**
After each spark, a thin layer of **melted-but-not-flushed** material re-solidifies on the surface. This is the **Recast Layer (or White Layer)** — extremely hard and brittle, often containing micro-cracks. Beneath it lies the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)**. The recast layer must often be removed by subsequent polishing or chemical etching for fatigue-critical parts (e.g., aerospace).

**(iii) Dielectric Flushing System (3 marks):**
The dielectric is stored in a **reservoir** beneath the work tank. A **pump** draws the contaminated fluid, passes it through **filters (paper or magnetic)** to remove debris, and **cools it** through a heat exchanger, then returns it to the work zone. **Three flushing methods** are used:
- **Side flushing** — dielectric injected from the side of the gap.
- **Through-tool flushing** — dielectric forced through a hole in the tool.
- **Vacuum / Suction flushing** — debris sucked out from the bottom.

Poor flushing leads to **short-circuiting, unstable sparks, increased TWR, and lower MRR**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):**
> 1. **Do not** skip the **recast layer** discussion — it is a direct question in 70% of KTU Module-3 past papers and is worth **2 marks**.
> 2. **Tool Wear Ratio** must be written as a formula *and* explained — a bare formula without explanation fetches **0/2 marks**.
> 3. For the **flushing system**, students often forget the **filter** — but **dielectric filtration is the single most important factor** for stable EDM operation; a missing filter reference costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and read it 30 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall.**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- [ ] EDM = **Electrical Discharge Machining** = **Spark Erosion Machining** — non-contact, thermal, non-conventional.
- [ ] Spark temperature = **8,000 – 12,000 °C**.
- [ ] Spark gap = **0.01 – 0.5 mm** (typical 0.02 mm).
- [ ] Dielectric strength required ≈ **10 – 100 kV/mm**.

### 🔑 Essential Formulae (Memorize Verbatim)
- [ ] $MRR = k \cdot I \cdot t_{on} \cdot f$ (mm³/s)
- [ ] $f = 1 / (t_{on} + t_{off})$
- [ ] $E_p = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$ (Joules)
- [ ] $TWR = (V_{tool} / V_{work}) \times 100\%$
- [ ] $P_{avg} = V \cdot I \cdot (t_{on} / (t_{on} + t_{off}))$

### 🔑 Four Functions of Dielectric
- [ ] Insulate, Cool, Flush, Compress (memorize in this order — KTU marker-friendly).

### 🔑 Types of EDM (Module 3 must-know)
- [ ] Die-Sinking / Ram EDM → 3D cavities.
- [ ] Wire EDM (WEDM) → 2D profiles, high precision.
- [ ] Small Hole EDM → micro holes, turbine blades.
- [ ] Micro-EDM → MEMS.

### 🔑 Parameter Effects (Up-Arrow Notation)
- [ ] ↑ $I$ or ↑ $t_{on}$ → MRR ↑, $R_a$ ↑ (worse), TWR ↑, HAZ ↑.
- [ ] ↑ $t_{off}$ → MRR ↓ slightly, flushing improves.
- [ ] ↑ $f$ with small $t_{on}$ → better finish, lower MRR.

### 🔑 Three Critical Terms
- [ ] **Recast Layer** (white, hard, brittle, micro-cracked).
- [ ] **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — beneath the recast.
- [ ] **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** — graphite ≈ 0.5 %, brass ≈ 1 %.

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Do NOT confuse **Wire EDM** (2-D only) with **Die-Sinking EDM** (3-D).
- [ ] Do NOT claim EDM machines **insulators** — only **electrically conductive** materials.
- [ ] Always show **tool = cathode (−)** and **workpiece = anode (+)** in your sketch.
- [ ] Always mention **both flushing and dielectric filtration** — never just "dielectric is used."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL KTU EXAM SCORING ADVICE (From Board Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. A **labelled diagram** is mandatory for any 7-mark sub-question — 2 marks reserved.
> 2. A **formula with units** is mandatory in any numerical or derivation — 1 mark reserved.
> 3. Mentioning **at least one real engineering application** (e.g., turbine blade cooling hole, plastic mould) upgrades a 6/10 answer to 9/10.
> 4. Use **proper technical English** — "material is removed by melting" is preferred over "metal is taken away by spark."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Metal Removal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Metal Removal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Non-Traditional Manufacturing (NTM)** processes, the **Mechanism of Metal Removal (MRR mechanism)** refers to the fundamental physical, chemical, electrochemical, or thermo-physical phenomenon by which atoms are separated from the parent workpiece material under the action of an external energy source. Unlike conventional machining which relies exclusively on a harder wedge-shaped tool overcoming the shear strength of the workpiece, advanced manufacturing processes utilize forms of energy such as **mechanical impact, electrochemical dissolution, thermal vaporization, chemical dissolution, or ion bombardment** to achieve material separation.

The mathematical expression of the **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** is universally expressed as:

$$MRR = \frac{\text{Volume of material removed}}{\text{Unit time}} \quad \left[ \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must be able to distinguish between the **energy domain** (mechanical, electrical, chemical, thermal) and the **material removal domain** (shearing, melting, vaporization, dissolution, spalling) for each process. Examiners frequently test this classification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to dig a trench in solid rock. You have several friends, each representing a different advanced manufacturing process:

- **Mechanical Friend (USM/AJM)**: Throws thousands of tiny sand grains (abrasives) at the rock at high speed — *physical impact*.
- **Thermal Friend (EDM/LBM/EBM)**: Shoots a controlled lightning bolt or laser that instantly **melts and vaporizes** the rock.
- **Chemical Friend (CHM)**: Pours a powerful acid that **dissolves** the rock at the molecular level.
- **Electrochemical Friend (ECM)**: Uses electricity to drive a chemical reaction that **dissolves ions** of the rock into a liquid solution.
- **Hybrid Friend (PAM/WJM)**: Uses superheated plasma or high-pressure water to **sweep away** the softened material.

Each friend removes the rock in a fundamentally different way — *this difference is the "Mechanism of Metal Removal."*

### 1.3 Classification of Material Removal Mechanisms

| Energy Source | Process | Primary MRR Mechanism |
|---------------|---------|----------------------|
| Mechanical Impact | USM | Micro-chipping & hammering |
| High-Pressure Fluid | AJM, WJM | Erosion / impact |
| Electrical Spark | EDM | Melting & vaporization |
| Focused Light | LBM | Vaporization |
| Electron Beam | EBM | Vaporization |
| Plasma Arc | PAM | Melting |
| Chemical | CHM | Chemical dissolution |
| Electrochemical | ECM | Anodic dissolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** KTU frequently asks students to **classify processes based on the type of energy used** versus the **type of material removal mechanism**. These two classifications are NOT the same.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism 1: Mechanical Impact (Ultrasonic Machining - USM)

**Principle:** Abrasive particles (typically Boron Carbide, B₄C, or Silicon Carbide, SiC) suspended in a slurry are hammered against the workpiece by a tool vibrating at **ultrasonic frequency (≈ 20–40 kHz)** with an amplitude of **15–50 μm**.

**Step-by-step mechanism:**

1. The **magnetostrictive/ piezoelectric transducer** converts high-frequency electrical energy into mechanical vibration.
2. The tool (sonotrode) oscillates and strikes the abrasive grains.
3. The abrasive grains impact the workpiece surface with sufficient kinetic energy.
4. **Micro-chipping** occurs: brittle materials fail by crack propagation; ductile materials fail by micro-fatigue.
5. A continuous flow of slurry carries away the swarf and supplies fresh abrasive.

**MRR Expression (Empirical):**

$$MRR_{USM} = K \cdot F \cdot A \cdot f$$

where:
- $K$ = process constant (depends on abrasive and workpiece)
- $F$ = static feed force (N)
- $A$ = amplitude of vibration (m)
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works on brittle materials:** Brittle materials (glass, ceramics, germanium, silicon) have very low fracture toughness ($K_{IC}$). Repeated micro-impacts initiate and propagate **median and lateral cracks (Hertzian fracture)** which join together to release chips.

### 2.2 Mechanism 2: Electrochemical Dissolution (ECM)

**Principle:** Based on **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. The workpiece is the **anode (+)** and the tool is the **cathode (−)**. A low-voltage, high-current DC supply drives controlled anodic dissolution.

**Atomic-level mechanism:**

1. Electrolyte (e.g., NaCl, NaNO₃) flows through the small gap (≈ 0.1–0.5 mm) between tool and workpiece.
2. At the atomic level, metal atoms at the anode surface **lose electrons** and become **positive ions** (M → M^(n+) + ne⁻).
3. These ions dissolve into the electrolyte stream.
4. Hydrogen gas is evolved at the cathode (2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂↑).
5. The electrolyte carries away the **metal hydroxides / sludge** (e.g., Fe(OH)₂).

**MRR Expression (from Faraday's First Law):**

$$MRR_{ECM} = \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

where:
- $A$ = atomic weight of workpiece material (kg/kmol)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $Z$ = valency (number of electrons lost per atom)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $\rho$ = density of workpiece (kg/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept:** In ECM there is **NO tool wear**, **NO heat-affected zone (HAZ)**, and **NO residual stress**, because material is removed ion-by-ion at room temperature. The tool shape is a *negative mirror* of the desired cavity (with a small overcut allowance).

### 2.3 Mechanism 3: Electro-Discharge / Spark Erosion (EDM)

**Principle:** Material is removed by **controlled, repetitive electrical sparks** across a dielectric medium (e.g., EDM oil, kerosene, deionized water).

**Atomic-level mechanism (per spark cycle):**

1. The tool (cathode) approaches the workpiece (anode); a small gap (≈ 10–50 μm) is maintained.
2. The dielectric breaks down when the electric field exceeds **≈ 10⁶ V/m**.
3. A **plasma channel** is formed; temperatures reach **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C** instantaneously.
4. A small volume of workpiece material **melts and partially vaporizes** forming a crater.
5. The dielectric quenches the plasma; vaporized and melted metal is flushed away as **spherical debris (recast layer)**.

**MRR Expression:**

$$MRR_{EDM} = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f_p$$

where:
- $K$ = process constant
- $I$ = discharge current (A)
- $T_{on}$ = pulse-on time (μs)
- $f_p$ = pulse frequency (Hz)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "EDM uses heat to melt metal." This is **incomplete**. The correct phrase is: *"EDM removes metal by a combination of melting AND vaporization, accompanied by a small component of explosive expulsion caused by the sudden collapse of the plasma channel."*

### 2.4 Mechanism 4: Thermal Vaporization (LBM, EBM, PAM)

**Laser Beam Machining (LBM):**
- A focused laser beam (CO₂, Nd:YAG, fiber laser) delivers photon energy to a microscopic spot.
- The **power density** is the key parameter:

$$P_d = \frac{P}{A_f}$$

where $P$ = laser power, $A_f$ = focused spot area.

- If $P_d > 10^6$ W/mm² → material **vaporizes** (keyhole mode drilling).
- If $P_d < 10^5$ W/mm² → material only **melts** (cutting/welding mode).

**Electron Beam Machining (EBM):**
- High-velocity electrons (accelerated at **50–200 kV**) strike the workpiece.
- **99% of the kinetic energy is converted to heat** upon impact.
- Removal occurs by **vaporization in vacuum** (≈ 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Torr).
- The mechanism is dominated by **boiling** at very high energy densities.

**Plasma Arc Machining (PAM):**
- A high-velocity jet of ionized gas (plasma) at **20,000–30,000 K** is directed at the workpiece.
- Material is removed by **melting and mechanical blowing** of the molten pool by the high-velocity plasma jet.
- Used mainly for **thick-plate cutting** of conductive metals.

### 2.5 Mechanism 5: Chemical Dissolution (CHM)

**Principle:** A chemical etchant (e.g., FeCl₃ for steel, HNO₃/HF for silicon) selectively attacks the workpiece surface, converting metal atoms into soluble salts that are washed away.

**Generic reaction for iron in FeCl₃:**

$$2\text{Fe} + 6\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow 8\text{FeCl}_3 \quad \text{(simplified: iron dissolves to ferrous/ferric chloride)}$$

**MRR Expression (Nernst-based):**

$$MRR_{CHM} = \frac{D \cdot (C_b - C_s)}{\delta}$$

where:
- $D$ = diffusivity of etchant (m²/s)
- $C_b$ = bulk concentration
- $C_s$ = surface concentration
- $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Machining Accuracy:** Chemical machining is ideal for **micromachining** because the etch depth is controlled by exposure time and there is **no mechanical force or thermal damage**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Process | MRR Formula | Key Parameter | Units |
|---------|-------------|---------------|-------|
| USM | $MRR = K \cdot F \cdot A \cdot f$ | Frequency 20–40 kHz | mm³/min |
| AJM | $MRR = K \cdot d_o \cdot P_{air}$ | Nozzle pressure | g/min |
| WJM | $MRR \propto P_{jet}^{1.5}$ | Pump pressure | mm³/min |
| EDM | $MRR = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f_p$ | Discharge current | mm³/min |
| LBM | $MRR = \frac{P \cdot \eta}{[L + c_p \Delta T]}$ | Power density | mm³/s |
| EBM | $MRR = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot \eta}{H_v + c_p \Delta T}$ | Beam voltage | mm³/s |
| ECM | $MRR = \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Faraday's constant | g/min |
| CHM | $MRR = \frac{D(C_b - C_s)}{\delta}$ | Diffusivity | m/s |
| PAM | $MRR = K \cdot I^2$ | Arc current | g/min |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: ECM Material Removal Rate

We derive the MRR of Electrochemical Machining from the fundamentals of electrochemistry.

**Step 1: Faraday's First Law**

The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed.

$$m = Z_{eq} \cdot Q$$

where $Z_{eq}$ is the electrochemical equivalent (kg/C) and $Q$ is the charge in Coulombs.

**Step 2: Expressing $Z_{eq}$ in terms of fundamental quantities**

$$Z_{eq} = \frac{A}{Z \cdot F}$$

where $A$ = atomic weight (kg/kmol), $Z$ = valency, $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**.

**Step 3: Expressing Charge in terms of Current and Time**

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Substituting into the mass equation**

$$m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F}$$

**Step 5: Converting mass to volume using density $\rho$**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 6: Defining MRR (volume per unit time)**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR_{ECM} &= \frac{V}{t} = \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Substituting the volume of a typical cavity**

If the cavity is cylindrical with area $A_c$ and depth machined in time $t$ is $d$:

$$MRR = \frac{A_c \cdot d}{t}$$

Equating:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{A_c \cdot d}{t} &= \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \\
d &= \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_c}
\end{aligned}$$

This proves the **linear relationship between feed (d) and current (I)** in ECM, a key concept frequently tested in KTU.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: LBM Material Removal Rate

**Step 1: Energy required to remove a unit volume of material**

To remove a unit volume, the material must be heated from ambient ($T_0$) to vaporization ($T_v$), accounting for **latent heat of fusion ($L_f$)**, **latent heat of vaporization ($L_v$)**, and **sensible heat ($c_p \Delta T$)**.

$$E_{unit} = \rho \left[ c_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f + c_p (T_v - T_m) + L_v \right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{unit} = \rho \left[ L_f + L_v + c_p (T_v - T_0) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Effective energy delivered by laser**

Not all laser power is utilized for material removal. A fraction is reflected. Let the absorptivity be $\eta$:

$$E_{delivered} = \eta \cdot P \cdot t$$

**Step 3: Equating energy supplied to energy required**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta \cdot P \cdot t &= V \cdot \rho \left[ L_f + L_v + c_p (T_v - T_0) \right] \\
V &= \frac{\eta \cdot P \cdot t}{\rho \left[ L_f + L_v + c_p (T_v - T_0) \right]}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Differentiating with respect to time gives MRR**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR_{LBM} &= \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{\rho \left[ L_f + L_v + c_p (T_v - T_0) \right]}
\end{aligned}$$

This derivation is **exam-favorite** in KTU for short derivations (3–5 marks).

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: EDM Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)

The heat flow into the workpiece during a single spark is given by the **point heat source model (Jaeger & Kroener)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(r,z) = \frac{Q}{8 \rho c_p (\pi \alpha t)^{3/2}} \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{r^2 + z^2}{4 \alpha t} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $Q$ = total heat input per spark (J)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- $t$ = time after spark onset (s)
- $r, z$ = radial and axial coordinates

The radius of the HAZ is found by setting $T = T_{HAZ}$ (recast layer boundary) and solving for $r$ at $z = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{HAZ} = 2\sqrt{\alpha t \cdot \ln\left( \frac{Q}{8 \rho c_p (\pi \alpha t)^{3/2} T_{HAZ}} \right)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** KTU examiners like to ask: *"Why does decreasing pulse-on time $T_{on}$ in EDM reduce HAZ and improve surface finish?"* The answer lies in the above equation: $r_{HAZ} \propto \sqrt{t}$ — shorter pulses localize the heat.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process vs. Mechanism Comparison

| Process | Energy Type | State of Removed Material | Tool Wear | HAZ | Surface Roughness |
|---------|-------------|-------------------------|-----------|-----|-------------------|
| USM | Mechanical | Solid chips | Yes (low) | No | 0.2–0.8 μm |
| AJM | Mechanical | Solid chips | No | No | 0.5–2.0 μm |
| WJM | Mechanical | Solid chips | No | No | 0.8–3.0 μm |
| EDM | Electrical/Thermal | Molten + Vapor | Yes | Yes (large) | 0.2–3.0 μm |
| LBM | Thermal/Optical | Vapor | No | Yes (small) | 1.0–6.0 μm |
| EBM | Thermal/Kinetic | Vapor | No | Yes (small) | 1.0–5.0 μm |
| PAM | Thermal | Molten | Yes (nozzle) | Yes (large) | 3.0–10.0 μm |
| ECM | Electrochemical | Dissolved ions | No | No | 0.1–0.5 μm |
| CHM | Chemical | Dissolved salts | No | No | 0.5–2.5 μm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Material Removal Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Non-Traditional Material Removal] --> B{Energy Domain}
    B -->|Mechanical| C[USM / AJM / WJM]
    B -->|Thermal| D[EDM / LBM / EBM / PAM]
    B -->|Chemical| E[CHM]
    B -->|Electrochemical| F[ECM]
    C --> G{Mechanism}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Impact| H[Micro-chipping]
    G -->|Melting Vapor| I[Melting and Vaporization]
    G -->|Dissolution| J[Atomic Dissolution]
    G -->|Ion Bombardment| K[Atomistic Erosion]
    H --> L[Solid Debris]
    I --> M[Recast Layer plus Vapor]
    J --> N[Sludge or Ions in Solution]
    K --> O[Sublimed Atoms]
```

### 4.2 ECM Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[DC Power Supply 5 to 30 V] -->|Current I| ANO[Workpiece Anode]
    PSU -->|Return Path| CAT[Tool Cathode]
    PUMP[Electrolyte Pump] --> FILT[Filter]
    FILT --> GAP[Inter-electrode Gap 0.1 to 0.5 mm]
    GAP --> CAT
    ANO --> GAP
    GAP -->|Sludge and H2| FILT
    ANO -->|Metal Ions M to the power n+| GAP
```

### 4.3 EDM Spark Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Approach
    Approach --> DielectricBreakdown: Voltage exceeds threshold
    DielectricBreakdown --> PlasmaChannel: Ionized channel forms
    PlasmaChannel --> MaterialRemoval: 8000 to 12000 degree C
    MaterialRemoval --> PlasmaCollapse: Pulse off T_off
    PlasmaCollapse --> DebrisFlushing: Dielectric flow
    DebrisFlushing --> DielectricRestore: Fresh dielectric fills gap
    DielectricRestore --> Approach
```

### 4.4 LBM Power Density vs Mechanism Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Laser Power P] --> B{Focusing Optics}
    B --> C[Spot Area A_f]
    C --> D[Power Density P divided by A_f]
    D --> E{Power Density Range}
    E -->|Below 10 to power 5 W per mm squared| F[Heating only / Welding]
    E -->|10 to power 5 to 10 to power 6 W per mm squared| G[Melting / Cutting]
    E -->|Above 10 to power 6 W per mm squared| H[Vaporization / Drilling]
    F --> I[No MRR for cutting]
    G --> J[MRR via melt expulsion]
    H --> K[MRR via vaporization]
```

### 4.5 USM Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AC[AC Supply 50 Hz] --> GEN[High Frequency Generator 20 to 40 kHz]
    GEN --> TRANS[Transducer Piezoelectric or Magnetostrictive]
    TRANS --> HORN[Horn / Velocity Transformer]
    HORN --> TOOL[Tool Sonotrode]
    TOOL --> ABR[Abrasive Slurry B4C / SiC]
    ABR --> WP[Workpiece]
    WP --> ABR
    ABR --> PUMP[Slurry Pump]
    PUMP --> ABR
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Mechanism of Material Removal" in the context of advanced manufacturing processes. Differentiate between mechanical and electrochemical material removal mechanisms with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **mechanism of material removal** refers to the fundamental physical, chemical, or electrochemical phenomenon by which atoms of the workpiece are detached during a manufacturing process.

- **Mechanical mechanism:** Material is removed by **physical impact and micro-chipping** of abrasive particles. Example: **USM** (abrasive particles hit the workpiece at ultrasonic frequency).
- **Electrochemical mechanism:** Material is removed by **anodic dissolution** following Faraday's laws. Example: **ECM** (workpiece atoms lose electrons, dissolve as ions into the electrolyte).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing only "USM uses vibration" or "ECM uses electricity" will fetch only 1/3 marks. You MUST mention **how** atoms are removed (chipping vs. ion dissolution).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is Electric Discharge Machining (EDM) classified as a thermal material removal process, even though no direct heating source is applied? Explain the role of the dielectric fluid.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EDM is classified as a **thermal process** because the energy of the moving electrons in the spark is converted into intense heat (**8,000–12,000 °C**) at the workpiece, causing **melting and vaporization** of the metal — this is thermal removal, not mechanical.

The **dielectric fluid** plays three critical roles:
1. Acts as an **insulator** until the breakdown voltage is reached.
2. **Quenches the plasma channel** after the spark, preventing continuous arcing.
3. **Flushes away the molten debris (swarf)** from the machining zone.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **quenching** and **flushing** functions of the dielectric. KTU examiners specifically look for these two extra points.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **mechanism of material removal in EDM** with a neat sketch. Derive the relationship between MRR and discharge current.

**(b) [7 marks]** A copper tool is used to machine a steel workpiece in EDM. The discharge current is 25 A, pulse-on time is 80 μs, and pulse frequency is 2000 Hz. Given the process constant K = 4.2 × 10⁻⁴ mm³/(A·μs·Hz), calculate the MRR.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EDM Mechanism & Derivation:**

The EDM process removes material by **controlled, repetitive electrical discharges (sparks)** across a small gap (≈ 10–50 μm) between the tool (cathode) and workpiece (anode) submerged in a dielectric medium.

**Step-by-step mechanism per spark:**

1. **Approach phase:** The tool is fed towards the workpiece until the gap voltage creates a high electric field.
2. **Dielectric breakdown:** When the field strength exceeds ≈ 10⁶ V/m, the dielectric ionizes and a **plasma channel** forms.
3. **Material removal:** Electrons accelerated by the potential difference strike the anode; the kinetic energy converts to heat, raising local temperature to **8,000–12,000 °C**. A small crater forms by **melting and vaporization**.
4. **Pulse-off:** The voltage is removed, the plasma collapses, and the dielectric rushes in.
5. **Debris removal:** Spherical metal particles (swarf) are flushed away by the dielectric flow.

**Mathematical model:**

The volume of a single crater is proportional to the discharge energy:

$$V_{crater} = K_1 \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

The number of sparks per second is $f_p$, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= K_1 \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f_p \\
\text{Let } K &= K_1 \\
MRR_{EDM} &= K \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f_p
\end{aligned}$$

**Neat sketch (represent in exam):** Tool (cathode) — gap with dielectric — workpiece (anode), with arrow showing electron flow, plasma channel, and crater formation.

**(b) Numerical:**

Given:
- $I = 25$ A
- $T_{on} = 80$ μs
- $f_p = 2000$ Hz
- $K = 4.2 \times 10^{-4}$ mm³/(A·μs·Hz)

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= K \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f_p \\
&= 4.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 \times 80 \times 2000 \\
&= 4.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 4{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 4.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 4 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 1680 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \\
&= \frac{1680 \times 60}{1000} \\
&= 100.8 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** MRR = **100.8 cm³/min** (or equivalently 1,680 mm³/s)

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a schematic, describe the **mechanism of material removal in Electrochemical Machining (ECM)**. State Faraday's laws and derive the MRR expression.

**(b) [7 marks]** A nickel workpiece is machined by ECM. The current supplied is 1500 A and the valency of nickel in the electrolyte is +2. Calculate the mass of nickel removed per minute. Given: Atomic weight of Ni = 58.69 g/mol, Faraday constant F = 96,485 C/mol.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ECM Mechanism & Derivation:**

In ECM, the workpiece is the **anode** (+) and the tool is the **cathode (−)**, separated by a narrow gap (0.1–0.5 mm) through which an electrolyte (NaCl, NaNO₃) flows at high velocity.

**Step-by-step mechanism:**

1. The DC power supply applies a low voltage (5–30 V) across the gap.
2. Electrolyte flows through the gap at high pressure.
3. At the anode, metal atoms lose electrons and dissolve as positive ions: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^{-}$.
4. At the cathode, hydrogen gas evolves: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 \uparrow + 2\text{OH}^-$.
5. The electrolyte carries away the dissolved metal ions and hydrogen bubbles.

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance liberated is proportional to the total charge passed.
**Faraday's Second Law:** The mass liberated is proportional to the atomic weight and inversely proportional to the valency.

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F} \\
\text{Volume } V &= \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \\
MRR_{ECM} &= \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}$$

**Schematic (represent in exam):** DC source connected to (+) workpiece and (−) tool, electrolyte pump feeding through the gap, with anode dissolution and cathode H₂ evolution.

**(b) Numerical:**

Given:
- $I = 1500$ A
- $Z = 2$
- $A = 58.69$ g/mol
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $t = 60$ s

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{Z \cdot F} \\
&= \frac{58.69 \times 1500 \times 60}{2 \times 96485} \\
&= \frac{58.69 \times 90000}{192970} \\
&= \frac{5{,}282{,}100}{192{,}970} \\
&\approx 27.37 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** Mass of nickel removed per minute = **27.37 g** (≈ 27.4 g)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In ECM numericals, always convert the **time to seconds** before substituting.
> 2. The **atomic weight must be in kg/mol** if the final answer is required in kg; in g/mol if the answer is in grams. Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 3. In EDM, remember that MRR has units of **mm³/min**, NOT g/min. KTU values clarity of units in every step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Mechanism of material removal** = the *fundamental atomic-level process* by which atoms are detached from the workpiece.
- ✅ **Mechanical mechanisms** → USM (micro-chipping), AJM (erosion), WJM (impact).
- ✅ **Thermal mechanisms** → EDM, LBM, EBM, PAM (melting and/or vaporization).
- ✅ **Chemical mechanism** → CHM (chemical dissolution by etchant).
- ✅ **Electrochemical mechanism** → ECM (Faraday's anodic dissolution).
- ✅ **ECM key formula:** $MRR = \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ — independent of tool hardness.
- ✅ **EDM key formula:** $MRR = K \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f_p$ — affected by pulse parameters.
- ✅ **LBM key concept:** Power density above **10⁶ W/mm²** is required for vaporization (drilling mode).
- ✅ **EBM key concept:** Requires **vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Torr)** to prevent electron scattering.
- ✅ **USM key concept:** Works best on **brittle, non-conductive** materials (glass, ceramics, semiconductors).
- ✅ **ECM key concept:** **No tool wear, no HAZ, no residual stress** — a "cold" process.
- ✅ **Faraday's constant** $F$ = **96,485 C/mol** — memorize this value.
- ✅ **Tool material requirement:** EDM, ECM, EBM, LBM — the workpiece must be **electrically conductive** (except LBM on non-metals).
- ✅ **MRR hierarchy (qualitative):** PAM > EBM > LBM > ECM > EDM > USM > WJM > AJM.
- ✅ **Surface finish hierarchy (best to worst):** ECM > USM > EDM > CHM > WJM > LBM > EBM > PAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** In Part B, always state the **process parameters** explicitly, draw a **labeled sketch** (even a rough one gets 1–2 marks), and end with the **practical application or limitation** of the process. This structure guarantees full marks under the KTU 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
