<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — ELECTRODE FEED CONTROL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electrode Feed Control (EFC)** is the regulated, servo-driven displacement of the tool electrode (or workpiece) along the machining axis in **Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)** such that the **inter-electrode gap (IEG)** is continuously maintained within the **dielectric breakdown zone** (typically **5 µm to 50 µm**, depending on pulse energy). It is fundamentally a **closed-loop electromechanical feedback problem** in which the *average gap voltage* is sensed, compared against a *reference voltage*, and the resulting *error signal* drives an actuator that advances or retracts the electrode to compensate for material erosion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Electrode feed control is the technique of automatically maintaining a constant spark gap between the tool electrode and the workpiece during EDM by comparing the actual gap voltage with a preset reference voltage and using the error signal to drive a servo mechanism."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are **driving a car in heavy fog** and you must keep a *constant distance* of 2 meters from the car in front of you:
- If the gap becomes too large (the other car accelerates) → you press the **accelerator** (advance the electrode).
- If the gap becomes too small (the other car brakes) → you press the **brake** (retract the electrode).
- Your **eyes** are the gap voltage sensor, the **setpoint** of 2 m is the reference voltage, and the **accelerator/brake pedal** is the servo motor.

In EDM, the "other car" is the *eroding workpiece surface*, and you (the servo system) must continually chase it as it moves away due to **material removal**. Lose control for even a fraction of a second and you either **stall** (open circuit) or **crash** (short circuit → arcing damage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EFC is Non-Trivial:** The MRR in EDM is unpredictable — it varies with **flushing efficiency**, **recast layer formation**, **debris concentration**, and **electrode wear**. A *fixed* feed rate will therefore always fail. The system MUST be **adaptive and closed-loop**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Spark Gap (IEG) | **5 – 50 µm** | micrometers |
| Reference Voltage ($V_{ref}$) | **50 – 90 V** (typical setpoint) | Volts |
| Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$) | **80 – 250 V** DC | Volts |
| Gap Voltage during spark | **20 – 25 V** | Volts |
| Servo Response Time | **10 – 100 ms** | milliseconds |
| Electrode Feed Velocity | **0.1 – 10 mm/min** | mm/min |
| Pulse Frequency (modern generators) | **5 kHz – 500 kHz** | kHz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Problem of Electrode Feed

As sparks erode the workpiece at a rate of **Material Removal Rate (MRR)**, the gap widens. If the electrode does not advance, the gap voltage rises, eventually exceeding the dielectric strength of the flushed dielectric — **sparks cease (open-circuit condition)**. Conversely, if the electrode advances too aggressively, it physically contacts the workpiece — **short-circuit (DC arc)**, causing tool breakage and surface burn.

The feed controller must therefore continuously satisfy:

$$\text{Feed Velocity} \approx \text{Instantaneous MRR}$$

But because MRR is *non-stationary* (varies pulse-to-pulse), a **closed-loop servo** is mandatory.

## 2.2 Functional Logic of the Servo Loop (Step-by-Step)

The control loop executes the following sequence *thousands of times per second*:

1. **Sensing Stage:** A high-impedance voltage probe measures the instantaneous gap voltage $V_g(t)$.
2. **Averaging Stage:** A low-pass filter extracts the *average* gap voltage $\bar{V}_g$.
3. **Comparison Stage:** A summing junction computes the error: 
$$e(t) = V_{ref} - \bar{V}_g$$
4. **Amplification Stage:** A servo amplifier scales $e(t)$ into a drive current.
5. **Actuation Stage:** The current drives a hydraulic cylinder or DC servo motor, advancing/retracting the electrode.
6. **Mechanical Stage:** The electrode physically moves at a velocity $v_f$ proportional to $e(t)$:
$$v_f = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt$$
(The second term is the *integral* action that eliminates steady-state offset.)

## 2.3 Three States the Controller Must Distinguish

| Condition | Gap Voltage | Controller Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Circuit (no spark)** | $V_g \to V_{oc}$ | **Advance fast** (gap too large) |
| **Normal Sparking** | $V_g \approx V_{ref}$ | **Hold / slow advance** |
| **Short Circuit (arcing)** | $V_g \to 0$ | **Retract fast** (gap collapsed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Concept:** Explain why a *single reference voltage* is insufficient for **roughing** vs. **finishing** operations. In roughing, a *higher* $V_{ref}$ is used to allow aggressive feeding; in finishing, a *lower* $V_{ref}$ maintains a small gap for fine surface finish.

## 2.4 Types of Electrode Feed Mechanisms (KTU-Mapped Classification)

### A. **Electro-Hydraulic Servo Control** (Most common in die-sinking EDM)
- A flapper-nozzle converts electrical error signal into hydraulic pressure.
- The hydraulic piston moves the ram at high force and good damping.
- **Advantage:** Excellent for heavy electrodes (forces up to 500 N).
- **Disadvantage:** Oil leakage, slower response, oil temperature drift.

### B. **Electro-Mechanical (DC Servo Motor) Control**
- A DC servo motor drives a precision **ball screw** or **linear motor**.
- Position feedback via **LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer).
- **Advantage:** High precision, no hydraulic oil, easy to digitally control.
- **Disadvantage:** Lower force capacity, backlash in ball screw.

### C. **Piezoelectric / Voice-Coil Feed (Modern, Nanosecond EDM)**
- Used in **micro-EDM** and **wire EDM** with high-frequency pulse generators.
- Direct linear motion, no mechanical transmission → zero backlash.
- **Advantage:** Sub-micron positioning.
- **Disadvantage:** Very small stroke (~mm range).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = V_{ref} - \bar{V}_g$ | Error signal | $V_{ref}$ ∈ $\vert 50, 90 \vert$ V |
| 2 | $v_f = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt$ | PID feed velocity | $K_p$: proportional gain, $K_i$: integral gain |
| 3 | $MRR = K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on} \cdot f$ | Material Removal Rate (volume/time) | $K$ depends on workpiece material |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{material}}{V_{total\,eroded}}$ | Wear ratio (tool wear) | $\eta < 1\%$ for graphite on steel |
| 5 | $R_a = K_s \cdot I_p^{0.4} \cdot T_{on}^{0.2}$ | Surface roughness (empirical) | $I_p$ in A, $T_{on}$ in µs |
| 6 | $V_{spark} \approx 20 - 25$ V | Spark-on voltage (constant) | Independent of $V_{oc}$ |
| 7 | $V_{ref} = \alpha \cdot V_{oc}$ | Reference voltage as fraction | $\alpha \in \vert 0.4, 0.7 \vert$ |
| 8 | $Gap = f(I_p, T_{on}, flushing)$ | Inter-electrode gap | $\vert 5, 50 \vert$ µm |
| 9 | $t_{delay} \rightarrow 0$ for adaptive | Deionization time | Must be < 50% of $T_{off}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, writing only the *formula* without *boundary conditions* and *typical values* results in **partial credit (60-70%)** of the mark allotted to that step. Always state units.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electrode feed control is **not confined to die-sinking EDM**. The same principle is reused in:
- **Wire-EDM (WEDM):** Wire is fed between upper and lower guides; tension and feed control prevent wire breakage.
- **Micro-EDM drilling:** Sub-millimeter holes in fuel injectors, turbine blades.
- **Die & mould manufacturing:** Automobiles, aerospace forging dies.
- **Surgical needle manufacturing:** Micro-holes in Nitinol stents.

The robustness of the feed controller essentially **defines the productivity** of the entire EDM cell.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Average Gap Voltage as a Function of Pulse Parameters

Consider a single EDM pulse cycle of period $T_p$ consisting of:
- **$T_{on}$** = pulse-on (spark) duration
- **$T_{off}$** = pulse-off (deionization) duration
- $T_p = T_{on} + T_{off}$

During $T_{on}$, the gap voltage is approximately constant at the spark-sustaining voltage:

$$V_{spark} \approx 20 \text{ V to } 25 \text{ V}$$

During $T_{off}$, the gap voltage rises back to the open-circuit voltage (since the capacitor bank recharges):

$$V_{off} \approx V_{oc} = \text{set open-circuit voltage (e.g., 100 V)}$$

**Step 1 — Define the average gap voltage over one cycle:**

$$\bar{V}_g = \frac{1}{T_p} \int_0^{T_p} V_g(t)\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the piecewise waveform:**

$$\bar{V}_g = \frac{1}{T_p} \left[ \int_0^{T_{on}} V_{spark}\,dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T_p} V_{oc}\,dt \right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals (constants over their intervals):**

$$\bar{V}_g = \frac{1}{T_p} \left[ V_{spark} \cdot T_{on} + V_{oc} \cdot T_{off} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{off} = T_p - T_{on}$:**

$$\bar{V}_g = \frac{V_{spark} \cdot T_{on} + V_{oc} \cdot (T_p - T_{on})}{T_p}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify into a duty-cycle (D) form:**

Let $D = T_{on} / T_p$ be the **duty cycle**. Then:

$$\bar{V}_g = V_{spark} \cdot D + V_{oc} \cdot (1 - D)$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form:**

$$\bar{V}_g = V_{oc} - (V_{oc} - V_{spark}) \cdot D$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** If $D = 0$ (no sparks, pure open circuit), then $\bar{V}_g = V_{oc}$. If $D = 1$ (continuous spark/short), then $\bar{V}_g = V_{spark}$. The feed controller exploits the *continuous variation* of $\bar{V}_g$ between these two extremes.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Reference Voltage Selection Strategy

The KTU 2024 examiner frequently tests the *mapping* between **operation type**, **$V_{ref}$**, and **expected outcome**. The complete matrix is:

| Operation Type | Reference Voltage $V_{ref}$ | Duty Cycle Target | Resulting Gap | Surface Finish $R_a$ | MRR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Roughing** | **High** (≈ 70% of $V_{oc}$) | High D (~0.6) | Large (30-50 µm) | Coarse (4-8 µm) | **Maximum** |
| **Semi-Finishing** | **Medium** (≈ 50% of $V_{oc}$) | Medium D (~0.4) | Medium (15-25 µm) | Medium (2-3 µm) | Moderate |
| **Finishing** | **Low** (≈ 40% of $V_{oc}$) | Low D (~0.2) | Small (5-10 µm) | Fine (0.4-1 µm) | **Minimum** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Equation (the heart of EFC design):** $MRR \times R_a \approx \text{constant for fixed material}$. To improve $R_a$ (lower it), you must sacrifice MRR. The feed controller does NOT violate this — it merely *optimizes* the operating point.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An EDM machine has $V_{oc} = 100$ V, $V_{spark} = 25$ V. The pulse generator operates at $T_{on} = 50$ µs and $T_{off} = 30$ µs. The operator sets $V_{ref} = 70$ V. Determine whether the controller will advance or retract the electrode.

**Step 1 — Compute the pulse period:**

$$T_p = T_{on} + T_{off} = 50 + 30 = 80 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the duty cycle:**

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_p} = \frac{50}{80} = 0.625$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average gap voltage using the derived formula:**

$$\bar{V}_g = V_{oc} - (V_{oc} - V_{spark}) \cdot D$$

$$\bar{V}_g = 100 - (100 - 25) \times 0.625$$

$$\bar{V}_g = 100 - 75 \times 0.625 = 100 - 46.875$$

$$\bar{V}_g = 53.125 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the reference:**

$$e = V_{ref} - \bar{V}_g = 70 - 53.125 = 16.875 \text{ V} > 0$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the error:**

Since $e > 0$, the *actual* average gap voltage is **lower** than the reference. This means the *gap is too small* (too many sparks / approaching short circuit). The controller will command the electrode to **RETRACT**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students write "advance" when $e > 0$. This is **backwards**. Recall: higher $V_g$ = larger gap (dielectric resistance ↑). So $V_{ref} > \bar{V}_g$ means *controller wants larger gap* → retract.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Feed-Velocity-Error Relationship

The simplest model of a servo actuator (neglecting dynamics) is:

$$v_f(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the error definition:**

$$v_f(t) = K_p \left[ V_{ref} - \bar{V}_g(t) \right]$$

**Step 2 — At steady state, $v_f = MRR$:**

$$MRR = K_p \left[ V_{ref} - \bar{V}_g^{ss} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the steady-state gap voltage:**

$$\bar{V}_g^{ss} = V_{ref} - \frac{MRR}{K_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** To increase $MRR$ for the same $V_{ref}$, the designer must either increase $K_p$ (faster actuator) or accept a *smaller* steady-state gap (lower $\bar{V}_g^{ss}$). This is why high-$K_p$ systems (linear motors, piezo) are preferred in micro-EDM.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Servo Architecture (KTU Board Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **canonical electrode feed control loop** as typically expected in KTU ESE question papers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Sets Reference Voltage Vref] --> B[Summing Comparator Junction]
    C[Gap Voltage Sensor Probes IEG] --> D[Low Pass Filter Average Vg]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Error Signal e equals Vref minus Vgbar]
    E --> F[Servo Amplifier with Gain Kp]
    F --> G[Summing with Integral Ki]
    G --> H[Power Amplifier Stage]
    H --> I[Servo Actuator Hydraulic Cylinder or DC Motor]
    I --> J[Ball Screw and Ram Assembly]
    J --> K[Electrode Translates Toward Workpiece]
    K --> L[Spark Erodes Workpiece Material]
    L --> M[Gap Alters Gap Voltage Vg]
    M --> C
    L --> N[Debris and Flushing Affects IEG Stability]
    N --> M
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic: Controller State Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Measure Vgbar] --> Q1{Is Vgbar greater than Vref high}
    Q1 -- YES --> S1[STATE OPEN CIRCUIT Advance Electrode Fast]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is Vgbar within Vref band}
    Q2 -- YES --> S2[STATE NORMAL SPARKING Hold or Slow Advance]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is Vgbar less than Vref low}
    Q3 -- YES --> S3[STATE SHORT CIRCUIT Retract Electrode Fast]
    Q3 -- NO --> S4[STATE ABNORMAL Latch Alarm and Stop]
    S1 --> Start
    S2 --> Start
    S3 --> Start
    S4 --> Stop[Emergency Stop Output]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Hydraulic vs. Electric Servo

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HS[HYDRAULIC SERVO PATH]
        H1[Error Signal e] --> H2[Flapper Nozzle Amplifier]
        H2 --> H3[Spool Valve Hydraulic]
        H3 --> H4[Double Acting Cylinder]
        H4 --> H5[Ram Carries Electrode]
    end
    subgraph ES[ELECTRIC SERVO PATH]
        E1[Error Signal e] --> E2[PWM Servo Amplifier]
        E2 --> E3[DC Servo Motor or Linear Motor]
        E3 --> E4[Ball Screw or Linear Encoder]
        E4 --> E5[Electrode Mounted on Z Axis]
    end
    H1 -.alternative.-> E1
    E1 -.alternative.-> H1
```

## 4.4 Reference-Voltage Tuning Strategy Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP{Operation Type Selected} --> R1[Roughing Mode]
    OP --> R2[Semifinishing Mode]
    OP --> R3[Finishing Mode]
    R1 --> V1[Vref set to 70 percent of Voc]
    R2 --> V2[Vref set to 50 percent of Voc]
    R3 --> V3[Vref set to 40 percent of Voc]
    V1 --> X1[Large Gap Allowed Aggressive Feed Maximum MRR]
    V2 --> X2[Medium Gap Balanced Feed and Finish]
    V3 --> X3[Small Gap Conservative Feed Fine Surface Ra]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Exam Tip (Visual Mapping):** Whenever a question contains a phrase like *"with the help of a block diagram explain…"*, the KTU examiner expects *at minimum* a **sensing → comparing → amplifying → actuating** block sequence with a **feedback arrow** closing the loop. Omission of the feedback arrow causes a 2-mark penalty.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is electrode feed control necessary in Electrical Discharge Machining? What happens if the feed rate is too high or too low?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Electrode feed control is necessary in EDM to **maintain a constant spark gap** (typically 5–50 µm) between the tool electrode and the workpiece, ensuring continuous and stable discharge. The gap must be neither too wide (where the dielectric does not break down and sparking stops) nor too narrow (where the tool contacts the workpiece and a short circuit or DC arc occurs, damaging both surfaces).

- If the **feed rate is too low**, the gap widens progressively as material is eroded. The dielectric strength exceeds the open-circuit voltage, and sparking **stops** (open-circuit condition). MRR drops to zero.
- If the **feed rate is too high**, the electrode advances faster than material is removed. The gap collapses, leading to a **short circuit** with severe arcing, surface damage, and possible tool breakage.

Hence, an **automatic servo-controlled feed** is mandatory to track the time-varying MRR in real time.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between open-circuit, normal sparking, and short-circuit conditions in EDM in terms of gap voltage. How does the feed controller use this distinction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Condition | Gap Voltage | Gap Status | Controller Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Circuit** | ≈ $V_{oc}$ (80–250 V) | Too large, no discharge | **Advance fast** |
| **Normal Spark** | ≈ 20–25 V (during $T_{on}$) | Stable discharge | Hold or slow advance |
| **Short Circuit** | ≈ 0 V (DC arc) | Electrode touches workpiece | **Retract fast** |

The controller measures the *average* gap voltage, compares it with the **reference voltage $V_{ref}$** (set between $V_{spark}$ and $V_{oc}$), and uses the **error signal** to drive the servo actuator. The reference voltage is the **decision threshold** that classifies the state of the gap.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop servo system** used for electrode feed control in EDM. Label all major blocks and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **average gap voltage** in terms of open-circuit voltage, spark voltage, and duty cycle. A die-sinking EDM operates at $V_{oc} = 120$ V, $V_{spark} = 22$ V, with $T_{on} = 80$ µs and $T_{off} = 40$ µs. If the operator sets $V_{ref} = 75$ V, determine the controller action. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):**

The closed-loop servo system for EDM electrode feed control consists of the following blocks in sequence:

1. **Reference Voltage Source (1 mark):** The operator sets a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ via a potentiometer, representing the *desired* average gap voltage. Typical range: 50–90 V.
2. **Gap Voltage Sensor (1 mark):** A high-impedance voltage probe continuously measures the instantaneous voltage $V_g(t)$ across the spark gap without drawing current.
3. **Low-Pass Filter / Averager (1 mark):** Converts the pulse-train waveform into a smooth DC signal representing the *average* gap voltage $\bar{V}_g$.
4. **Comparator (Error Detector) (1 mark):** Computes the error $e(t) = V_{ref} - \bar{V}_g$.
5. **Servo Amplifier (1 mark):** Amplifies the small error signal into a power-level drive signal with proportional gain $K_p$ and integral action $K_i$.
6. **Actuator (Hydraulic Cylinder / DC Servo Motor) (1 mark):** Converts the amplified signal into mechanical motion, advancing or retracting the electrode.
7. **Feedback Path (1 mark):** The mechanical motion alters the gap, which alters $V_g$, closing the loop.

**(b) Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3 marks):** As shown in Section 3.1, the average gap voltage is:

$$\bar{V}_g = V_{oc} - (V_{oc} - V_{spark}) \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T_p}$$

where $T_p = T_{on} + T_{off}$.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the pulse period:**

$$T_p = T_{on} + T_{off} = 80 + 40 = 120 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the duty cycle:**

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_p} = \frac{80}{120} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average gap voltage:**

$$\bar{V}_g = 120 - (120 - 22) \times 0.667 = 120 - 98 \times 0.667 = 120 - 65.33 = 54.67 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the error signal:**

$$e = V_{ref} - \bar{V}_g = 75 - 54.67 = 20.33 \text{ V} > 0$$

**Step 5 — Determine controller action:** Since $e > 0$, the actual gap voltage is *lower* than the reference → the gap is **too small** → controller commands the electrode to **RETRACT** to open the gap.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of electrode feed mechanisms** used in EDM. Compare hydraulic servo control with DC servo motor control in terms of force, accuracy, response time, and maintenance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the role of the **reference voltage** in electrode feed control? How does its value differ for roughing, semi-finishing, and finishing operations? Justify your answer using the relationship between $V_{ref}$, gap, and surface finish. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Types of Feed Mechanisms (7 marks):**

- **Electro-Hydraulic Servo (3 marks):** Uses flapper-nozzle → spool valve → double-acting cylinder. Force: 100–500 N. Accuracy: ±5 µm. Response: 50–100 ms. Maintenance: oil filtration, leak checks, temperature compensation.
- **Electro-Mechanical (DC Servo) (2 marks):** DC motor + ball screw. Force: 50–200 N. Accuracy: ±1 µm. Response: 10–30 ms. Maintenance: ball-screw lubrication, encoder alignment.
- **Piezoelectric / Voice-Coil (2 marks):** Direct linear motion, no transmission. Force: 5–50 N. Accuracy: ±0.1 µm. Response: <1 ms. Used in micro-EDM. Maintenance: minimal.

**(b) Role of Reference Voltage (7 marks):**

The reference voltage is the **commanded setpoint** of the average gap voltage, acting as the **decision threshold** between open-circuit, sparking, and short-circuit states. Its value directly determines the inter-electrode gap size and consequently the surface roughness and MRR (2 marks).

- **Roughing (2 marks):** $V_{ref} \approx$ 70% of $V_{oc}$ → large gap (~30–50 µm) → aggressive feed → high MRR, coarse $R_a$ (4–8 µm).
- **Semi-finishing (1.5 marks):** $V_{ref} \approx$ 50% of $V_{oc}$ → medium gap (~15–25 µm) → balanced trade-off.
- **Finishing (1.5 marks):** $V_{ref} \approx$ 40% of $V_{oc}$ → small gap (~5–10 µm) → conservative feed → fine $R_a$ (0.4–1 µm), low MRR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark-Loss Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the feedback loop arrow (–2 marks):** A block diagram without a closed-loop arrow is treated as an open-loop system — examiner deducts 2 of the 7 marks allocated to part (a).
> 2. **Writing "advance" for a positive error (–1 mark):** This is the most common conceptual error. Always clarify: *positive error means the gap is too small → retract*.
> 3. **Omitting numerical units in calculations (–1 mark):** "75" without "V" loses 1 mark; "120 µs" without the unit loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $V_{ref}$ with $V_{oc}$ (–2 marks):** $V_{ref}$ is the *setpoint* chosen by the operator; $V_{oc}$ is a *machine constant* set by the generator design.
> 5. **Not mentioning duty cycle in derivations (–1 mark):** Any derivation of $\bar{V}_g$ must contain the duty cycle $D = T_{on}/T_p$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision (5-Minute Drill):**

- ✅ **Electrode Feed Control (EFC)** = closed-loop servo regulation of the inter-electrode gap in EDM.
- ✅ **Sensing element** = high-impedance gap voltage probe.
- ✅ **Decision variable** = *average* gap voltage $\bar{V}_g$ (not instantaneous).
- ✅ **Control law** = $e = V_{ref} - \bar{V}_g$.
- ✅ **Three states** = Open-circuit ($V_g \to V_{oc}$, advance), Sparking ($V_g \approx V_{ref}$, hold), Short-circuit ($V_g \to 0$, retract).
- ✅ **Master formula:** $\bar{V}_g = V_{oc} - (V_{oc} - V_{spark}) \cdot D$ where $D = T_{on}/T_p$.
- ✅ **Reference voltage bands:** Roughing ≈ 70% $V_{oc}$; Semi-finishing ≈ 50% $V_{oc}$; Finishing ≈ 40% $V_{oc}$.
- ✅ **Trade-off law:** $MRR \cdot R_a \approx \text{constant}$ for a given material — the controller only *optimises*, never *violates*.
- ✅ **Three mechanism families:** Electro-hydraulic (high force), Electro-mechanical DC servo (high precision), Piezo/voice-coil (micro-EDM).
- ✅ **Modern trend:** Iso-energetic pulse generators + adaptive control with real-time $I_p$ and $T_{on}$ modulation.
- ✅ **Critical exam one-liner:** *"A positive error ($V_{ref} > \bar{V}_g$) means the gap is too small → retract the electrode."*
- ✅ **Units to memorize:** V (volts), µs (microseconds), µm (micrometres), mm/min (feed velocity), N (actuator force), Hz/kHz/MHz (pulse frequency).
- ✅ **Block diagram must contain:** Reference source → Comparator → Amplifier → Actuator → Electrode → Gap → Sensor → back to comparator.
- ✅ **Forgetting the feedback arrow in diagrams = –2 marks.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dielectric Fluids
topics.content:

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Dielectric Fluids

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Dielectric Fluid** (also called *dielectric medium* or *dielectric liquid*) is a non-conductive, electrically insulating liquid that possesses high dielectric strength, high flash point, and excellent thermal stability. In the context of **Advanced Manufacturing Processes**, dielectric fluids are primarily used as the working medium in **Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)**, **Wire-EDM (WEDM)**, and **Electrical Discharge Grinding (EDG)** operations.

The KTU 2024 Scheme defines the dielectric fluid as:

> *"A dielectric fluid in EDM is an insulating liquid that fills the inter-electrode gap between the tool electrode and the workpiece. It acts as a spark generator medium, a coolant, a debris flusher, and an arc-quenching agent."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Dielectric fluids are explicitly listed under the EDM working principles. Students **must** know the **properties**, **types**, **functions**, and **selection criteria** of dielectric fluids for KTU University Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **lightning storm** between two clouds. The air between the clouds acts as an **insulator** (preventing continuous flow of current). However, when the voltage becomes high enough, a spark **jumps across the gap**, ionizing the air momentarily to complete the circuit. 

Now imagine doing this in a **controlled environment** — a glass tank filled with oil (instead of air), with two metal plates (one shaped like your desired cavity) placed close to a metal workpiece. When voltage is applied:

- The **oil** prevents continuous current flow (insulation) — like the air gap.
- When the voltage crosses a critical limit, a **spark jumps** through the oil, vaporizing a tiny bit of metal from the workpiece.
- The same oil **cools** the molten crater, **flushes** the debris away, and **quenches** the arc to prevent short-circuiting.

This oil **IS** the dielectric fluid. It is the "blood" of the EDM process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a dielectric fluid as a **busy traffic controller at a junction**. It temporarily allows tiny "vehicles" (sparks/electrons) to pass through one at a time, ensures no collision (short circuit), removes the "wreckage" (debris), and keeps the temperature down (cooling) — all while the main road (workpiece) is being slowly carved out.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally used to characterize dielectric fluids in EDM:

- **Dielectric Strength (Breakdown Voltage):** Typically **10–30 kV/mm** for EDM-grade fluids.
- **Dielectric Constant (Relative Permittivity, $\varepsilon_r$):** Usually between **2.0 and 5.0** for hydrocarbon oils.
- **Viscosity (Kinematic):** Between **2 to 10 cSt** at 40 °C (low viscosity preferred for better flushing).
- **Flash Point:** Above **100 °C** (safety requirement).
- **Density:** Between **0.75 and 0.90 g/cm³** at room temperature.
- **Thermal Conductivity:** Approximately **0.15 to 0.20 W/m·K**.
- **Volume Resistivity:** Above **$10^{10}$ Ω·m** (excellent insulator).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These values are **board-exam worthy**. If a numerical is given (e.g., breakdown voltage vs. gap distance), use these ranges as sanity checks.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functions of Dielectric Fluid in EDM (The "Four Pillars")

The dielectric fluid performs **four critical functions** simultaneously during the EDM process. These are heavily tested in KTU exams.

### Pillar 1: **Insulation / Dielectric Barrier**
- Prevents premature current flow between tool and workpiece.
- Holds the gap open so that material removal occurs **only** during controlled spark discharge.
- **Why?** Without insulation, the tool and workpiece would short-circuit, stopping the process.

### Pillar 2: **Spark Generation Medium**
- Acts as the **ionizable medium** for spark formation.
- When voltage across the gap exceeds the **breakdown voltage** of the fluid, the fluid **ionizes** to form a plasma channel.
- **How?** The molecules of the dielectric break into ions and electrons, creating a conductive plasma bridge for the spark.

### Pillar 3: **Cooling Agent**
- Absorbs the **intense heat** (temperatures of **8,000–12,000 °C** in the plasma channel) generated during each spark.
- Prevents thermal damage to the workpiece, tool, and machine components.
- **Why?** Without cooling, the tool electrode would wear rapidly, and the workpiece surface would suffer from excessive heat-affected zones (HAZ).

### Pillar 4: **Debris Flushing**
- Carries away the **eroded particles** (debris) from the machining zone.
- Prevents **recast layer** buildup and **secondary sparking** (unwanted sparks).
- **How?** The fluid is circulated through the gap using a **pump and filter system** under pressure (typically **0.5 to 5 kg/cm²**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams: "ISCF"** — **I**nsulation, **S**park Medium, **C**ooling, **F**lushing.

## 2.2 Desired Properties of an Ideal Dielectric Fluid

A high-yield KTU question: *"List the properties of an ideal dielectric fluid for EDM."*

| S.No | Property | Desired Value / Behavior | Engineering Reason |
|------|----------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **High Dielectric Strength** | > 10 kV/mm | Allows higher gap voltage without premature breakdown |
| 2 | **Low Viscosity** | 2–5 cSt | Better flushing of debris; better flow in narrow gaps |
| 3 | **High Flash Point** | > 100 °C | Safety against ignition at spark temperatures |
| 4 | **Good Thermal Stability** | Stable up to 200 °C | No decomposition under repeated heating |
| 5 | **Chemical Inertness** | No reaction with workpiece/tool | Prevents corrosion and contamination |
| 6 | **Low Volatility** | High boiling point (> 200 °C) | Reduces evaporation losses |
| 7 | **Non-Toxic & Odorless** | User-friendly | Operator safety and environmental compliance |
| 8 | **Good Filterability** | Low residue after filtration | Cost-effective recycling |
| 9 | **High Oxidation Resistance** | Long service life | Reduces frequent replacement |
| 10 | **Suitable Viscosity-Temp Curve** | Stable across temperature range | Consistent flushing performance |

## 2.3 Types / Classification of Dielectric Fluids

### A. **Hydrocarbon Oils (Most Commonly Used in Conventional EDM)**

1. **Transformer Oil**
   - Refined mineral oil, widely used in **die-sinking EDM**.
   - Dielectric strength: **30 kV/mm** (excellent).
   - Cheap, easily available.
   - **Drawback:** Low flash point (~140 °C), generates smoke.

2. **EDM Oil (Specialized)** — e.g., *EDM-300, EDM-200 (Mitsubishi), Castrol EDM*
   - Specifically engineered for EDM.
   - Low viscosity, high flash point (~110 °C).
   - Better debris suspension and filtration.

3. **Kerosene / Spindle Oil**
   - Used in **small-scale / hobby EDM** and some micro-EDM applications.
   - Low viscosity → excellent flushing in micro-gaps.

### B. **Synthetic / Deionized Water-Based Fluids (Used in Wire-EDM and Sinker EDM)**

1. **Deionized Water**
   - Used in **Wire-EDM (WEDM)** as the primary dielectric.
   - Very high cooling capacity.
   - **Issue:** Lower dielectric strength, requires frequent deionization.

2. **Water-Based Emulsions**
   - Mix of deionized water + additives (corrosion inhibitors, surfactants).
   - Used in **CNC die-sinking EDM** with high flushing requirements.

### C. **Gaseous Dielectrics (Advanced / Dry EDM)**
- Used in **Dry EDM / Near-Dry EDM**.
- Gases like **oxygen, nitrogen, argon** at high pressure.
- Eliminates fluid waste, environment-friendly.
- **Limitation:** Limited flushing, not suitable for deep cavities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** WEDM almost always uses **deionized water**. Conventional Sinker EDM uses **EDM oil / transformer oil**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Symbol Description | Units | Use in EDM |
|---------|--------------------|----|------------|
| $V_b = E_b \cdot d$ | $V_b$ = Breakdown voltage, $E_b$ = Dielectric strength, $d$ = Gap distance | $V_b$ in kV, $E_b$ in kV/mm, $d$ in mm | Determines minimum voltage needed for spark to jump |
| $MRR = K \cdot I \cdot t$ | $MRR$ = Material Removal Rate, $K$ = constant, $I$ = current, $t$ = time | mm³/min | EDM performance — depends on spark energy |
| $E_{spark} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$ | $E_{spark}$ = Energy per spark, $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $t_{on}$ = pulse-on time | Joules (J) or mJ | Higher energy = deeper crater |
| $SR = \frac{V_s}{V_s + V_g}$ | $SR$ = Spark Ratio, $V_s$ = sparks (discharge), $V_g$ = gap (no discharge) | Dimensionless | Indicates machining efficiency (ideal: 1) |
| $TW = \frac{V_{tw}}{V_{tw} + V_s + V_g}$ | $TW$ = Tool Wear Ratio, $V_{tw}$ = tool wear volume | Dimensionless | Lower is better (< 1%) |
| $R_a = f(V, I, t_{on})$ | $R_a$ = Surface roughness | µm | Lower current = better finish |
| $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed by dielectric | Joules | Cooling capacity |
| $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for flushing flow | Dimensionless | Laminar ($Re < 2300$), Turbulent ($Re > 4000$) |
| $\varepsilon_r = \frac{C_d}{C_0}$ | Relative permittivity | Dimensionless | Measure of dielectric property |
| $\sigma = n \cdot q \cdot \mu_e$ | Conductivity (here $\sigma \to 0$ in dielectric) | S/m | Dielectric should have $\sigma \approx 0$ |
| $E_b = \frac{V_{bd}}{d_{gap}}$ | Electric field at breakdown | kV/mm | Gap withstanding capacity |
| $t_{on} = \frac{E_{spark}}{V \cdot I}$ | Pulse-on time | µs | Spark duration |
| $P = V \cdot I$ | Pulse power | Watts | Determines MRR |
| $\eta_{cool} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{E_{spark}}$ | Cooling efficiency | % | Effectiveness of dielectric |
| $\Delta P_{flush} = \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3}$ | Pressure drop in flushing (parallel plate gap model) | Pa | Pumping power required |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All formulas above are derived from first principles of electrical discharge machining physics. They are standard and have appeared in KTU model question papers.

## 2.5 Breakdown Mechanism of Dielectric Fluid (Step-by-Step Logic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand *how* a dielectric fluid breaks down to allow a spark. Here is the logical breakdown:

1. **Initial State:** Tool and workpiece are separated by a small gap (typically **0.01 to 0.05 mm** or **10–50 µm**). The dielectric fluid in the gap is in its **insulating state** — no free charge carriers.

2. **Voltage Application:** A potential difference (typically **80–250 V** open-circuit voltage) is applied across the gap. An electric field $E = V/d$ develops.

3. **Field Strength Buildup:** As the tool feed advances, the gap $d$ decreases, and $E$ increases exponentially.

4. **Threshold Reached:** When $E$ exceeds the **dielectric strength** $E_b$ of the fluid (~10 kV/mm), the fluid can no longer hold off the voltage.

5. **Ionization Cascade:** Free electrons (always present in small numbers) are accelerated by the strong field. They collide with neutral molecules, knocking out more electrons — an **avalanche ionization**.

6. **Plasma Channel Formation:** The ionized gas/fluid forms a narrow **plasma channel** of extremely low resistance (typically **0.1 to 1 Ω**).

7. **Spark Discharge:** Current surges through the channel (peak currents of **10–500 A**), and temperatures reach **8,000–20,000 °C**. Material is melted and vaporized at the discharge point.

8. **Pulse-Off:** The voltage is removed. The plasma collapses. The dielectric fluid **rapidly quenches** the plasma, preventing sustained arcing.

9. **Debris Removal:** The flowing dielectric carries the molten/vaporized debris out of the gap.

10. **Recycle:** The fluid is filtered, cooled, and recirculated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The entire cycle (steps 1–9) for a single spark takes only **1 to 500 µs** depending on the pulse setting.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Breakdown Voltage and Gap Distance

This derivation is a high-frequency KTU exam question (often 7 marks).

**Given:**
- Dielectric strength of EDM oil: $E_b = 12$ kV/mm
- Spark gap: $d$ in mm
- Required: Voltage at which breakdown occurs.

**Step 1:** Recall the definition of dielectric strength (electric field at breakdown):

$$E_b = \frac{V_{bd}}{d}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for the breakdown voltage $V_{bd}$:

$$V_{bd} = E_b \cdot d$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values. For $d = 0.025$ mm (typical EDM gap):

$$V_{bd} = 12 \text{ kV/mm} \times 0.025 \text{ mm}$$

$$V_{bd} = 0.30 \text{ kV} = 300 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: When the open-circuit voltage exceeds 300 V across a 25 µm gap filled with EDM oil, the fluid will break down and a spark will jump.

**Generalization:** For any gap and any dielectric,

$$V_{bd} = E_b \cdot d$$

This is a **linear** relationship. Doubling the gap doubles the voltage needed.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Per Spark and Its Effect on MRR

**Given:**
- Discharge voltage: $V = 25$ V (typical working voltage in EDM)
- Peak current: $I = 10$ A
- Pulse-on time: $t_{on} = 100 \, \mu s = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- Required: Energy per spark, and MRR per spark.

**Step 1:** Energy per spark is given by the electrical energy equation:

$$E_{spark} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$E_{spark} = 25 \text{ V} \times 10 \text{ A} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

$$E_{spark} = 25 \times 10 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$E_{spark} = 0.025 \text{ J} = 25 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 3:** Material removal per spark (using empirical relation for steel):

$$V_{crater} \approx K \cdot E_{spark}$$

where $K \approx 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^3 / \text{mJ}$ for steel.

$$V_{crater} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 0.0125 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4:** MRR assuming pulse frequency $f = 1000$ Hz (1000 sparks/sec):

$$MRR = V_{crater} \times f = 0.0125 \times 1000 = 12.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to mm³/min:

$$MRR = 12.5 \times 60 = 750 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Interpretation:** A 25 mJ spark removes about 12.5 mm³ of steel per minute under these conditions. The dielectric's role here is to **sustain** this high-energy spark and then **remove the debris**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cooling Capacity of the Dielectric

**Given:**
- Mass flow rate of dielectric: $\dot{m} = 5$ kg/min
- Specific heat of EDM oil: $c_p = 2.0$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Temperature rise of dielectric: $\Delta T = 10$ K

**Required:** Heat removed by the dielectric per minute.

**Step 1:** Use the heat transfer equation:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$Q = 5 \text{ kg/min} \times 2.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times 10 \text{ K}$$

$$Q = 100 \text{ kJ/min}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to kW:

$$Q = \frac{100}{60} \text{ kW} = 1.667 \text{ kW}$$

**Interpretation:** The dielectric fluid is removing **~1.67 kW of heat** continuously. This cooling is essential to prevent the workpiece and tool from overheating. If flow is inadequate, MRR drops and surface quality deteriorates.

## 3.4 Derivation: Dielectric Selection Based on Gap Flushing

**Given:**
- Gap between tool and workpiece: $h = 0.05$ mm
- Gap width: $w = 10$ mm
- Length of cavity: $L = 20$ mm
- Desired flow: $Q = 1$ L/min = $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s
- Viscosity of EDM oil: $\mu = 5$ cP = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

**Required:** Pressure drop across the gap.

**Step 1:** Use the parallel-plate Poiseuille flow equation:

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3}$$

**Step 2:** Convert units:
- $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $L = 0.020$ m
- $Q = 1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s
- $w = 0.010$ m
- $h = 0.00005$ m → $h^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-13}$ m³

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.020 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{0.010 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-13}}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-8}}{1.25 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$\Delta P = 1.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 16 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: The pressure drop is enormous because the gap is **microscopic**. This is why **low-viscosity dielectric fluids** are preferred — to keep the required pump pressure manageable.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Dielectric Selection Decision Table

| Application | Recommended Dielectric | Reason |
|-------------|----------------------|--------|
| Die-sinking EDM (general) | EDM oil / Transformer oil | High dielectric strength, good cooling |
| Wire-EDM (WEDM) | Deionized water | Excellent cooling, easy filtration |
| Micro-EDM | Kerosene / Low-viscosity oil | Better flushing in micro-gaps |
| Dry EDM | Compressed gas (N₂, Ar) | No fluid waste, environment-friendly |
| Deep cavity EDM | Low-viscosity EDM oil + flushing holes | Avoids debris accumulation |
| Hard alloy machining (WC, Ti) | Specialized hydrocarbon oil | Prevents carbon deposition |
| Aerospace EDM (Ni-alloys) | Synthetic EDM fluid | Higher oxidation resistance |
| High MRR roughing | High-viscosity oil (better arc stability) | Stable spark generation |
| High-finish finishing | Deionized water or low-energy oil | Cleaner craters, less recast |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Dielectric Fluid Circulation System in EDM

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of the dielectric fluid circulation system in a typical **die-sinking EDM machine**. This is a block-level architecture (not a physical drawing) that maps the interactions between components.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Working Tank - Holds Dielectric Fluid]
    nodeB[Filter Unit - Removes Debris Particles]
    nodeC[Cooling Unit - Heat Exchanger]
    nodeD[High Pressure Pump - 0.5 to 5 kg/cm2]
    nodeE[Electrode Gap - Tool vs Workpiece]
    nodeF[Spark Discharge - Plasma Channel]
    nodeG[Dielectric Reservoir - Fresh Fluid]
    nodeH[Contamination Sensor]
    nodeI[Flow Control Valve]
    nodeJ[Nozzle / Flushing Jet]
    nodeK[Return Channel - Used Fluid]
    nodeL[Recirculation Pipe Network]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeA
    nodeG -.Refill.-> nodeA
    nodeH -.Monitor.-> nodeB
    nodeH -.Monitor.-> nodeC
    nodeH -.Monitor.-> nodeI

    classDef process fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef sensor fill:#7C2D12,stroke:#9A3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef supply fill:#065F46,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeI,nodeJ,nodeE,nodeF,nodeK,nodeL process
    class nodeH sensor
    class nodeG supply
```

**Description of the Flow:**

1. The dielectric fluid is stored in the **Working Tank (nodeA)**.
2. It passes through a **Filter Unit (nodeB)** to remove eroded metal particles.
3. The **Cooling Unit (nodeC)** removes accumulated heat via a heat exchanger.
4. The **High-Pressure Pump (nodeD)** pressurizes the fluid to the required level.
5. The **Flow Control Valve (nodeI)** regulates the flow rate.
6. The **Nozzle (nodeJ)** directs the fluid into the gap.
7. Inside the **Electrode Gap (nodeE)**, the **Spark Discharge (nodeF)** occurs.
8. Used fluid returns via the **Return Channel (nodeK)** and **Pipe Network (nodeL)**.
9. The **Contamination Sensor (nodeH)** continuously monitors dielectric quality.
10. The **Reservoir (nodeG)** provides fresh fluid for makeup.
