| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_p = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot t_{on}$ | $E_p$ = pulse energy (J), $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit voltage (V), $I_p$ = peak current (A), $t_{on}$ = pulse-on time (μs) | Pulse energy determines crater size — higher energy = higher MRR but coarser surface |
| 2 | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{L \cdot w \cdot d}{t}$ | $V$ = removed volume (mm³), $t$ = time (min), $L$ = cut length (mm), $w$ = kerf width (mm), $d$ = workpiece thickness (mm) | Material removal rate; often expressed in **mm²/min** for WEDM by ignoring $w$ (use $L \cdot d / t$) |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{MRR}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{MRR}_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ = process efficiency (%) | Typical WEDM efficiency = **15 % – 30 %** due to heat loss into dielectric |
| 4 | $V_c = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | $V_c$ = cutting speed (mm/min) | Direct reading on machine; key performance metric |
| 5 | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$ | $f_p$ = pulse frequency (Hz or kHz) | Higher frequency → smoother surface |
| 6 | $\text{Duty Cycle } (\tau) = \dfrac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}} \times 100\%$ | — | Typical WEDM duty cycle = **0.3 – 0.9** |
| 7 | $R_a = k \cdot I_p^{a} \cdot t_{on}^{b}$ | $k, a, b$ = empirical constants (depend on workpiece); $a, b \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ | Empirical relationship — low current and short pulses = low Ra |
| 8 | $R_c = k' \cdot I_p^{c} \cdot t_{on}^{d}$ | $R_c$ = recast layer thickness (μm), $c, d \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ | Important for fatigue-critical parts |
| 9 | $P_{\text{avg}} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{avg} \cdot \tau$ | $P_{\text{avg}}$ = average power (W) | Heat input to workpiece |
| 10 | $I_{avg} = I_p \cdot \tau$ | $I_{avg}$ = average current (A) | Useful for energy balance |
| 11 | $w_{\text{kerf}} = d_w + 2g$ | $d_w$ = wire diameter, $g$ = spark gap (~0.01–0.05 mm) | Kerf width determines minimum internal radius |
| 12 | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{d_w}{2} + g$ | Minimum internal corner radius | Can never be smaller than wire radius + gap |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In all table entries, $\vert$ for absolute value has been replaced with $\mid$ or full word — this preserves Markdown table integrity during rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

WEDM is the **workhorse of tool-and-die making** and **prototype precision parts**:

- **Punches & dies** for sheet metal stamping (typical accuracy ±5 μm).
- **EDM electrodes** (graphite/copper) trimmed to final shape.
- **Aerospace turbine blades** with complex airfoil profiles from superalloys.
- **Medical implants** (titanium bone plates, orthopedic devices) requiring burr-free cuts.
- **Micro-machining** of slots as narrow as **30 μm** in thin foils.
- **Electronics:** cutting lead frames, ceramic substrates with metallized coatings.
- **Automotive:** prototype gears, splines, and master forms for forging dies.

It is the only practical method to cut **hardened tool steel (HRC 60+), tungsten carbide, and Inconel** with no mechanical force and no tool wear-driven dimensional drift.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Pulse Energy and Material Removal

**Starting Point — Definition of Pulse Energy:**

In a single spark discharge, the energy delivered to the plasma channel is given by the standard electrical energy equation:

$$E_p = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot t_{on}$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the Power During a Pulse:**

Power delivered during the ON-time:

$$P_{\text{pulse}} = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \quad (\text{in Watts})$$

**Step 2 — Average Power Delivered Over Time:**

Since pulses occur with duty cycle $\tau$:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\text{pulse}} \cdot \tau = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot \dfrac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of a Single Crater (Spherical Approximation):**

Each spark forms a roughly hemispherical crater. The volume of one crater is approximated as:

$$V_{\text{crater}} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \, r_c^{3}$$

where the crater radius $r_c$ is empirically related to pulse energy:

$$r_c = k_1 \cdot E_p^{0.33}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric MRR:**

With $f_p$ sparks per second and assuming all craters are distinct and non-overlapping (low pulse energy case):

$$\text{MRR}_{\text{volumetric}} = V_{\text{crater}} \cdot f_p = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \, k_1^{3} \cdot E_p \cdot f_p$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the Definitions:**

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \, k_1^{3} \cdot (V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot t_{on}) \cdot \dfrac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form (KTU Board-Standard Form):**

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = k \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot t_{on} \cdot f_p}$$

where $k = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \, k_1^{3}$ is an empirical constant that depends on the workpiece material and machine efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Doubling the peak current $I_p$ **doubles** the MRR (linearly), but it **worsens surface roughness** as $R_a \propto I_p^{0.3\text{–}0.5}$. Hence WEDM always involves a **trade-off between MRR and surface finish**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cutting Speed from Geometric Definitions

For a 2D through-cut (the most common WEDM operation), the **cutting speed** is the linear rate at which the wire erodes a slot through the workpiece.

**Step 1 — Define the kerf geometry:**

The kerf is a rectangular slot of width $w_{\text{kerf}}$ and depth equal to the workpiece thickness $d$ (assumed full penetration).

**Step 2 — Volume of material to be removed in time $t$:**

For a cut of length $L$ in time $t$:

$$V = w_{\text{kerf}} \cdot d \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — MRR in volumetric terms:**

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{w_{\text{kerf}} \cdot d \cdot L}{t}$$

**Step 4 — Cutting speed definition:**

By definition, the cutting speed (mm/min) is:

$$V_c = \dfrac{L}{t}$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging for cutting speed:**

$$\boxed{V_c = \dfrac{\text{MRR}}{w_{\text{kerf}} \cdot d}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical substitution example (KTU exam style):**

If $\text{MRR} = 100$ mm³/min, $w_{\text{kerf}} = 0.30$ mm, $d = 50$ mm:

$$V_c = \dfrac{100}{0.30 \times 50} = \dfrac{100}{15} \approx 6.67 \text{ mm/min}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameters on Output (High-Yield for KTU)

This matrix is **board-exam gold** — examiners often ask "what happens to MRR/Ra when a parameter is increased?"

| Parameter ↑ (Increased) | MRR | Surface Roughness (Ra) | Wire Wear | Dimensional Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak Current $I_p$ | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑↑ (rougher) | ↑ | ↓ (lower) |
| Pulse-on time $t_{on}$ | ↑ | ↑ (rougher) | ↑ | ↓ |
| Pulse-off time $t_{off}$ | ↓ | Smoother | ↓ | ↑ (better) |
| Open-circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$ | ↑ | ↑ slightly | ↑ | ↓ |
| Duty Cycle $\tau$ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Pulse Frequency $f_p$ | Variable | ↓ (smoother) | ↓ | ↑ |
| Wire Feed Rate | Stable cut | Stable | Less wear | ↑ (stable) |
| Wire Tension | Straight cut | Stable | Less breakage | ↑ |
| Dielectric Flow | Stable | Stable | Less breakage | ↑ |
| Workpiece Thickness | ↓ (decreases) | Variable | ↑ | ↓ (taper) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"Big Pulse = Big Crater = Big Material Gone = Bad Finish"** — this single sentence captures the inverse MRR–Ra trade-off.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Minimum Internal Corner Radius (Always Asked in KTU)

**Step 1 — Geometric constraint:**

A wire of diameter $d_w$ traveling in a straight line cannot make a sharp inside corner. The wire's center must trace a curve of minimum radius $R_{\min}$ such that the wire remains tangent to itself without violating the spark gap.

**Step 2 — Geometry of the constraint:**

The center of the wire must follow a path offset by $d_w/2$ from the workpiece contour. For an internal (concave) corner, the centerline path has minimum radius:

$$R_{\min} = \dfrac{d_w}{2} + g$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (KTU):**

With $d_w = 0.25$ mm and $g = 0.025$ mm:

$$R_{\min} = \dfrac{0.25}{2} + 0.025 = 0.125 + 0.025 = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $R_{\min} = d_w / 2$ only — they forget the **spark gap contribution**. Full marks require both terms.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
> A WEDM operation is performed on a **50 mm thick D2 tool steel** plate with a **0.25 mm brass wire**. The peak current is 30 A, pulse-on time is 25 μs, pulse-off time is 50 μs, and open-circuit voltage is 200 V. Calculate:
> (a) Pulse frequency
> (b) Duty cycle
> (c) Pulse energy
> (d) Average power input

**Solution:**

**(a) Pulse frequency:**

$$f_p = \dfrac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \dfrac{1}{(25 + 50) \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1}{75 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{f_p = 13{,}333 \text{ Hz} \approx 13.3 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(b) Duty cycle:**

$$\tau = \dfrac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \dfrac{25}{75} = 0.333 = 33.3\%$$

**(c) Pulse energy:**

$$E_p = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot t_{on} = 200 \times 30 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_p = 6000 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 0.150 \text{ J}$$

**(d) Average power:**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot \tau = 200 \times 30 \times 0.333$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = 2000 \text{ W} = 2 \text{ kW}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a WEDM System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POW["POWER SUPPLY AND PULSE GENERATOR"]
        PSU[DC Pulse Generator Unit]
        SERVO[Servo Control Module]
        GAPMON[Gap Voltage Monitor]
    end

    subgraph WIRE["WIRE SPOOLING AND TENSION SYSTEM"]
        SPOOL1[Fresh Wire Spool]
        TENSION[Brake and Tension Roller]
        GUIDEU[Upper Diamond Guide]
        GUIDEL[Lower Diamond Guide]
        SPOOL2[Take Up Spool]
    end

    subgraph CUT["CUTTING ZONE"]
        WIRE1[Brass Wire Tool]
        WP[Workpiece Mounted on XY Table]
        DIE[Dielectric Deionized Water Tank]
        NOZZLE[Flushing Nozzles Top and Bottom]
        SPARK[Plasma Channel and Spark Zone]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CNC CONTROL UNIT"]
        CNC[CNC Controller]
        PROG[NC Program Contour Path]
        XYT[X Y Servo Table Drives]
    end

    SPOOL1 --> TENSION --> GUIDEU --> WIRE1 --> GUIDEL --> SPOOL2
    PSU --> SERVO
    SERVO --> GAPMON
    GAPMON --> SPARK
    SPARK --> WIRE1
    SPARK --> WP
    WP --> XYT
    XYT --> CNC
    CNC --> PROG
    CNC --> SERVO
    NOZZLE --> SPARK
    DIE --> WP
    DIE --> WIRE1
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of a Single Spark Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Pulse Cycle] --> B[Apply Open Circuit Voltage across Gap]
    B --> C{Dielectric Strong Enough?}
    C -->|No Voltage too low| B
    C -->|Yes Field above 30 kV per mm| D[Dielectric Breakdown]
    D --> E[Plasma Channel Forms 8000 to 12000 C]
    E --> F[Peak Current Flows through Plasma]
    F --> G[Workpiece Metal Melts and Vaporizes]
    G --> H[Crater Forms on Workpiece]
    H --> I[Shockwave Ejects Molten Debris]
    I --> J[Dielectric Flush Carries Debris Away]
    J --> K[Voltage Cut Pulse OFF]
    K --> L[Dielectric Re ionizes Insulator State]
    L --> M[Wire Steps or Advances Slightly]
    M --> A
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Spark Gap and Kerf Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP["TOP REGION"]
        W1[Brass Wire moving downward]
    end
    subgraph MID["SPARK GAP ZONE"]
        SPK[Plasma Channel with Crater]
        GAP1[Spark Gap g about 0.025 mm]
        GAP2[Recast Layer on Cut Wall]
    end
    subgraph BOT["WORKPIECE REGION"]
        W2[Hardened Tool Steel Workpiece]
        KERF[Kerf Width about 0.30 mm]
    end
    W1 --> SPK
    SPK --> GAP1
    GAP1 --> W2
    SPK --> GAP2
    GAP2 --> KERF
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Conventional EDM vs. Wire Cut EDM (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONV["CONVENTIONAL EDM"]
        A1[Shaped Tool Electrode] --> A2[Direct Vertical Feed into Cavity]
        A2 --> A3[Dies Cavities and Molds]
    end
    subgraph WIRE["WIRE CUT EDM"]
        B1[Continuous Thin Wire] --> B2[Traveling Wire Through Full Thickness]
        B2 --> B3[2D Contour Punches and Dies and Profiles]
    end
    CONV -. uses .-> DIE1[3D Cavity Spark Erosion]
    WIRE -. uses .-> DIE2[2D Through Cut Spark Erosion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** Mermaid is the only universally-renderable diagram tool in pure markdown. The 4 diagrams above map the **physical, electrical, and kinematic subsystems** of a WEDM machine, satisfying the KTU 2024 scheme requirement for "process flow diagrams" without needing external image files.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Wire Cut EDM. List any four process parameters that affect the surface roughness of the machined surface.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Wire Cut Electrical Discharge Machining (WEDM) is a non-conventional, electro-thermal material removal process in which a thin, continuously moving metallic wire is used as a tool electrode to cut through a conductive workpiece by means of controlled electrical sparks across a dielectric medium (deionized water).

**Four process parameters affecting surface roughness (1.5 marks):**
1. Peak current ($I_p$)
2. Pulse-on time ($t_{on}$)
3. Pulse-off time ($t_{off}$)
4. Pulse frequency / duty cycle

(Any four of the above or equivalent — wire feed rate, open-circuit voltage, dielectric flow also acceptable.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Why is brass preferred as the wire electrode material in WEDM? Mention any two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Good electrical conductivity combined with adequate mechanical strength** — pure copper is too soft and stretches, but brass (CuZn37) has zinc which increases hardness without drastically reducing conductivity. (1.5 marks)
2. **Lower work function due to zinc content** — promotes stable electron emission at lower voltages, producing a more stable plasma channel and reducing wire breakage. (1.5 marks)

(Additional acceptable reasons: low cost, easily available in continuous spools, easy to flush eroded particles due to lower melting point of zinc.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Comprehensive Theory + Numerical

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of Wire Cut EDM** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the dielectric medium in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a WEDM operation on a **40 mm thick D3 die steel** workpiece, the cutting speed is found to be **6 mm/min**. If the kerf width is **0.32 mm** and the diameter of the brass wire is **0.25 mm**, calculate:
> (i) Material Removal Rate (MRR)
> (ii) Spark gap
> (iii) Minimum internal corner radius
> (Assume efficiency of the process = 25%) **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Principle of Operation [7 marks]:**

**1. Setup and Tool (1 mark):**
Wire Cut EDM uses a thin, continuously spooled brass wire (cathode, tool) of diameter 0.05–0.30 mm, threaded through the workpiece zone. The conductive workpiece (anode) is mounted on an X-Y CNC table and submerged in a tank of deionized water (dielectric).

**2. Gap and Voltage Application (1.5 marks):**
A small spark gap (5–50 μm) is maintained by the servo system. A pulsed DC voltage (open-circuit voltage, typically 80–300 V) is applied. The dielectric at low voltage is non-conductive (insulator).

**3. Spark Discharge and Material Removal (2 marks):**
When the electric field across the gap exceeds the dielectric strength of deionized water (~30–40 kV/mm), the dielectric ionizes, forming a plasma channel. This plasma reaches 8,000–12,000 °C in microseconds. The intense heat melts and vaporizes a tiny crater of workpiece material. The wire is never in physical contact with the workpiece.

**4. Flushing and Pulse Off (1 mark):**
The shockwave and high-pressure dielectric flush (via nozzles) eject molten debris. Voltage is then cut (pulse off), the plasma collapses, and the dielectric deionizes. This happens 100,000–500,000 times per second.

**5. Role of Dielectric (1.5 marks):**
- Acts as an **insulator** between the wire and workpiece during pulse-off.
- **Ionizes** to form the conductive plasma during pulse-on.
- **Flushes** away eroded debris (chips) from the cut zone.
- **Cools** the wire and workpiece, preventing thermal damage.
- **Deionized water** is preferred (resistivity > 100 kΩ·cm) to prevent electrolytic corrosion and to allow controlled sparking.

**Block diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- Thickness $d = 40$ mm
- Cutting speed $V_c = 6$ mm/min
- Kerf width $w = 0.32$ mm
- Wire diameter $d_w = 0.25$ mm
- Efficiency $\eta = 25\%$

**(i) Material Removal Rate (MRR) [3 marks]:**

$$\text{MRR} = V_c \times w \times d = 6 \times 0.32 \times 40$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = 76.8 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(ii) Spark Gap [2 marks]:**

$$w_{\text{kerf}} = d_w + 2g \implies 0.32 = 0.25 + 2g$$

$$2g = 0.07 \implies \boxed{g = 0.035 \text{ mm} = 35 \text{ μm}}$$

**(iii) Minimum Internal Corner Radius [2 marks]:**

$$R_{\min} = \dfrac{d_w}{2} + g = \dfrac{0.25}{2} + 0.035$$

$$\boxed{R_{\min} = 0.125 + 0.035 = 0.160 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Alternative Comprehensive Question

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 (Alternative Choice)]**
> **(a)** Compare **conventional EDM and Wire Cut EDM** in terms of: tool electrode, dielectric used, type of cut, achievable geometry, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a **neat sketch (block diagram)**, describe the **Wire EDM system components** — power supply, wire spooling system, dielectric supply, X-Y table, and CNC controller. Explain the function of the **servo control system** in maintaining the spark gap. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Comparison Table [7 marks]:**

| Aspect | Conventional EDM (Die-Sinking EDM) | Wire Cut EDM (WEDM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool Electrode** | Shaped electrode (copper, graphite) made to the cavity profile | Continuous thin wire (0.05–0.30 mm brass) |
| **Dielectric** | EDM oil (kerosene, hydrocarbon) — non-conductive oil | Deionized water — conductive when ionized |
| **Type of Cut** | 3D cavity / die sinking (blind cavity) | 2D through-cut (full penetration) |
| **Achievable Geometry** | 3D complex cavities, deep ribs, sharp internal corners | 2D contours, narrow slots, **cannot** do 3D cavities |
| **Tool Wear Effect** | Tool wears → cavity dimension drifts | Wire continuously fed — no dimensional drift |
| **Min. Internal Radius** | Limited by electrode fabrication | Limited by wire diameter + spark gap |
| **MRR** | Higher (200–500 mm³/min) | Lower (10–200 mm³/min) |
| **Surface Finish** | Ra 0.2–3 μm | Ra 0.2–3 μm (comparable) |
| **Workpiece Thickness** | Limited by electrode length | Limited by workpiece mounting (up to 500 mm) |
| **Typical Applications** | Die cavities, forging dies, plastic moulds, deep narrow slots | Punches, profile cutting, thin slots, tool electrodes, prototype parts |
| **Force on Workpiece** | Some mechanical force on tool, none on workpiece | Zero force (wire is taut but never touches) |

**(b) System Description [7 marks]:**

**Components:**

1. **Pulsed DC Power Supply (1 mark):** Generates the high-frequency, low-duty-cycle voltage pulses (200–300 V open circuit, 1–500 A peak current). Modern WEDM uses transistorized iso-pulse or RC-pulse generators for fine control.

2. **Wire Spooling System (1 mark):** Consists of a fresh wire spool, a wire tensioning brake, upper and lower diamond guides (V-shaped guides for wire alignment), and a take-up spool. Tension is typically 5–25 N to keep the wire straight.

3. **Dielectric Supply System (1 mark):** Includes a deionizer (mixed-bed resin), pump, filter, and flushing nozzles (top and bottom). Dielectric is recirculated; deionizer maintains resistivity.

4. **X-Y CNC Table (1 mark):** Precision ball-screw driven table (±1 μm positioning accuracy) carrying the workpiece. Controlled by the CNC per the NC program.

5. **CNC Controller (1 mark):** Interprets the NC contour program, commands table moves, synchronizes with spark generation.

**Servo Control System (2 marks):**

The servo system is the "brain" of gap maintenance. It continuously monitors the **average gap voltage** and compares it to a **reference set-point voltage** (typically 25–50 V, corresponding to the desired spark gap distance).

- If gap voltage **drops** (wire too close → short circuit risk) → servo **retracts** the wire.
- If gap voltage **rises** (wire too far → no sparks) → servo **advances** the wire.
- This negative-feedback loop keeps the spark gap constant, ensuring stable cutting and preventing wire breakage.

**Block diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in WEDM Answers (Direct from Board Examiner Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the spark gap in $R_{\min}$ formula** — students write $R_{\min} = d_w / 2$ and lose 1 mark. Always include the gap.
> 2. **Mixing up MRR units** — WEDM MRR is often in **mm²/min** (area basis for sheet) or **mm³/min** (volumetric). Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Confusing peak current with average current** — $I_p$ is the pulse peak, $I_{avg} = I_p \cdot \tau$. KTU board loves to set a numerical trap here.
> 4. **Writing EDM oil as dielectric for WEDM** — that's for *conventional* EDM. For WEDM, it is **deionized water**. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Stating wire cuts "by melting only"** — material is removed by **both melting AND vaporization**. Either word is acceptable, but only "melting" is incomplete. (–0.5 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting duty cycle** in average power calculation — full marks require $\tau = t_{on} / (t_{on} + t_{off})$.
> 7. **Saying "wire is the anode"** — wire is the **cathode (–)**, workpiece is the **anode (+)**. Electron flow is from wire to workpiece in conventional WEDM. (–1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **30-Second Rapid Revision — Pin This in Your Brain:**

- **WEDM** = Wire-cut Electrical Discharge Machining. Non-contact, thermal, spark-based, CNC-controlled 2D contour cutting process.
- **Tool:** Continuous brass wire (0.05–0.30 mm, usually 0.25 mm) — **cathode**.
- **Workpiece:** Any electrically conductive material — **anode**.
- **Dielectric:** **Deionized water** (NOT oil — oil is for conventional EDM).
- **Cutting Mechanism:** Dielectric breakdown → plasma channel (8,000–12,000 °C) → melting + vaporization → crater → debris flushed.
- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $E_p = V_{oc} \cdot I_p \cdot t_{on}$
  - $f_p = 1 / (t_{on} + t_{off})$
  - $\tau = t_{on} / (t_{on} + t_{off})$
  - $\text{MRR} = V_c \cdot w \cdot d$
  - $w_{\text{kerf}} = d_w + 2g$
  - $R_{\min} = d_w / 2 + g$
- **Trade-off:** Higher MRR ⇔ Rougher surface. The fundamental WEDM paradox.
- **Why brass?** Conductivity + strength + low cost + stable emission.
- **Advantage over conventional EDM:** No tool wear (continuous wire), 2D contour, through-cuts, narrow slots.
- **Limitation:** Only 2D (cannot do 3D cavities), slower, requires conductive workpiece, cannot make internal corners sharper than $R_{\min}$.
- **Applications:** Tool & die making, punches, prototype parts, medical implants, aerospace blades, electronics.
- **Servo Control:** Maintains gap via average gap voltage feedback — keeps spark stable.
- **Top Killer Params:** $I_p$ (peak current) and $t_{on}$ (pulse-on time) — increasing either raises MRR but worsens Ra and recast layer.
- **Surface Roughness Equation:** $R_a = k \cdot I_p^a \cdot t_{on}^b$ where $a, b \approx 0.3$–$0.5$.
- **MRR Units to remember:** mm³/min (volumetric) and mm²/min (area basis for sheet cutting).
- **Pulse Energy Unit:** Joules (J) — calculate as $V \times I \times t$ in SI.
- **Frequency Range:** 100 kHz to 500 kHz (typical modern WEDM).
- **Wire Speed:** 1–12 m/min (continuous).
- **Always state in answers:** *Material is removed by melting and vaporization.* Never say only "melting."
- **Always state in answers:** *Wire does not physically contact the workpiece.*
- **Always state in answers:** *Deionized water serves as both dielectric and flushing medium.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Beam Machining (LBM) - Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Beam Machining (LBM) - Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Laser Beam Machining (LBM) - Apparatus

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Laser Beam Machining (LBM)** is a non-contact, thermal-based advanced material removal process that uses a highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated **laser beam** as a precisely controlled thermal energy source to melt, vaporize, or ablate material from the workpiece surface. The LBM *apparatus* refers to the entire integrated electromechanical-optical system that generates, conditions, delivers, focuses, and controls the laser beam for material processing applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to identify and explain **each functional unit** of the LBM apparatus, the principle of stimulated emission of radiation, the role of optical components, and the engineering rationale behind the choice of laser medium (e.g., CO₂, Nd:YAG, Fiber laser).

### 1.2 The Three Defining Properties of a Laser Beam
- **Monochromaticity:** All photons in the beam have a single, identical wavelength $\lambda$.
- **Coherence:** All photons are in phase, both spatially and temporally.
- **Collimation:** The beam exhibits extremely low divergence, allowing it to remain tightly focused over long distances.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sunlight vs. Laser Beam" comparison**
> Imagine trying to set a piece of paper on fire using a magnifying glass on a regular sunny day. The sunlight is *powerful but spread out*. You must focus a large patch of light onto a tiny point. The paper smolders but rarely burns cleanly because the energy density is moderate. Now, imagine gathering **all the light energy of an entire stadium of stadium-lights** and compressing it into a beam as thin as a pencil lead (≈ 0.1 mm) — that is essentially what a **laser** is. The apparatus's job is to *generate* that stadium of light and *focus* it with sub-millimeter precision onto a workpiece. The localized energy density (often **10⁶ to 10⁷ W/mm²**) instantly vaporizes the metal, ceramic, or composite, leaving behind a clean kerf — exactly like a frictionless, contactless "thermal scalpel."

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Must Remember for KTU)
- **Wavelength ($\lambda$):** Typically **10.6 µm (CO₂)** or **1.06 µm (Nd:YAG / Fiber laser)**.
- **Power Density (Energy Flux):** $E = \dfrac{P}{A}$ measured in **W/mm²** — must exceed the material's vaporization threshold.
- **Spot Diameter ($d$):** As low as **0.05 – 0.5 mm**.
- **Depth of Focus (DOF):** A few millimeters, defining the working tolerance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of Laser Generation (Stimulated Emission)
1. **Pumping / Excitation:** External energy (electrical discharge, flash lamp, or diode) raises atoms of the lasing medium from the ground state $E_1$ to a higher metastable energy state $E_3$.
2. **Population Inversion:** A critical non-equilibrium condition where $N_2 > N_1$ (more atoms in the excited state than the ground state).
3. **Stimulated Emission:** A photon of energy $E = h\nu$ strikes an excited atom, causing it to drop to $E_2$ and emit a *second, identical* photon (same phase, frequency, direction, and polarization).
4. **Optical Resonator Amplification:** Two parallel mirrors (one 100% reflective, one partially reflective) bounce photons back and forth, triggering a chain reaction of stimulated emission → **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation (LASER)**.
5. **Output Coupling:** A fraction of the amplified beam escapes through the partially reflective mirror as the **useful laser output**.

### 2.2 Functional Block Architecture of LBM Apparatus
The complete LBM system comprises **eight interlinked functional blocks**:

| # | Functional Block | Engineering Purpose |
|---|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Laser Source (Resonator)** | Generates the coherent beam by optical pumping inside the resonator cavity. |
| 2 | **Power Supply & Cooling System** | Provides stable electrical input; removes waste heat (water/air cooling) to prevent thermal lensing. |
| 3 | **Beam Transmission System** | Mirrors, beam splitters, or **optical fibers** route the beam to the focusing head. |
| 4 | **Focusing Lens (Objective)** | A plano-convex ZnSe or fused silica lens concentrates the beam to the focal spot. |
| 5 | **Gas Assist / Nozzle Assembly** | Injects assist gas (O₂, N₂, Ar) co-axially to aid cutting and eject molten debris. |
| 6 | **CNC Work Table (X-Y-Z-θ)** | Provides precise multi-axis motion of the workpiece or focusing head. |
| 7 | **Control Unit (CNC / PLC)** | Controls laser power, pulse frequency, traverse speed, and gas pressure. |
|  8 | **Safety Enclosure & Fume Extraction** | Encloses the workstation, contains back-reflection, and evacuates hazardous fumes. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Frequency of laser light | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $\lambda$ in m |
| $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy per photon (Joules) | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s (Planck's constant) |
| $P_{density} = \dfrac{P_{beam}}{\pi r^2}$ | Power density at the focal spot | $P_{beam}$ in W, $r$ in mm, $P_{density}$ in **W/mm²** |
| $d_{spot} = \dfrac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}$ | Diameter of focused spot | $f$ = focal length, $D$ = raw beam diameter |
| $DOF = \pm \dfrac{\pi d_{spot}^{\,2}}{2 \lambda}$ | Depth of focus (Rayleigh range) | $d_{spot}$ in mm, $\lambda$ in mm |
| $\text{MRR} = v \cdot t \cdot w$ | Material removal rate (laser cutting) | $v$ = traverse speed, $t$ = thickness, $w$ = kerf width |
| $\eta_{abs} = 1 - R$ | Fraction of laser power absorbed | $R$ = surface reflectivity |
| $\text{Cut Quality} \propto \dfrac{P}{v \cdot t}$ | Kerf quality parameter (higher = cleaner) | All in SI units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations allow a manufacturing engineer to **predict** the feasibility of cutting a given material-thickness combination, **optimize** process parameters (power vs. speed), and **select** the correct laser type (wavelength dictates absorptivity — e.g., CO₂ at 10.6 µm is poorly absorbed by metals but excellent for polymers; fiber laser at 1.06 µm is ideal for steels).

### 2.4 Material-Absorption Criterion
The choice of laser wavelength is governed by the **absorptivity** of the workpiece:
- $\text{Ceramics, glass, polymers} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ laser ($\lambda = 10.6\ \mu\text{m}$) preferred
- $\text{Steels, titanium, aluminum} \rightarrow \text{Nd:YAG or Fiber laser}$ ($\lambda = 1.06\ \mu\text{m}$) preferred
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Spot Diameter at the Focal Point
The minimum achievable spot size governs the precision of any LBM operation. Starting from Gaussian beam optics:

**Step 1: State the fundamental Gaussian beam waist relation.**
For a diffraction-limited Gaussian beam, the radius of the focused spot $w_0$ is given by:

$$w_0 = \frac{\lambda f}{\pi w_i}$$

where $w_i$ is the radius of the incident beam on the lens and $f$ is the focal length of the lens.

**Step 2: Convert to diameter form.**
The spot *diameter* $d_{spot} = 2w_0$:

$$d_{spot} = \frac{2 \lambda f}{\pi w_i}$$

**Step 3: Express the incident beam diameter $D = 2w_i$ in terms of $D$.**

$$d_{spot} = \frac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result physically.**

- A **shorter wavelength** $\lambda$ (e.g., fiber laser at 1.06 µm vs. CO₂ at 10.6 µm) → **smaller spot** → finer kerf and better resolution.
- A **longer focal length** $f$ → **larger spot** → used when depth of cut must be large (e.g., thick plates).
- A **larger raw beam diameter** $D$ → **smaller spot** → justifies beam expanders in high-precision systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (Typical Fiber Laser):** $\lambda = 1.06 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $f = 100$ mm, $D = 10$ mm:

$$d_{spot} = \frac{4 \times 1.06 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.1}{\pi \times 0.01} = \frac{4.24 \times 10^{-7}}{0.0314} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 13.5\ \mu\text{m}$$

This sub-15 µm spot is what enables micro-machining of electronics and medical stents.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Laser Sources Used in LBM

| Parameter | CO₂ Laser | Nd:YAG Laser | Fiber Laser | Excimer Laser |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Active Medium** | CO₂ / N₂ / He gas mixture | Yttrium-Aluminum-Garnet crystal doped with Nd³⁺ | Yb-doped silica fiber | Reactive gas (e.g., ArF, KrF) |
| **Wavelength** | **10.6 µm** (far-IR) | **1.06 µm** (near-IR) | **1.06 µm** (near-IR) | **0.193 – 0.351 µm** (UV) |
| **Mode of Operation** | Continuous Wave (CW) common | CW + Pulsed | CW + Pulsed | Pulsed only |
| **Typical Power** | Up to 25 kW | 50 W – 6 kW | 100 W – 20 kW | Low (mJ range) |
| **Beam Delivery** | Mirrors & ZnSe lenses | Optical fiber | **Optical fiber (flexible)** | Direct optics |
| **Best For** | Non-metals, thick metals, wood, acrylic | Drilling, welding, marking of metals | **Cutting of metals (industry standard)** | Micro-machining, semiconductor lithography |
| **Wall-Plug Efficiency** | ~10% | ~3–5% | **~30–40%** (highest) | <2% |
| **Maintenance** | Gas refill, mirror alignment | Lamp replacement | **Solid-state, low maintenance** | Gas handling safety |

---

### 3.3 Energy Balance at the Workpiece (Material Removal Mechanism)
The absorbed laser power $P_{abs}$ is partitioned into three thermal pathways:

$$P_{abs} = P_{melt} + P_{vap} + P_{rad} + P_{refl\_loss}$$

For most engineering metals during LBM cutting:

- $P_{melt} = \rho V H_f$ — energy to *melt* the material (latent heat of fusion $H_f$).
- $P_{vap} = \rho V H_v$ — energy to *vaporize* the material (latent heat of vaporization $H_v$).
- $P_{rad}$ — radiative heat loss (small at cutting timescales).
- $P_{refl\_loss}$ — energy lost to surface reflection (often 60–80% for polished metals at IR wavelengths).

Since $H_v \gg H_f$ (typically $H_v \approx 10 \cdot H_f$), the **dominant energy sink** in LBM is vaporization. This is why LBM is classified as a **vaporization-controlled** process — distinguishing it from conventional machining where chip formation (shear) dominates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Complete LBM Apparatus
*Block-level mapping of all interacting subsystems inside an industrial LBM workstation.*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply Unit] --> B[Laser Resonator Cavity]
    B1[Cooling System Chiller] --> B
    B --> C[Beam Shutter and Attenuator]
    C --> D[Beam Expander Optics]
    D --> E[Beam Bending Mirrors M1 and M2]
    E --> F[Focusing Lens Assembly ZnSe or Fused Silica]
    F --> G[Coaxial Gas Assist Nozzle O2 or N2]
    G --> H[Workpiece Mounted on CNC XY Table]
    H --> I[Material Removal Melt Vaporize]
    J[CNC Controller] --> A
    J --> H
    K[Fume Extraction Unit] --> H
    L[Safety Interlock Enclosure] --> H
    L --> J
```

### 4.2 Optical Resonator Schematic (Internal to the Laser Source)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[High Reflectance Mirror R equals 99.5 percent] --> B1[Active Lasing Medium CO2 or Nd YAG]
    B1 --> C1[Output Coupler Mirror R equals 80 percent]
    C1 --> D1[Useful Laser Beam Output]
    B1 --> A1
    E1[Optical Pump Source Flash Lamp or Diode] --> B1
```

### 4.3 Beam Delivery Modes — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1[Free Space Delivery Used in CO2 Systems]
        A2[Laser Source] --> B2[Beam Bending Mirror 1]
        B2 --> C2[Beam Bending Mirror 2]
        C2 --> D2[Focusing Head]
    end
    subgraph S2[Fiber Optic Delivery Used in Fiber and NdYAG Systems]
        E2[Laser Source] --> F2[Coupling Optics]
        F2 --> G2[Flexible Optical Fiber]
        G2 --> H2[Collimator and Focusing Head]
    end
    D2 --> I2[Workpiece]
    H2 --> I2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*List and briefly explain the **three essential properties** of a laser beam that make it suitable for material processing in LBM.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Monochromaticity** (1 mark): All photons possess the same wavelength $\lambda$, ensuring uniform interaction with the workpiece material.
2. **Coherence** (1 mark): Photons oscillate in phase both spatially and temporally, allowing the beam to be focused to a diffraction-limited spot.
3. **Collimation** (1 mark): Extremely low divergence, enabling delivery of high power density over long working distances.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is **water cooling** essential for high-power laser resonators in LBM apparatus?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The optical pumping process and stimulated emission generate significant **waste heat** inside the resonator (1 mark).
- Excessive heat causes **thermal lensing**, mirror distortion, and wavelength drift, degrading beam quality (1 mark).
- Water cooling maintains **thermal stability**, ensuring consistent power output and preventing damage to optical components and the active medium (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a) With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the complete functional apparatus of a Laser Beam Machining (LBM) system. Explain the role of each major component. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The LBM apparatus consists of **eight** interlinked functional units:

1. **Laser Source / Resonator (1 mark):** Contains the active medium (e.g., CO₂, Nd:YAG, fiber) between two parallel mirrors. Generates the coherent beam via stimulated emission of radiation.
2. **Power Supply & Cooling System (1 mark):** Supplies regulated DC/high-frequency excitation to the discharge/pump. Water chiller removes waste heat to prevent thermal lensing and maintain beam quality.
3. **Beam Transmission System (0.5 mark):** For CO₂ lasers — gold-coated copper mirrors route the beam. For fiber/Nd:YAG lasers — flexible optical fibers deliver the beam to remote cutting heads.
4. **Beam Expander & Shutter (0.5 mark):** Beam expander reduces divergence and increases raw beam diameter $D$ to shrink the focused spot size $d_{spot}$. Shutter provides fast on/off control.
5. **Focusing Lens (1 mark):** A plano-convex lens (ZnSe for 10.6 µm; fused silica for 1.06 µm) focuses the beam to spot diameters of 50–500 µm, achieving power densities of 10⁶–10⁷ W/mm².
6. **Gas Assist Nozzle (0.5 mark):** Coaxial nozzle injects O₂ (for exothermic oxidation of steel), N₂ (for stainless steel to prevent oxidation), or Ar (for reactive metals) — removes molten debris from the kerf.
7. **CNC Work Table (1 mark):** Multi-axis (X, Y, Z, rotational) positioning under closed-loop servo control to follow the programmed toolpath with µm-level accuracy.
8. **Control & Safety System (1 mark):** PLC/CNC synchronizes laser power, pulse frequency, traverse speed, gas pressure, and triggers emergency interlocks (door sensors, back-reflection monitors, fume extraction).

> **Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4 — Functional Architecture Flow diagram.)*

---

**(b) Compare CO₂, Nd:YAG, and Fiber lasers as energy sources for LBM, with reference to wavelength, beam delivery, efficiency, and typical industrial applications. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | CO₂ Laser | Nd:YAG Laser | Fiber Laser |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Active Medium** | CO₂/N₂/He gas | Nd³⁺ doped YAG crystal | Yb-doped silica fiber |
| **Wavelength** | 10.6 µm (far-IR) | 1.06 µm (near-IR) | 1.06 µm (near-IR) |
| **Beam Delivery** | Mirrors + ZnSe lenses | Optical fiber possible | **Optical fiber (highly flexible)** |
| **Efficiency** | ~10% | ~3–5% | **~30–40%** |
| **Power Range** | Up to 25 kW | 50 W – 6 kW | 100 W – 20 kW |
| **Maintenance** | Gas refill, mirror alignment | Lamp replacement (frequent) | **Solid-state, minimal** |
| **Best For** | Acrylic, wood, non-metals; thick steel plates | Drilling, spot welding, marking | **Sheet metal cutting (industry benchmark)** |

**Key Engineering Insights (1 mark):**
- CO₂ laser is best for non-metals because the 10.6 µm wavelength is strongly absorbed by organics and ceramics.
- Fiber laser dominates modern metal cutting because of its high wall-plug efficiency, fiber delivery flexibility, and low maintenance.
- Nd:YAG is increasingly being replaced by fiber lasers in industrial settings.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Derive the expression for the diameter of the focused spot in a laser beam machining setup. Discuss the effect of focal length, beam diameter, and wavelength on spot size. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

For a diffraction-limited Gaussian TEM₀₀ beam, the minimum focused spot radius $w_0$ produced by a thin lens of focal length $f$ for an incident beam of radius $w_i$ is:

$$w_0 = \frac{\lambda f}{\pi w_i}$$

Multiplying both sides by 2 to obtain **diameter**:

$$d_{spot} = 2 w_0 = \frac{2 \lambda f}{\pi w_i} = \frac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}$$

where $D = 2 w_i$ is the incident beam diameter.

**Parametric Effects (each 1.5 marks):**
- **Wavelength $\lambda$:** Spot diameter is *directly proportional* to wavelength. A 10× reduction in wavelength (10.6 µm → 1.06 µm, CO₂ → Fiber) shrinks the spot by 10×, enabling finer cuts.
- **Focal length $f$:** Spot is *directly proportional* to focal length. Shorter focal lenses (e.g., 50 mm vs. 200 mm) yield tighter spots but reduce depth of focus.
- **Incident beam diameter $D$:** Spot is *inversely proportional* to $D$. Beam expanders are used to enlarge $D$ and hence produce smaller focused spots for high-precision work.

**Engineering Trade-off:** Decreasing $d_{spot}$ improves kerf precision but reduces the *depth of focus*, making the process more sensitive to workpiece flatness deviations.

---

**(b) Explain the mechanism of material removal in LBM. Why is LBM classified as a vaporization-controlled process? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism (5 marks):**
1. The focused laser beam impinges on the workpiece surface with power density of 10⁶–10⁷ W/mm².
2. The surface absorbs a fraction of the energy, causing an instantaneous temperature rise above the material's **melting point**.
3. Further energy input raises the temperature above the **vaporization point** — material transitions directly from solid → vapor (with a transient liquid phase).
4. The assist gas (O₂/N₂/Ar) ejects the molten and vaporized debris from the kerf as a *dross plume*.
5. A clean cut profile is obtained once the beam traverses the full thickness.

**Why Vaporization-Controlled (2 marks):**
- The latent heat of vaporization $H_v$ is approximately **10× the latent heat of fusion** $H_f$ for most metals.
- Hence, the dominant energy sink in the energy balance is $P_{vap} = \rho V H_v$.
- Unlike conventional machining where plastic shear dominates, LBM is governed almost entirely by **phase change to vapor**, justifying its classification as a *vaporization-controlled thermal process*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the role of gas:** Many students wrongly assume the assist gas *causes* cutting. In reality, the laser beam does the cutting; the gas merely *ejects molten debris* and may add exothermic heat (O₂ on mild steel).
> 2. **Incorrect unit for power density:** Always express in **W/mm²**, not W/m². A mistake here is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Spot-size formula sign:** Examiners expect the **diameter** version $d_{spot} = \dfrac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}$. Writing only the *radius* form and missing the factor of 2 is a frequent slip.
> 4. **Skipping beam delivery mode:** Always mention whether the laser uses *mirror-based* (CO₂) or *fiber-optic* (Nd:YAG/Fiber) delivery. Omitting this loses **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting cooling:** A common omission. Water cooling is *non-negotiable* for high-power systems — failing to mention it costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ LBM = **non-contact**, **thermal**, **vaporization-controlled** advanced machining process.
- ✅ The full apparatus = **Laser source + Power supply + Cooling + Beam delivery + Focusing + Gas nozzle + CNC table + Control + Safety**.
- ✅ Three defining laser properties: **Monochromaticity, Coherence, Collimation**.
- ✅ Stimulated emission requires **population inversion** inside an **optical resonator** (two mirrors).
- ✅ **Spot diameter formula:** $d_{spot} = \dfrac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- ✅ **Power density formula:** $P_{density} = \dfrac{P}{\pi r^2}$ — typical LBM range **10⁶–10⁷ W/mm²**.
- ✅ **Wavelength choice:** CO₂ (10.6 µm) for non-metals; Fiber/Nd:YAG (1.06 µm) for metals.
- ✅ **Fiber laser** = highest wall-plug efficiency (~30–40%), flexible fiber delivery, low maintenance → modern industry standard.
- ✅ **Assist gas functions:** eject debris + (for O₂ on mild steel) provide exothermic energy boost.
- ✅ **Cooling (water chiller)** is mandatory to prevent thermal lensing and resonator damage.
- ✅ Energy balance is **dominated by latent heat of vaporization** → LBM is vaporization-controlled.
- ✅ **Safety enclosure + fume extraction** are integral to any industrial LBM cell.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Removal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET521 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material Removal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Material Removal Processes (MRPs)**, in the context of Advanced Manufacturing, refer to a class of non-traditional manufacturing processes that utilize various forms of energy—*mechanical, electrical, chemical, thermal, or a combination thereof*—to remove excess material from a workpiece, thereby achieving the desired geometry, tolerance, and surface finish. Unlike conventional machining (turning, milling, drilling) which relies on a physically harder cutting tool, advanced MRPs decouple tool hardness from workpiece hardness, enabling the machining of extremely hard, brittle, and exotic materials (e.g., hardened tool steels, ceramics, glass, tungsten carbide, superalloys, and composites).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Classification)**
> Material removal processes covered in Module 3 include: **Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM), Water Jet Machining (WJM), Ultrasonic Machining (USM), Electric Discharge Machining (EDM), Electrochemical Machining (ECM), Laser Beam Machining (LBM), and Electron Beam Machining (EBM).** These are categorized as **Non-Traditional / Unconventional Manufacturing Processes (NTM/UTM)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you need to carve a delicate pattern into a slab of glass (which is extremely brittle and would shatter if you used a hammer or chisel).

- **Conventional Machining Analogy**: Like using a metal chisel on glass — high force, high hardness requirement, high risk of fracture.
- **Advanced Material Removal Analogy**: Like using a *focused stream of sand particles carried by air* (AJM), or a *focused beam of light* (LBM), or a *controlled spark* (EDM), or even a *chemical reaction* (ECM) to gently eat away the material. The tool and the workpiece do not physically clash — the energy does the work.

In essence, Advanced Material Removal Processes are like a **surgical laser** versus a **cleaver** — both remove material, but one is precise, contactless, and works on materials the other cannot even touch.

### 1.3 Why Advanced Material Removal?

| Conventional Machining Limitation | Advanced MRP Solution |
|---|---|
| Tool must be harder than workpiece | Tool hardness independent (uses energy) |
| High mechanical force → workpiece distortion | Low/no mechanical force (no distortion) |
| Poor surface finish on hard/brittle parts | Excellent surface finish (Ra < 1 µm possible) |
| Cannot machine complex cavities, curved holes | Can machine intricate 3D cavities, micro-holes |
| Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) on brittle materials | Controlled HAZ or no HAZ (chemical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Parameter — Material Removal Rate (MRR)** is universally measured in **mm³/min** or **mm³/s** across all MRPs. This is the most asked KTU 2-mark/3-mark concept.

### 1.4 Classification of Material Removal Processes (KTU Taxonomy)

```
MATERIAL REMOVAL PROCESSES
├── Mechanical Energy Based
│   ├── Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)
│   ├── Water Jet Machining (WJM / AWJM)
│   └── Ultrasonic Machining (USM)
├── Electrical Energy Based
│   ├── Electric Discharge Machining (EDM)
│   ├── Wire Electric Discharge Machining (WEDM)
│   └── Electron Beam Machining (EBM)
├── Chemical Energy Based
│   ├── Chemical Machining (CHM)
│   └── Electrochemical Machining (ECM)
├── Thermal Energy Based
│   ├── Laser Beam Machining (LBM)
│   └── Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Always classify the process by the *primary energy form* used for material detachment. This is a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)

#### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
A high-velocity stream of abrasive particles (typically **aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃)**, **silicon carbide (SiC)**, or **glass beads**) is carried by a pressurized gas (air, N₂, CO₂) through a nozzle and directed at the workpiece. The kinetic energy of the impinging particles causes micro-chipping and erosion of the workpiece material.

#### 2.1.2 Process Parameters
- **Abrasive**: Al₂O₃ (60–200 grit), SiC, glass beads
- **Carrier Gas Pressure**: 2–7 bar (typical: 4 bar)
- **Nozzle**: Tungsten carbide or sapphire, orifice diameter 0.25–1.5 mm
- **Stand-off Distance (SOD)**: 0.5–25 mm (optimal: 1–5 mm)
- **MRR**: 0.1–50 mm³/min (very low)

#### 2.1.3 Theory of Material Removal — The Bystrova Equation

The **Bystrova Model** is the foundation stone of AJM theory and is universally tested in KTU examinations.

$$Z = \frac{K \cdot m \cdot v^{n} \cdot d^{m_1}}{H_v^{m_2}}$$

Where:
- $Z$ = depth of penetration (mm)
- $K$ = constant (process-specific)
- $m$ = mass flow rate of abrasive (g/s)
- $v$ = velocity of abrasive particles (m/s)
- $n$ = velocity exponent (typically 2.0–2.4)
- $d$ = abrasive grain size (µm)
- $H_v$ = Vickers hardness of workpiece
- $m_1, m_2$ = empirical constants

> [!NOTE]
> The MRR in AJM is **proportional to (velocity)²** — this quadratic dependence is the single most tested relationship. Double the velocity, quadruple the MRR (until nozzle wear dominates).

### 2.2 Ultrasonic Machining (USM)

#### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
A tool (horn/sonotrode) vibrates at **ultrasonic frequency (19–25 kHz, typically 20 kHz)** with an amplitude of **15–50 µm**. Abrasive slurry (boron carbide or SiC in water) is continuously fed into the machining zone. The tool does not directly contact the workpiece — it hammers the abrasive particles, which in turn impact the workpiece.

#### 2.2.2 Process Components
1. **Ultrasonic Generator** → converts electrical energy to ~20 kHz electrical signal
2. **Transducer** → converts electrical signal to mechanical vibration (piezoelectric or magnetostrictive)
3. **Tool Holder / Horn (Sonotrode)** → amplifies amplitude (titanium, monel, or aluminium)
4. **Slurry System** → recirculating abrasive + water
5. **Workpiece + Fixture**

#### 2.2.3 MRR Formula for USM

$$\text{MRR} = K \cdot \rho_a \cdot d_a \cdot f \cdot a \cdot N \cdot C$$

Where:
- $\rho_a$ = density of abrasive
- $d_a$ = average abrasive grain diameter
- $f$ = frequency of vibration
- $a$ = amplitude of vibration
- $N$ = number of active abrasive grains
- $C$ = constant depending on material
- $K$ = proportionality constant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> USM is **exclusively suited for brittle, non-metallic, and hard materials** (glass, ceramics, quartz, germanium, silicon, tungsten carbide). It is *not* used for soft, ductile metals (aluminium, copper) because the abrasive gets embedded into the workpiece.

### 2.3 Electric Discharge Machining (EDM)

#### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
Material is removed by a **series of discrete electrical discharges (sparks)** between the tool (cathode) and workpiece (anode) immersed in a dielectric fluid (EDM oil or deionized water). Each spark generates temperatures of **8,000–12,000 °C**, vaporizing and melting a tiny crater of material.

#### 2.3.2 Critical Process Parameters
- **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)**: 50–380 V DC
- **Spark Gap**: typically **0.025–0.05 mm** (very small)
- **Pulse Duration ($T_{on}$)**: 1–2000 µs
- **Duty Cycle ($\tau$)**: $\tau = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$
- **Current (Peak Current, $I_p$)**: 1–500 A
- **Dielectric**: EDM oil (kerosene), deionized water (for fine finishing)

#### 2.3.3 MRR Formula in EDM

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot \eta}{H_m \cdot \rho_w}$$

Where:
- $V$ = voltage (V)
- $I$ = average current (A)
- $\eta$ = sparking efficiency (typically 0.6–0.9)
- $H_m$ = melting point of workpiece (°C or K)
- $\rho_w$ = density of workpiece (g/cm³)

**More commonly used engineering form:**

$$\text{MRR} = K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$$

Where $K$ is an empirical constant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In EDM, the **tool is the cathode (negative)** and the **workpiece is the anode (positive)** in the conventional setup — current flows from anode to cathode via the spark. Many students reverse this in their diagrams — losing 1–2 marks.

### 2.4 Electrochemical Machining (ECM)

#### 2.4.1 Operating Principle
Based on **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. The workpiece is made the **anode (+)**, the tool is the **cathode (−)**, and an electrolyte (NaCl, NaNO₃, NaOH) flows through the small gap. Metal atoms at the anode are ionized and dissolved into the electrolyte, leaving the workpiece.

#### 2.4.2 Critical Concept — No Tool Wear
ECM produces **ZERO mechanical and thermal damage** to the workpiece. The tool (cathode) does not wear out because no material is removed from it — only the anode (workpiece) is consumed.

#### 2.4.3 MRR Formula (Faraday's Law Applied)

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta \cdot E_w}}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho_w}$$

Or in volume form:

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{I \cdot \eta \cdot E_w}}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho_w}$$

Where:
- $I$ = current (A)
- $\eta$ = current efficiency (typically 90–100%)
- $E_w$ = atomic weight of workpiece material (g/mol)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,487 C/mol ≈ 96,500 C/mol**
- $n$ = valency (number of electrons exchanged)
- $\rho_w$ = density of workpiece (g/cm³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Faraday's Constant F = 96,487 Coulombs/mol** is a *must-memorize* value for KTU numericals. Marks are awarded for writing the correct constant with units.

### 2.5 Laser Beam Machining (LBM)

#### 2.5.1 Operating Principle
A **high-power, monochromatic, coherent, collimated** laser beam (CO₂, Nd:YAG, or Fiber laser) is focused onto the workpiece using lenses. The intense heat melts and vaporizes the material at the focal point.

#### 2.5.2 Properties of Laser Beams
1. **Monochromaticity** → single wavelength
2. **Coherence** → waves in phase (temporal + spatial)
3. **Directionality (collimation)** → very low divergence
4. **High intensity** → 10⁶–10⁹ W/mm² at focal point

#### 2.5.3 Process Parameters
- **Power (P)**: 10 W – 10 kW
- **Pulse Duration**: µs to ns (for pulsed) or CW (continuous wave)
- **Focal Spot Diameter (D)**: 0.05–0.5 mm
- **Depth of Cut (h)**: function of power and time
- **Assist Gas**: O₂ (for cutting steel), N₂ (for stainless), He (inert)

#### 2.5.4 MRR Formula

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{E_p \cdot f \cdot \eta_m}{H_v + L_v}}$$

Where:
- $E_p$ = pulse energy (J)
- $f$ = pulse frequency (Hz)
- $\eta_m$ = material removal efficiency
- $H_v$ = heat of vaporization
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (latent heat of melting sometimes included)

### 2.6 Electron Beam Machining (EBM)

#### 2.6.1 Operating Principle
A focused beam of high-velocity electrons (accelerated by 50–200 kV) strikes the workpiece. The kinetic energy converts to thermal energy on impact, melting and vaporizing the material. Must be performed in a **vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr)** to prevent electron scattering.

#### 2.6.2 Comparison: LBM vs. EBM

| Parameter | LBM | EBM |
|---|---|---|
| Energy medium | Photons | Electrons |
| Atmosphere | Air or gas | Vacuum required |
| Beam diameter | ~0.1 mm | ~0.01–0.1 mm |
| Power density | 10⁶–10⁹ W/mm² | 10⁹ W/mm² and above |
| HAZ | Larger | Smaller (less heat input) |
| Workpiece size | Limited by chamber | Limited by vacuum chamber |
| Cost | Moderate | Very high |

### 2.7 Water Jet Machining (WJM / AWJM)

#### 2.7.1 Operating Principle
- **WJM (Pure Water Jet)**: High-pressure water (200–400 MPa) without abrasives — used for soft materials (paper, food, plastics, textiles).
- **AWJM (Abrasive Water Jet)**: Water + abrasive (garnet) — used for hard materials (metals, stone, composites, ceramics).

#### 2.7.2 Process Components
- **Intensifier Pump** → generates ultra-high pressure
- **On/Off Valve** → controls jet
- **Nozzle** → sapphire or tungsten carbide, 0.1–0.5 mm
- **Focusing Tube / Mixing Tube** (in AWJM)
- **Abrasive Feeder** (garnet, Al₂O₃, SiC)
- **Catcher Tank** → absorbs jet energy, prevents overspray

### 2.8 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| Process | MRR Formula | Key Constant | Critical Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AJM** | $Z \propto m \cdot v^{2.0\text{ to }2.4} \cdot d$ | Velocity exponent = 2 | Gas pressure, SOD |
| **USM** | $\text{MRR} = K \cdot \rho_a \cdot d_a \cdot f \cdot a$ | $f = 20$ kHz | Amplitude 15–50 µm |
| **EDM** | $\text{MMR} = K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$ | Spark gap = 0.025–0.05 mm | Pulse on time |
| **ECM** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{I \cdot \eta \cdot E_w}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho_w}$ | $F = 96{,}487$ C/mol | Faraday's Law |
| **LBM** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{E_p \cdot f \cdot \eta_m}{H_v + L_v}$ | Power density 10⁶–10⁹ W/mm² | Pulse energy |
| **EBM** | $\text{MRR} \propto \dfrac{I_b \cdot V}{C_p \cdot \rho}$ | Vacuum 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr | Accelerating voltage 50–200 kV |
| **WJM/AWJM** | $\text{MRR} \propto P \cdot d_{orifice} \cdot \dot{m}_{abrasive}$ | Pressure 200–400 MPa | Orifice diameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend**: $Z$ = depth of cut, $m$ = mass flow rate, $v$ = velocity, $d$ = grain size, $H_v$ = Vickers hardness, $\rho_a$ = abrasive density, $d_a$ = abrasive diameter, $f$ = frequency, $a$ = amplitude, $I_p$ = peak current, $T_{on}$ = pulse on-time, $I$ = current, $\eta$ = current efficiency, $E_w$ = equivalent weight, $F$ = Faraday constant, $n$ = valency, $\rho_w$ = workpiece density, $E_p$ = pulse energy, $H_v$ = heat of vaporization, $L_v$ = latent heat, $I_b$ = beam current, $V$ = voltage, $C_p$ = specific heat, $P$ = pressure, $d_{orifice}$ = nozzle diameter, $\dot{m}_{abrasive}$ = abrasive mass flow.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: MRR in ECM Using Faraday's Law

**Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis:** *The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.*

Mathematically:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot E_w \cdot \eta}{F \cdot n}$$

Given that $Q = I \cdot t$ (charge = current × time), we have:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot E_w \cdot \eta}{F \cdot n}$$

**Step 1: Convert mass to volume**

Volume removed is mass divided by density:

$$V_{\text{removed}} = \frac{m}{\rho_w} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot E_w \cdot \eta}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to time to obtain MRR**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I \cdot E_w \cdot \eta}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Step 3: Practical ECM MRR equation (volumetric form)**

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = \frac{I \cdot \eta \cdot E_w}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho_w}} \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation in Module 3** for KTU. You may be asked to derive the MRR equation in a 7-mark sub-question. Practice writing it from Faraday's Law on a blank sheet.

### 3.2 Solved Numerical: ECM Material Removal Rate

**Problem:** Calculate the MRR in ECM for machining a low-carbon steel workpiece using a current of 1000 A and current efficiency of 90%. Given: $E_w = 55.85$ g/mol (Fe), valency $n = 2$, $\rho_w = 7.87$ g/cm³.

**Step 1: List given values**
- $I = 1000$ A
- $\eta = 0.90$
- $E_w = 55.85$ g/mol
- $F = 96{,}487$ C/mol
- $n = 2$
- $\rho_w = 7.87$ g/cm³

**Step 2: Substitute into the ECM MRR equation**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{I \cdot \eta \cdot E_w}{F \cdot n \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Step 3: Numerator evaluation**

$$\text{Numerator} = 1000 \times 0.90 \times 55.85 = 50{,}265 \text{ g·A/mol}$$

**Step 4: Denominator evaluation**

$$\text{Denominator} = 96{,}487 \times 2 \times 7.87 = 1{,}518{,}946.18 \text{ C·g/(mol·cm}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 5: Final MRR calculation**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{50{,}265}{1{,}518{,}946.18} = 0.0331 \text{ cm}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 6: Unit conversion to mm³/min**

$$\text{MRR} = 0.0331 \times 1000 \times 60 = 1985 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 1985 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 1.985 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 2 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}}$$

### 3.3 Solved Numerical: AJM Depth of Penetration

**Problem:** In AJM, the depth of penetration follows $Z = K \cdot m \cdot v^{2.2} \cdot d / H_v$. If gas pressure is doubled (assuming particle velocity scales as $\sqrt{P}$), by what factor does the depth of penetration change?

**Step 1: Write the proportionality**

$$Z \propto m \cdot v^{2.2} \cdot d / H_v$$

**Step 2: Mass flow rate scales with gas pressure**

$$m \propto P$$

**Step 3: Velocity scales as square root of pressure**

$$v \propto \sqrt{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v^{2.2} \propto P^{1.1}$$

**Step 4: Combined scaling**

$$Z \propto P \cdot P^{1.1} = P^{2.1}$$

**Step 5: When pressure doubles (P → 2P)**

$$\frac{Z_{\text{new}}}{Z_{\text{old}}} = 2^{2.1} = 4.287$$

$$\boxed{Z_{\text{new}} \approx 4.29 \times Z_{\text{old}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** This non-linear response explains why AJM operators carefully control gas pressure — a small pressure increase can cause a dramatic rise in material removal. The same logic applies to EDM pulse on-time and USM amplitude.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Material Removal Process Selection

| Workpiece Material | Recommended MRP | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Hardened tool steel (HRC > 60) | EDM, EBM, LBM | Hardness independent of tool |
| Glass, quartz, ceramics | USM, AJM, LBM | Brittle — need low mechanical force |
| Tungsten carbide | EDM, USM, LBM | Extreme hardness |
| Aluminium, soft copper | **NOT** USM (abrasive embedding) | Use WJM, conventional milling |
| Deep narrow slots, sharp corners | WEDM, AWJM | Mechanical non-contact |
| Micro-holes (< 0.5 mm dia.) | EDM, LBM, EBM | High precision |
| Complex 3D cavities | ECM, EDM copy milling | High MRR + no tool wear |
| Non-conductive ceramics | LBM, EBM, USM | EDM/ECM require conductivity |
| Heat-sensitive parts | ECM, WJM | No thermal HAZ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Selection Question Trap:** If the workpiece is **non-conductive** (ceramic, glass, plastic), **EDM and ECM are NOT possible** because EDM needs electrical conductivity and ECM needs the workpiece to be an electrode. The correct answers for non-conductive materials are: **USM, AJM, LBM, EBM, or WJM**.

### 3.5 Derivation: Spark Energy in EDM

**Step 1: Energy per spark** is the energy dissipated in a single discharge:

$$E_{\text{spark}} = V \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$$

**Step 2: Number of sparks per second** is determined by the pulse frequency $f$:

$$N_{\text{sparks}} = f \quad \text{(sparks/second)}$$

**Step 3: Power input to the workpiece**

$$P_{\text{input}} = E_{\text{spark}} \cdot N_{\text{sparks}} = V \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on} \cdot f$$

**Step 4: Duty cycle $\tau$ relation**

$$\tau = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{on} = \tau \cdot T_p$$

Where $T_p$ is total pulse period and $f = 1/T_p$.

**Step 5: Volumetric MRR via energy balance**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{P_{\text{input}} \cdot \eta_{\text{thermal}}}{H_m \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{V \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on} \cdot f \cdot \eta}{H_m \cdot \rho_w}$$

This is the **thermal-energy-based EDM MRR equation** and is the formal derivation for the simpler $K \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on}$ form.

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{\text{EDM}} = \frac{V \cdot I_p \cdot T_{on} \cdot f \cdot \eta}{H_m \cdot \rho_w}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
