# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three applications of Additive Manufacturing in the medical field.**

**Model Answer:**

Additive Manufacturing has the following key applications in the medical field:

1. **Patient-Specific Implants (PSI):** Custom cranial plates, hip cups, and spinal cages manufactured using **SLM / DMLS** from Ti-6Al-4V powder, matched exactly to the patient's CT/MRI data.
2. **Dental Restorations:** Crowns, bridges, and aligners are produced using **SLA** (for patterns) or direct **DMLS** (for Co-Cr frameworks), with high accuracy of **±0.05 mm**.
3. **Surgical Guides and Anatomical Models:** **PolyJet** and **SLA** processes are used to create drill guides and patient-specific organ models for pre-surgical planning, improving surgical accuracy and reducing operation time.

> *(3 marks — 1 mark for each valid application with a correct process mapping)*

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Differentiate between Rapid Prototyping and Rapid Tooling as applications of AM.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Rapid Prototyping (RP) | Rapid Tooling (RT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To create a physical model for **visualization, design validation, and functional testing** | To create an **actual tool** (mold, die, fixture) for production |
| **End Product** | A prototype part (non-functional or test-functional) | A working tool used to produce end-use parts |
| **Material** | Polymers, resins, soft metals | Tool steel, hardenable alloys, sand, ceramic composites |
| **Process Examples** | SLA, FDM, SLS, PolyJet | DMLS (conformal cooling inserts), Binder Jetting (sand molds), 3D Printed Patterns |
| **Industrial Use** | Concept validation, ergonomic testing | Short-run production, low-volume manufacturing |

> *(3 marks — table format preferred; 1.5 marks per differentiation category)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Explain in detail the various applications of Additive Manufacturing in the aerospace industry. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The aerospace industry is one of the **largest adopters** of Additive Manufacturing due to the critical need for **lightweight, geometrically complex, and high-strength** components. The following are the key applications:

**1. Lightweight Lattice Structures for Brackets and Panels**
- Traditional aerospace brackets are machined from solid billets, leading to **up to 80% material waste**.
- AM processes like **SLM** and **EBM** can produce **lattice / topology-optimized** brackets with internal truss structures that reduce weight by **30–70%** while maintaining structural strength.
- Example: Airbus A350 XWB cabin brackets printed in **Ti-6Al-4V**.

**2. Turbine Engine Components (Combustion Liners, Fuel Injectors, Nozzles)**
- Modern turbofan engines require **convergent-divergent nozzles** with complex internal cooling channels that cannot be cast or machined conventionally.
- **DMLS** of **Inconel 718** and **Ti-6Al-4V** enables these intricate internal geometries.
- **General Electric (GE)** manufactures **LEAP fuel nozzle tips** using DMLS — a single part replaces an 18-part assembly, reducing weight by **25%** and increasing fuel efficiency.

**3. Conformal Cooling Channels in Engine and Tooling Components**
- Conventional drilled cooling channels are **straight** and cannot follow the contour of hot spots.
- AM enables **conformal cooling** channels that follow the surface contour, reducing thermal stress and extending part life by up to **300%**.

**4. Satellite and Spacecraft Components**
- **DED** and **SLM** are used to manufacture satellite brackets, antenna waveguides, and propulsion components.
- SpaceX uses DMLS for **SuperDraco engine chambers** (Inconel) on Crew Dragon spacecraft.

**5. Repair and Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO)**
- **DED / Laser Cladding** is used to **rebuild worn turbine blades** and compressor discs, adding material only where needed and avoiding complete part replacement.
- Saves up to **70% cost** compared to replacement.

> *(7 marks: 1 mark introduction, 5 marks for the 5 applications with 1 mark each, 1 mark for industrial examples)*

#### **(b) Calculate the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) for an SLM process with the following parameters and state whether the resulting part is likely to be dense. (7 Marks)**
- Laser Power $P = 250$ W
- Scan Speed $v = 1000$ mm/s
- Hatch Spacing $h = 0.12$ mm
- Layer Thickness $t = 0.03$ mm
- Material: **316L Stainless Steel**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the VED formula:

$$VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values:

$$VED = \frac{250}{1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.03}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator:

$$1000 \times 0.12 = 120$$

$$120 \times 0.03 = 3.6 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute VED:

$$VED = \frac{250}{3.6} = 69.44 \ \text{J/mm}^3$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the recommended range for 316L Stainless Steel:

> Recommended VED range for 316L SS = **80 – 150 J/mm³**

**Step 6:** Conclusion:

> The calculated VED of **69.44 J/mm³ is BELOW the recommended range**, indicating **insufficient energy input**. This will likely result in **porosity (lack of fusion defects)** and a **less dense (<99%)** part. **Recommendation:** Reduce scan speed to ~830 mm/s to achieve a VED of ~83 J/mm³, or increase laser power to ~290 W to maintain density.

> *(7 marks: 1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution and calculation, 2 marks comparison with standard range, 2 marks interpretation and recommendation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### **(a) Discuss the applications of Rapid Prototyping in product development and tooling. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Rapid Prototyping (RP) is a foundational application of AM that significantly **compresses the product development cycle** from months to days.

**1. Concept Visualization and Design Communication**
- Physical models help **designers, marketers, and customers** visualize the product before tooling investment.
- Example: SLA prototypes of consumer electronics housings (e.g., smartphone casings).

**2. Form, Fit, and Function Testing**
- SLS and FDM parts are used to test **fit** with mating components and **ergonomics** of handheld devices.
- Functional prototypes in engineering plastics (PA12, ABS) can undergo load tests for **mechanical behavior**.

**3. Pre-Production Prototyping for Soft-Tooling**
- **Rapid Tooling (RT)** involves 3D printing of:
  - **Sand casting patterns** using Binder Jetting — eliminates the need for traditional wooden patterns.
  - **Investment casting wax patterns** using SLA — high-resolution, intricate jewelry and turbine blade patterns.
  - **Injection mold inserts** with **conformal cooling** using DMLS — reduces cycle time by 20–40%.

**4. Marketing Samples and User Trials**
- PolyJet allows **multi-color, transparent, and soft-touch** prototypes used in **clinical trials, user focus groups**, and trade-show demos.

**5. Tooling Aids: Jigs, Fixtures, and Assembly Aids**
- FDM is widely used to produce **custom jigs and fixtures** for shop-floor assembly, replacing machined metal tools.
- **Cost reduction of 60–80%** and lead time reduction of **70%** are commonly reported.

> *(7 marks: 1 mark intro, 1.5 marks × 4 applications, 0.5 marks for concluding statement)*

#### **(b) Explain the major applications of Additive Manufacturing in the automotive industry. List at least two examples of companies using AM in automotive production. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The automotive industry uses AM in **three key stages**: prototyping, low-volume production, and aftermarket parts.

**1. Functional Prototyping of Engine and Interior Components**
- **SLA** and **SLS** are used to produce **intake manifolds, dashboard assemblies, and gear knobs** for design validation.
- BMW uses **SLA and SLS** to prototype **>10,000 parts per year** during vehicle development.

**2. End-Use Parts for Performance and Electric Vehicles (EVs)**
- **SLM** in aluminum alloys is used for **lightweight EV battery housings** and motor housings.
- **Bugatti** used **SLM to print the world's largest functional titanium brake caliper** (Ti-6Al-4V), reducing weight by **41%** compared to aluminum.

**3. Customized and On-Demand Spare Parts**
- **Volkswagen** uses AM in its **"Volkswagen Individual"** program to 3D print rare, vintage replacement parts on demand — eliminating the need to maintain decades-old injection molds.
- **Mini (BMW Group)** offers **3D printed customizable interior trim** for personalization.

**4. Lightweight Structural Components**
- **Chassis sub-frames and suspension arms** are being explored using topology-optimized AM designs, achieving **20–30% weight savings**.
- **Czinger 21C** hypercar uses AM-printed structural components.

**5. Conformal Cooling in Injection Molds**
- **DMLS inserts** with conformal cooling channels are used in plastic injection molds, reducing cycle time by **30%** and improving part quality.
- Used by **Ford, Honda, and Toyota** for dashboard and bumper production molds.

**Industry Examples:**
- **Bugatti (France):** 3D printed titanium brake caliper.
- **BMW (Germany):** SLA-based prototyping and SLS series production parts.
- **Local Motors (USA):** Entire car body (Strata) 3D printed using **Large-Scale AM**.
- **Volkswagen (Germany):** On-demand spare parts production.

> *(7 marks: 1 mark intro, 1.5 marks × 4 applications, 0.5 marks for company examples)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SLA with SLS.** SLA uses **liquid resin cured by UV laser**; SLS uses **powder sintered by CO₂ laser**. Mixing these up is a **frequently observed mistake** in KTU answer scripts and costs 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Do NOT state that AM parts are always weaker than wrought parts.** For **Ti-6Al-4V built via SLM with optimal VED**, the tensile strength can match or **exceed** the wrought material due to fine martensitic α' microstructure. This is a known KTU valuation trap.
> 3. **Always include units** (W, mm/s, J/mm³) in numerical VED calculations. Missing units = **-1 mark**.
> 4. **Staircase error must be expressed in the correct units** — typically μm for AM surfaces. Writing it in mm without context is penalized.
> 5. **Always mention a real industrial example** (GE, Bugatti, BMW, etc.) in 7-mark application questions. KTU examiners award **1 bonus mark** for relevant industry examples.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Applications:**

- ✅ **SLA** → Best for **dental, jewelry, high-detail visual prototypes** (UV laser + liquid resin).
- ✅ **SLS** → Best for **functional polymer parts** with complex geometry (CO₂ laser + nylon powder).
- ✅ **SLM / DMLS** → Best for **metal end-use parts** (Ti, SS, Inconel, Al) for **aerospace, medical, defence**.
- ✅ **EBM** → Best for **Ti implants and aerospace brackets** (electron beam in vacuum).
- ✅ **DED** → Best for **large part repair, cladding, and hybrid manufacturing** (aero MRO).
- ✅ **FDM** → Best for **low-cost concept models, jigs, fixtures, and education** (thermoplastic filament).
- ✅ **PolyJet** → Best for **multi-material and soft-touch prototypes** (consumer products).
- ✅ **Binder Jetting** → Best for **sand casting molds and large metal parts** (foundry and architecture).

- ✅ **VED formula** is $\dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ with units $\text{J/mm}^3$.
- ✅ **Optimal VED for Ti-6Al-4V** = **60–120 J/mm³**.
- ✅ **Optimal VED for 316L SS** = **80–150 J/mm³**.
- ✅ **Staircase error** = $r - \sqrt{r^2 - (a/2)^2}$.
- ✅ **Layer thickness range in AM** = **0.02 to 0.4 mm**.
- ✅ **AM tolerance range** = **±0.05 to ±0.2 mm**.
- ✅ **GE LEAP fuel nozzle** — 18 parts → 1 part, **25% weight reduction**.
- ✅ **Bugatti 3D-printed Ti brake caliper** — **41% weight reduction** vs. aluminum.
- ✅ **Airbus A350 XWB brackets** — topology-optimized, **30–70% weight reduction**.
- ✅ **Hybrid manufacturing = AM + CNC** in a single setup for **near-net shape + finishing**.
- ✅ **Rapid Prototyping (RP) ≠ Rapid Tooling (RT)** — RP = visualization, RT = functional tooling.

> **Last-Minute Tip:** When answering any KTU application question, **always follow the 3-point rule**: (1) **Name the process**, (2) **Describe the application**, (3) **Give a real industrial example**. This is the proven KTU topper's strategy for scoring full marks in Module 4 application questions.
